topics.title: Diffusion MRI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT723 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Diffusion MRI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diffusion Magnetic Resonance Imaging (Diffusion MRI / dMRI)** is a specialised, non-invasive MRI technique that maps the **stochastic (random) Brownian motion of water molecules** within biological tissue by applying pairs of strong, symmetric magnetic field gradients (diffusion-sensitising gradients) and measuring the resulting attenuation of the MR signal.

The technique yields two fundamental classes of contrast:

- **Diffusion-Weighted Imaging (DWI)** — provides the **Apparent Diffusion Coefficient (ADC)** map, a scalar parameter reflecting the magnitude of water self-diffusion (in $\text{mm}^2/\text{s}$).
- **Diffusion Tensor Imaging (DTI)** — provides an effective second-order diffusion tensor $\mathbf{D}$ (a $3 \times 3$ symmetric positive-definite matrix), from which scalar invariants such as **Mean Diffusivity (MD)** and **Fractional Anisotropy (FA)** are derived to characterise tissue microstructural anisotropy (e.g., white-matter tract orientation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard clinical metric — b-value:** the diffusion-weighting factor (in $\text{s/mm}^2$). Typical clinical DWI: $b = 0$ and $b = 1000 \,\text{s/mm}^2$. Typical DTI: $b \approx 1000$–$\,1500 \,\text{s/mm}^2$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

Imagine throwing a handful of **ping-pong balls into a swimming pool** (free water — isotropic) versus a **dense mangrove swamp** (white matter — anisotropic, full of cylindrical axons).

- In the **swimming pool**, balls scatter equally in every direction — diffusion is **isotropic** (same in all directions). A very high **b** (strong gradients) almost erases the MR signal because the water has moved far and randomly, dephasing spins completely.
- In the **mangrove swamp**, balls bounce mostly *along* the long channels of the roots and only trickle sideways. Diffusion is **anisotropic** (direction-dependent). If we apply the gradient *parallel* to the roots, balls escape fast (signal dies). If we apply it *perpendicular*, balls barely move (signal survives).
- **Diffusion MRI is exactly this experiment**, but with hydrogen protons instead of ping-pong balls, and magnetic field gradients instead of "tilted water" to "see" the motion indirectly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key intuition:** The signal loss (attenuation) is proportional to *how far* the water molecules have moved during the diffusion-encoding time. Healthy tissue, stroke, and tumour tissue have *different* water mobility → *different* signal loss → a *diagnostic contrast*.

### 1.3 Clinical Significance (Syllabus Highlight)

| Pathology | Typical ADC change | Diagnostic use |
|---|---|---|
| Acute ischaemic stroke | $\downarrow$ ADC (cytotoxic oedema) | Detection within minutes |
| Tumour (high cellularity) | $\downarrow$ ADC | Cellularity grading |
| Cyst / CSF | $\uparrow$ ADC (free water) | Differentiation |
| Demyelination (MS) | $\uparrow$ MD, $\downarrow$ FA | White-matter damage |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Foundation — The Pulsed Gradient Spin Echo (PGSE) Sequence

The seminal Stejskal–Tanner pulsed-gradient spin-echo experiment is the building block of every diffusion MRI pulse sequence.

**Step-by-step operational logic (the "How" and "Why"):**

1. **90° RF excitation pulse** — flips the bulk magnetisation $\mathbf{M}_0$ into the transverse (xy) plane, creating a coherent phase across all spins.
2. **First diffusion gradient lobe** ($\mathbf{G}_d$, duration $\delta$) — imparts a position-dependent phase $\phi_1 = \gamma \mathbf{G}_d \cdot \mathbf{r}_1 \, \delta$ to each spin at its initial position $\mathbf{r}_1$. Why? So each spin is "tagged" with a phase that depends on *where* it was.
3. **180° refocusing pulse** at $TE/2$ — flips the phase of all spins, converting the accumulated phase lag into a phase lead (sign reversal). This is the same trick used in conventional spin-echo imaging.
4. **Second diffusion gradient lobe** ($\mathbf{G}_d$, duration $\delta$, separated by $\Delta$) — re-imparts a position-dependent phase $\phi_2 = -\gamma \mathbf{G}_d \cdot \mathbf{r}_2 \, \delta$ at the new position $\mathbf{r}_2$. Why? So each spin is "untagged" — but only if it has *not moved*.
5. **Signal acquisition (echo at TE)**:
   - **Stationary spins** ($\mathbf{r}_1 = \mathbf{r}_2$): phases cancel exactly → full signal recovery.
   - **Moving spins** ($\mathbf{r}_1 \neq \mathbf{r}_2$): residual phase $\Delta\phi = \gamma \mathbf{G}_d \cdot (\mathbf{r}_2 - \mathbf{r}_1) \, \delta$ remains → spins dephase → **signal attenuation** proportional to mean-squared displacement.
6. **Attenuation encodes diffusion** — the larger the diffusion distance, the more dephasing, the lower the measured signal.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **direct KTU board-exam essentials**. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value-like notation to preserve markdown-table integrity.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Symbols & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stejskal–Tanner signal attenuation | $S(b) = S_0 \exp(-b \, D)$ | $S$ = signal, $S_0$ = signal at $b=0$, $D$ = diffusion coefficient ($\text{mm}^2/\text{s}$) |
| 2 | b-value (rectangular gradient) | $b = \gamma^2 \, G^2 \, \delta^2 \left(\Delta - \dfrac{\delta}{3}\right)$ | $\gamma$ = gyromagnetic ratio (rad/s/T), $G$ = gradient amplitude (T/m), $\delta$ = lobe duration (s), $\Delta$ = lobe separation (s) |
| 3 | Apparent Diffusion Coefficient (ADC) | $\text{ADC} = -\dfrac{1}{b} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{S(b)}{S_0}\right)$ | Result in $\text{mm}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 4 | Mean Diffusivity (MD) | $\text{MD} = \dfrac{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3}{3}$ | $\lambda_i$ = eigenvalues of tensor $\mathbf{D}$ |
| 5 | Fractional Anisotropy (FA) | $\text{FA} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}} \dfrac{\sqrt{(\lambda_1-\text{MD})^2+(\lambda_2-\text{MD})^2+(\lambda_3-\text{MD})^2}}{\sqrt{\lambda_1^2+\lambda_2^2+\lambda_3^2}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le \text{FA} \le 1$ |
| 6 | Diffusion tensor eigenvalue problem | $\mathbf{D} \mathbf{e}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{e}_i$ | $\mathbf{e}_i$ = principal eigenvector |
| 7 | Mean-squared displacement (free diffusion) | $\langle x^2 \rangle = 2 D t$ (1D) | $t$ = diffusion time |
| 8 | Eigenvalue for cylindrical geometry | $\lambda_\parallel = D_\parallel$, $\lambda_{\perp 1} = \lambda_{\perp 2} = D_\perp$ | Used in white-matter modelling |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Acute stroke triage** within the first 6-hour therapeutic window.
- **Pre-surgical tractography** — DTI-derived principal eigenvectors are followed voxel-to-voxel to map white-matter tracts (corticospinal, arcuate, optic radiations) and avoid iatrogenic damage.
- **Neuro-degenerative research** — longitudinal FA changes track MS lesion progression, Alzheimer's microstructural damage, and traumatic brain injury.
- **Body applications** — prostate lesion detection, breast cancer characterisation, hepatic fibrosis assessment (using IVIM extensions).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners love this fact:** Without diffusion gradients ($b=0$), the MR image is **T2-weighted**; with diffusion gradients ($b>0$), the image is **T2-weighted × diffusion-attenuated**. This is why *raw* DWI has mixed contrast — and why the **ADC map** (a calculated parametric map) is the diagnostic image, not the raw DWI.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stejskal–Tanner Diffusion Attenuation Law

We derive the fundamental signal-decay law for free (Gaussian) diffusion in a pulsed-gradient spin-echo experiment.

**Starting point:** each spin's accumulated phase after the two gradient lobes is

$$
\phi = \gamma \, \delta \, \mathbf{G} \cdot (\mathbf{r}_2 - \mathbf{r}_1)
$$

The total transverse signal is the vector sum of all spin magnetisations:

$$
S(b) = S_0 \, \langle \exp(i \phi) \rangle
$$

The expectation is taken over the stochastic displacement $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{r}_2 - \mathbf{r}_1$. For free Gaussian diffusion, the displacement probability distribution is

$$
P(\mathbf{R}) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(4\pi D t)^3}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\mathbf{R}\cdot\mathbf{R}}{4 D t}\right)
$$

where $t = \Delta - \delta/3$ is the effective diffusion time for rectangular gradients.

We now evaluate the characteristic function:

$$
\langle \exp(i\phi) \rangle = \left\langle \exp\!\big(i\,\gamma\,\delta\,\mathbf{G}\cdot\mathbf{R}\big) \right\rangle
$$

This is the moment-generating function of a zero-mean Gaussian, which yields

$$
\langle \exp(i\phi) \rangle = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\langle \phi^2 \rangle\right)
$$

The mean-squared phase is

$$
\langle \phi^2 \rangle = \gamma^2 \delta^2 \langle (\mathbf{G}\cdot\mathbf{R})^2 \rangle
$$

Using the isotropic identity $\langle (\mathbf{G}\cdot\mathbf{R})^2 \rangle = G^2 \langle R^2 \rangle / 3$ (in 3-D) and $\langle R^2 \rangle = 2 D t \cdot 3 = 6 D t$, we obtain

$$
\langle \phi^2 \rangle = \gamma^2 \delta^2 G^2 (2 D t) = 2 D \, \gamma^2 \delta^2 G^2 t
$$

Substituting $t = \Delta - \delta/3$:

$$
\langle \phi^2 \rangle = 2 D \, \gamma^2 \delta^2 G^2 \left(\Delta - \dfrac{\delta}{3}\right)
$$

Recognising the bracketed term as the **b-value**, $b = \gamma^2 G^2 \delta^2 \left(\Delta - \delta/3\right)$:

$$
\langle \phi^2 \rangle = 2 D b
$$

Therefore, the final Stejskal–Tanner equation is:

$$
\boxed{\,S(b) = S_0 \exp(-b\,D)\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor of 2 in $\langle \phi^2 \rangle$ comes from the **two** gradient lobes (each contributes equally to phase variance, and variances add).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Apparent Diffusion Coefficient (ADC) from Two-Point Measurement

A clinical DWI scan typically acquires two volumes, one at $b = 0$ (reference) and one at $b = b_1$.

**Stejskal–Tanner at the two b-values:**

$$
S_0 = S_0 \, \exp(0) = S_0 \qquad \text{(trivial at } b=0)
$$

$$
S_1 = S_0 \exp(-b_1 \, \text{ADC})
$$

Taking the natural logarithm of the ratio:

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{S_1}{S_0}\right) = -b_1 \, \text{ADC}
$$

Solving for ADC:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{ADC} = -\dfrac{1}{b_1} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{S_1}{S_0}\right)\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering caveat:** In practice, a *least-squares* fit over **multiple b-values** (e.g., $b = 0, 200, 500, 1000$) is used to improve accuracy and detect non-monoexponential behaviour (intravoxel incoherent motion, restricted diffusion).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Derivation of Fractional Anisotropy from Eigenvalues

We want a dimensionless scalar that quantifies the *degree* of anisotropy of the diffusion tensor $\mathbf{D}$, with $\text{FA} = 0$ for isotropic diffusion and $\text{FA} = 1$ for fully anisotropic (cylindrical) diffusion.

**Step 1 — Mean Diffusivity (trace-based scalar):**

$$
\text{MD} = \dfrac{\text{Tr}(\mathbf{D})}{3} = \dfrac{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3}{3}
$$

This is the rotationally-invariant measure of *magnitude* of diffusion.

**Step 2 — Anisotropy measure (variance of eigenvalues):**

The variance of the eigenvalues around the mean is

$$
\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{3}\left[(\lambda_1 - \text{MD})^2 + (\lambda_2 - \text{MD})^2 + (\lambda_3 - \text{MD})^2\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Normalise by total magnitude (root-mean-square of eigenvalues):**

$$
\langle \lambda^2 \rangle = \dfrac{\lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2}{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine to a unitless, normalised index:**

$$
\text{FA} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{\langle \lambda^2 \rangle}}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\sqrt{3}$ gives the standard form:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{FA} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}} \dfrac{\sqrt{(\lambda_1-\text{MD})^2+(\lambda_2-\text{MD})^2+(\lambda_3-\text{MD})^2}}{\sqrt{\lambda_1^2+\lambda_2^2+\lambda_3^2}}\,}
$$

**Sanity checks:**

- *Isotropic case* ($\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = \lambda$): numerator becomes $\sqrt{0} = 0$ → $\text{FA} = 0$. ✓
- *Linear case* ($\lambda_1 = \lambda, \lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = 0$): substitute and simplify → $\text{FA} = 1$. ✓
- *Cylindrical case* ($\lambda_\parallel, \lambda_\perp, \lambda_\perp$ with $\lambda_\parallel \gg \lambda_\perp$): $0 < \text{FA} < 1$, matching white-matter physiology. ✓

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Eigenvalues of a Diagonalised Diffusion Tensor

For a voxel containing a single fibre population, the diffusion tensor in its principal frame is diagonal:

$$
\mathbf{D} = \begin{pmatrix} \lambda_1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda_2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \lambda_3 \end{pmatrix}
$$

The **principal eigenvector** $\mathbf{e}_1$ corresponding to the largest eigenvalue $\lambda_1$ gives the **dominant fibre direction** in that voxel. Tractography algorithms integrate this direction along the white-matter bundle to reconstruct neural pathways.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

### 4.1 Block Diagram — PGSE Pulse Sequence (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["RF90 EXCITE"] --> B["GRADIENT G1 ON"]
    B --> C["WAIT DELTA DIV 2"]
    C --> D["RF180 REFOCUS"]
    D --> E["GRADIENT G1 ON"]
    E --> F["WAIT DELTA DIV 2"]
    F --> G["ECHO ACQUIRE S"]
    H["TISSUE WATER SPINS"] --> A
    I["STATIONARY SPINS"] --> J["FULL SIGNAL"]
    K["MOVING SPINS"] --> L["DEPHASED SIGNAL"]
    G --> I
    G --> K
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define the **b-value** in diffusion MRI. State the formula for a rectangular gradient pulse and explain the role of each parameter.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **b-value** (units: $\text{s/mm}^2$) is a diffusion-weighting factor that quantifies the sensitivity of an MRI sequence to diffusion. For a Stejskal–Tanner pulsed-gradient spin-echo with rectangular lobes:

$$
b = \gamma^2 \, G^2 \, \delta^2 \left(\Delta - \dfrac{\delta}{3}\right)
$$

- **$\gamma$** — gyromagnetic ratio of the proton ($42.58 \,\text{MHz/T}$).
- **$G$** — amplitude of the diffusion gradient (T/m); contributes quadratically.
- **$\delta$** — duration of each gradient lobe (s).
- **$\Delta$** — time between the centres of the two gradient lobes (s); represents the diffusion-encoding interval.

Larger $b$ → greater diffusion sensitivity → greater signal attenuation. **(1 mark formula + 1 mark parameter explanation + 1 mark role of $b$)**

---

**Q.A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What is the **Apparent Diffusion Coefficient (ADC)**? Why is it called "apparent" rather than the true diffusion coefficient?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

ADC is the quantitative measure of water mobility within tissue, derived from the Stejskal–Tanner relationship $\text{ADC} = -\dfrac{1}{b} \ln(S/S_0)$ and reported in $\text{mm}^2/\text{s}$. **(1 mark)**

It is called *apparent* because the measured value is **not** the true self-diffusion coefficient of free water ($D \approx 3 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{mm}^2/\text{s}$ at $37^\circ\text{C}$). Within tissue, water motion is **restricted** by cell membranes, organelles, macromolecules, and tissue microstructure, so the measured parameter reflects the *effective* or *apparent* diffusion — i.e., the diffusion of water *as it appears* within the tissue environment. **(2 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q.B1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Stejskal–Tanner pulsed gradient spin echo (PGSE) sequence** used in diffusion MRI. Derive the relationship $S = S_0 \exp(-bD)$.

**(b) [7 marks]** For a diffusion-weighted acquisition, $S_0 = 1000$ (a.u.) at $b = 0$ and $S = 400$ (a.u.) at $b = 1000 \,\text{s/mm}^2$. Calculate the **ADC**. Comment on whether the tissue is likely **ischaemic** or **normal**.

---

**Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]:**

1. **Block diagram description [2 marks]:** A 90° RF excitation pulse is followed by a diffusion-encoding gradient lobe of amplitude $G$ and duration $\delta$. After a delay of $\Delta/2$, a 180° refocusing pulse is applied, followed by a second identical gradient lobe at time $\Delta/2$ after the refocusing pulse. The spin echo forms at $TE = 2 \cdot (\text{first half interval})$ and is acquired.

2. **Phase-tag and dephase principle [2 marks]:** The first gradient imparts a position-dependent phase $\phi_1 = \gamma G r_1 \delta$. The second imparts $\phi_2 = -\gamma G r_2 \delta$. Net phase = $\gamma G (r_1 - r_2)\delta$. Stationary spins → net phase = 0; diffusing spins → net phase ≠ 0 → dephasing → signal loss.

3. **Mathematical derivation [3 marks]:** (See Derivation 1 in Section 3.1 for full steps.) Starting from

$$
S(b) = S_0 \langle \exp(i \phi) \rangle
$$

using the Gaussian moment-generating theorem and the effective diffusion time $t = \Delta - \delta/3$:

$$
S(b) = S_0 \exp\!\left(-\gamma^2 G^2 \delta^2 \left(\Delta - \dfrac{\delta}{3}\right) D\right) = S_0 \exp(-bD)
$$

---

**Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Substitute into ADC formula [2 marks]:**

$$
\text{ADC} = -\dfrac{1}{b} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{S}{S_0}\right) = -\dfrac{1}{1000} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{400}{1000}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Numerical evaluation [3 marks]:**

$$
\ln(0.4) = -0.9163
$$

$$
\text{ADC} = -\dfrac{1}{1000} \times (-0.9163) = 9.163 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{mm}^2/\text{s} \approx 0.92 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{mm}^2/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Clinical interpretation [2 marks]:**

Normal brain ADC $\approx 0.8$–$1.0 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{mm}^2/\text{s}$. Acute ischaemic (cytotoxic oedema) ADC $\approx 0.4$–$0.6 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{mm}^2/\text{s}$. The calculated value of $0.92 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{mm}^2/\text{s}$ falls within the **normal** range — likely **normal brain parenchyma**, *not* acute infarction.

---

**Q.B2. [Internal Choice — Alternative Question, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain **Diffusion Tensor Imaging (DTI)**. Define the diffusion tensor $\mathbf{D}$, explain its eigenvalue decomposition, and derive the expressions for **Mean Diffusivity (MD)** and **Fractional Anisotropy (FA)**.

**(b) [7 marks]** For a voxel, the diagonalised diffusion tensor has eigenvalues $\lambda_1 = 1.7$, $\lambda_2 = 0.3$, $\lambda_3 = 0.2$ (all in $\times 10^{-3} \,\text{mm}^2/\text{s}$). Calculate MD and FA, and comment on the tissue type.

---

**Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]:**

1. **DTI definition [2 marks]:** DTI models water diffusion in each voxel as a $3 \times 3$ symmetric positive-definite tensor $\mathbf{D}$, requiring at least 6 non-collinear gradient directions plus one $b=0$ reference. It captures **directional dependence** of diffusion in anisotropic tissue such as white matter.

2. **Eigenvalue decomposition [2 marks]:** $\mathbf{D} \mathbf{e}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{e}_i$. The three eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2 \ge \lambda_3$ and their eigenvectors form the principal diffusion frame. $\mathbf{e}_1$ is the dominant fibre direction.

3. **MD and FA derivation [3 marks]:** (See Section 3.3 Derivation 3 for the full derivation.)

$$
\text{MD} = \dfrac{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3}{3}
$$

$$
\text{FA} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}} \dfrac{\sqrt{(\lambda_1-\text{MD})^2+(\lambda_2-\text{MD})^2+(\lambda_3-\text{MD})^2}}{\sqrt{\lambda_1^2+\lambda_2^2+\lambda_3^2}}
$$

MD measures magnitude; FA measures anisotropy (0 = isotropic, 1 = fully anisotropic).

---

**Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Compute MD [2 marks]:**

$$
\text{MD} = \dfrac{1.7 + 0.3 + 0.2}{3} \times 10^{-3} = \dfrac{2.2}{3} \times 10^{-3} = 0.733 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{mm}^2/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute FA [3 marks]:**

Compute deviations from MD:
- $\lambda_1 - \text{MD} = 1.7 - 0.733 = 0.967$
- $\lambda_2 - \text{MD} = 0.3 - 0.733 = -0.433$
- $\lambda_3 - \text{MD} = 0.2 - 0.733 = -0.533$

Numerator squared sum:
$$
(0.967)^2 + (-0.433)^2 + (-0.533)^2 = 0.935 + 0.187 + 0.284 = 1.406
$$

Denominator squared sum:
$$
(1.7)^2 + (0.3)^2 + (0.2)^2 = 2.89 + 0.09 + 0.04 = 3.02
$$

$$
\text{FA} = \sqrt{1.5} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{1.406}}{\sqrt{3.02}} = 1.2247 \cdot \dfrac{1.1857}{1.7378} = 1.2247 \cdot 0.6823 = 0.836
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation [2 marks]:** MD $\approx 0.73 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{mm}^2/\text{s}$ is *moderately reduced* (typical white matter is $\approx 0.7$–$0.9 \times 10^{-3}$). FA $\approx 0.84$ is **highly anisotropic**, characteristic of **organised white-matter tract** (e.g., corpus callosum or internal capsule), where water diffuses strongly along myelinated axons and is restricted perpendicular to them.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss traps in Diffusion MRI questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $\langle \phi^2 \rangle = 2 b D$ during derivation — the two gradient lobes each contribute, and their variances *add*. Examiners *always* check this.
> 2. **Confusing "diffusion-weighted image" with "ADC map"** — raw DWI is T2 × diffusion, so a T2-bright lesion can mimic restricted diffusion ("T2 shine-through"). Always state that the **ADC map** is the diagnostic image.
> 3. **Mixing up units of $D$** — clinical convention is $\times 10^{-3} \,\text{mm}^2/\text{s}$ in brain; some textbooks use $\mu\text{m}^2/\text{ms}$ (numerically equivalent but different notation). State the unit explicitly.
> 4. **Wrong sign in ADC** — must be $-\dfrac{1}{b}\ln(S/S_0)$. Dropping the negative sign gives negative ADC — an instant zero.
> 5. **FA interpretation error** — high FA does *not* always mean "normal". Tumours infiltrating along tracts can also raise FA in early stages. Always pair with MD.
> 6. **Skipping the b-value formula derivation** — KTU board examiners expect the full $b = \gamma^2 G^2 \delta^2 (\Delta - \delta/3)$ with *each* parameter named. Don't truncate.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Diffusion MRI** measures the Brownian motion of water protons to infer tissue microstructure non-invasively.
- The **Stejskal–Tanner equation** $S = S_0 \exp(-bD)$ is the master relationship; **b-value** quantifies diffusion weighting.
- **b-value** for rectangular gradients: $b = \gamma^2 G^2 \delta^2 (\Delta - \delta/3)$. $b = 0$ → no diffusion weighting (T2-weighted); $b = 1000 \,\text{s/mm}^2$ → standard clinical DWI.
- **ADC** $= -\dfrac{1}{b}\ln(S/S_0)$ — the *true* diagnostic parameter; reported in $\text{mm}^2/\text{s}$ or $\times 10^{-3} \,\text{mm}^2/\text{s}$.
- **PGSE sequence** uses *two* symmetric gradient lobes around a 180° refocusing pulse. Stationary spins rephase; moving spins dephase.
- **DWI** = scalar ADC map (isotropic model). Used routinely for **acute stroke** (dark on ADC), **tumour cellularity** (low ADC), **cysts** (high ADC).
- **DTI** = second-order tensor model; needs ≥ 6 diffusion directions + $b=0$. Yields eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2 \ge \lambda_3$ and eigenvectors.
- **MD** = trace/3 = mean of eigenvalues = rotationally invariant magnitude of diffusion.
- **FA** = unitless anisotropy index (0 to 1). **High FA** = organised white matter; **low FA** = grey matter / CSF / pathology.
- **Principal eigenvector** $\mathbf{e}_1$ = dominant fibre direction; basis of **tractography**.
- **T2 shine-through** artefact: high signal on raw DWI due to long T2, not restricted diffusion — always cross-check with ADC.
- **Clinical thresholds** (brain, $37^\circ\text{C}$): normal white matter ADC $\approx 0.7$–$0.9 \times 10^{-3}$; acute stroke $\approx 0.4$–$0.6 \times 10^{-3}$; CSF $\approx 3.0 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{mm}^2/\text{s}$.
- **Engineering trade-off**: higher b-value → more diffusion contrast, but lower SNR and longer TE → must be balanced clinically.
- **EPI (Echo-Planar Imaging)** is the standard readout for diffusion MRI due to its speed, but it is sensitive to **susceptibility** and **eddy-current** artefacts.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute mantra for the exam hall:** *Stejskal–Tanner attenuates, ADC quantifies, DTI tensor decomposes, FA reports anisotropy, eigenvectors trace fibres, and the ADC map — not the raw DWI — is the diagnostic image.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MRI Fourier Reconstruction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT723 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** MRI Fourier Reconstruction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**MRI Fourier Reconstruction** is the mathematical and signal-processing framework used in Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) to convert the raw, time-domain radiofrequency (RF) signals acquired from hydrogen nuclei (protons) in tissue into a spatially resolved, diagnostically meaningful image. The process is governed by the **k-space formalism**, in which the acquired MR signal $S(t)$ is mathematically equivalent to the **2-Dimensional Fourier Transform** of the spin density distribution $\rho(x,y)$ of the imaged object.

The fundamental governing equation is:

$$S(k_x, k_y) = \iint \rho(x,y)\, e^{-j2\pi(k_x x + k_y y)}\, dx\, dy$$

and its inverse:

$$\rho(x,y) = \iint S(k_x, k_y)\, e^{+j2\pi(k_x x + k_y y)}\, dk_x\, dk_y$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The relationship between the **measured MR signal** and the **image (object) space** is a **2D Fourier Transform pair**. This is the single most important concept for all MRI reconstruction-based questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – k-space:** k-space is a temporary, abstract, spatial-frequency domain in which raw MRI data is stored *before* image reconstruction. Each point in k-space holds information from *every* pixel in the final image, not just one. It is the analog of the "frequency spectrum" of the image.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a swimming pool, and your eyes are closed. You can hear swimmers moving in the water. The sound waves arriving at your ear are a complex mixture of many frequencies. If you had a perfect "mathematical ear" that could perform a **Fourier decomposition** on that sound, you could determine **where** each swimmer is located in the pool.

**MRI works exactly like this:**

| Pool Analogy | MRI Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Swimmers in water | Hydrogen protons ($^1$H) in tissue |
| Sound waves reaching your ear | RF signal received by the coil |
| Fourier decomposition of sound | 2D Fourier Transform of the signal |
| Position of each swimmer | Pixel intensity in the reconstructed image |
| Different tones (bass / treble) | Low / high spatial frequencies in k-space |

- **Low spatial frequencies** (near the **center of k-space**) carry information about the **overall shape and contrast** of the image — like a blurry silhouette.
- **High spatial frequencies** (toward the **edges of k-space**) carry information about **fine edges and sharp details** — like crisp boundaries.

> [!TIP]
> A common student pitfall: **k-space is NOT the image.** It is the *Fourier domain* of the image. Filling k-space $\rightarrow$ inverse Fourier Transform $\rightarrow$ final image.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value (1.5 T Scanner) |
|---|---|---|
| Gyromagnetic ratio (proton) | $\gamma$ | **42.58 MHz/T** |
| Field strength | $B_0$ | 1.5 T or 3.0 T |
| Larmor frequency | $\omega_0$ | **63.87 MHz** (at 1.5 T) |
| Pixel bandwidth | $\Delta f$ | **±15.6 kHz** (typical) |
| Echo time | TE | 1 ms – 100 ms |
| Repetition time | TR | 100 ms – 5000 ms |
| Typical matrix | $N_x \times N_y$ | **256 × 256** |
| Field of View | FOV | 200–400 mm |

> [!NOTE]
> All equations in this module assume SI units, with $k_x$ and $k_y$ in **cycles/meter (m⁻¹)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The MR Signal Equation — Operational Breakdown

The MR signal in the **rotating frame of reference** (rotating at the Larmor frequency $\omega_0$) is described step-by-step below.

**Step 1 — Net Magnetization After Excitation**
A 90° RF pulse tips the longitudinal magnetization $M_0$ into the transverse plane:

$$M_{xy}(0) = M_0 \, \hat{x}$$

**Step 2 — Precession Under Applied Gradient**
With a linear magnetic field gradient $G_x$ applied along x, the local Larmor frequency becomes position-dependent:

$$\omega(x) = \gamma \, G_x \, x$$

**Step 3 — Received Signal from a Voxel**
A small volume element $dV$ at position $x$ contributes a signal:

$$dS(t) = \rho(x) \, e^{-j\gamma G_x x t}\, dx$$

where $\rho(x)$ is the **spin density** (the actual image content).

**Step 4 — Total Signal (Integration Over Object)**
Summing the contributions of all volume elements:

$$S(t) = \int \rho(x) \, e^{-j\gamma G_x x t}\, dx$$

**Step 5 — Introduction of k-space Variable**
Define the k-space coordinate:

$$k_x = \frac{\gamma G_x t}{2\pi} \quad \left[ \text{cycles/m} \right]$$

Then the signal becomes the **Fourier Transform** of $\rho(x)$:

$$S(k_x) = \int \rho(x)\, e^{-j2\pi k_x x}\, dx$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Time $t$ in MRI is mathematically equivalent to the k-space coordinate $k$. By varying gradient strength and duration, the scanner *walks* through k-space, sampling the Fourier transform of the object.

---

## 2.2 Extension to 2D — Phase and Frequency Encoding

To obtain a 2D image, MRI applies **two gradients in orthogonal directions**:

1. **Frequency Encoding (Readout Gradient, $G_x$)**
   - Applied *during* signal acquisition.
   - Encodes position along x into signal **frequency**.
   - A single readout acquires a full *line* (or **echo**) of k-space.

2. **Phase Encoding (Phase Gradient, $G_y$)**
   - Applied *briefly* between excitation and readout.
   - Encodes position along y into signal **phase**.
   - Each repetition (TR) uses a different $G_y$ amplitude to sample a *different row* of k-space.

The 2D signal is:

$$S(k_x, k_y) = \iint \rho(x,y)\, e^{-j2\pi(k_x x + k_y y)}\, dx\, dy$$

with k-space coordinates:

$$k_x(t) = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} \int_0^t G_x(\tau)\, d\tau, \qquad k_y(t) = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} \int_0^{t_{PE}} G_y(\tau)\, d\tau$$

---

## 2.3 The Image Reconstruction Equation

Once the full k-space matrix $S(k_x, k_y)$ is filled, the image is recovered by the **Inverse 2D Discrete Fourier Transform (2D IDFT)**:

$$\rho(m\Delta x, n\Delta y) = \frac{1}{N_x N_y} \sum_{p=0}^{N_x-1} \sum_{q=0}^{N_y-1} S(p\Delta k_x, q\Delta k_y)\, e^{+j2\pi\left(\frac{pm}{N_x} + \frac{qn}{N_y}\right)}$$

where $\Delta x, \Delta y$ are pixel dimensions and $N_x, N_y$ are matrix sizes.

> [!TIP]
> In modern scanners, the IDFT is implemented using the **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** algorithm, reducing computation from $O(N^4)$ to $O(N^2 \log_2 N)$.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ | Larmor precession frequency |
| 2 | $k_x(t) = \dfrac{\gamma}{2\pi}\displaystyle\int_0^t G_x(\tau)\, d\tau$ | k-space coordinate in x |
| 3 | $k_y = \dfrac{\gamma G_y T_{PE}}{2\pi}$ | k-space coordinate in y (blipped gradient) |
| 4 | $S(k_x, k_y) = \mathcal{F}\{\rho(x,y)\}$ | Signal is FT of object |
| 5 | $\rho(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{S(k_x, k_y)\}$ | Image is inverse FT of signal |
| 6 | $\Delta k_x = \dfrac{1}{\text{FOV}_x}$ | k-space sampling step (readout) |
| 7 | $\Delta k_y = \dfrac{1}{\text{FOV}_y}$ | k-space sampling step (phase) |
| 8 | $k_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot \text{pixel size}}$ | Maximum k-space extent (Nyquist) |
| 9 | $T_{\text{readout}} = \dfrac{N_x}{\text{BW}}$ | Readout window duration |
| 10 | $\text{BW} = \dfrac{\gamma G_x \cdot \text{FOV}_x}{2\pi}$ | Receiver bandwidth per pixel |
| 11 | $\text{SNR} \propto \text{FOV}_x \cdot \text{FOV}_y \cdot \sqrt{N_{avg}} \cdot \sqrt{N_x N_y}$ | SNR scaling (key scan parameter) |
| 12 | $G_x = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot \text{BW}}{\gamma \cdot \text{FOV}_x}$ | Readout gradient amplitude |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU exam answers, **always state the units** of $k_x$ and $k_y$ explicitly. Examiners frequently deduct 1 mark for missing units.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of Fourier Reconstruction |
|---|---|
| **Diagnostic Radiology** | Produces T1, T2, and proton-density weighted images of soft tissue |
| **Functional MRI (fMRI)** | Detects BOLD signal via EPI (Echo Planar Imaging) Fourier recon |
| **Diffusion MRI / DTI** | Uses q-space sampling → Fourier → ADC / fiber tract maps |
| **MR Spectroscopy** | 1D/2D FT separates chemical shift peaks |
| **Real-time Cardiac MRI** | Radial k-space sampling + sliding-window reconstruction |
| **Compressed Sensing MRI** | Sparse k-space sampling + non-linear iterative reconstruction |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Bloch Equations to the MR Signal Equation

We begin with the **transverse magnetization** $M_{xy}(t) = M_x + jM_y$ in a frame rotating at the Larmor frequency $\omega_0$.

**Step 1 — Rotating Frame Assumption**
In the rotating frame, the static field $B_0$ effect vanishes. The effective field is only due to applied gradients $G(t)$:

$$B_{\text{eff}}(x,y,z,t) = \vec{r} \cdot \vec{G}(t)$$

**Step 2 — Local Precession Frequency**
For a spin at position $\vec{r} = (x,y,z)$:

$$\omega(\vec{r},t) = \gamma\, \vec{r} \cdot \vec{G}(t)$$

**Step 3 — Phase Accumulation**
The phase accumulated by a spin between time $0$ and $t$ is:

$$\phi(\vec{r},t) = \int_0^t \omega(\vec{r},\tau)\, d\tau = \gamma \int_0^t \vec{r}\cdot\vec{G}(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\vec{k}(t)$ Definition**
Define the **k-space vector**:

$$\vec{k}(t) = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} \int_0^t \vec{G}(\tau)\, d\tau$$

Then the phase simplifies to:

$$\phi(\vec{r},t) = 2\pi\, \vec{k}(t)\cdot \vec{r}$$

**Step 5 — Total Signal from the Object**
The signal received is the integral of the spin density weighted by the accumulated phase:

$$S(t) = \int_{\text{object}} \rho(\vec{r})\, e^{-j 2\pi \vec{k}(t)\cdot \vec{r}}\, d\vec{r}$$

**Step 6 — Recognition as Fourier Transform**
This integral is precisely the **Fourier Transform** of the spin density, evaluated at spatial frequency $\vec{k}(t)$:

$$\boxed{\,S(\vec{k}) = \mathcal{F}\{\rho(\vec{r})\} \equiv \iint \rho(\vec{r})\, e^{-j2\pi \vec{k}\cdot \vec{r}}\, d\vec{r}\,}$$

**Step 7 — Inverse Operation for Image**
By the **Fourier Inversion Theorem**, the image is recovered by:

$$\boxed{\,\rho(\vec{r}) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{S(\vec{k})\} = \iint S(\vec{k})\, e^{+j2\pi \vec{k}\cdot \vec{r}}\, d\vec{k}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the *theoretical cornerstone* of MRI. KTU examiners often ask for either the full derivation or the final boxed equation. Memorize both forms.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nyquist Sampling Condition in k-Space

The **Field of View (FOV)** is set by the spacing between k-space samples, and **spatial resolution** is set by the *extent* of k-space.

**Step 1 — Sampling Step in k-space**
In a Cartesian acquisition, samples are taken on a rectangular grid with spacing $\Delta k_x$ and $\Delta k_y$.

**Step 2 — Nyquist Theorem Applied to Spatial Frequencies**
A signal that is **band-limited** in image space (i.e., spatially confined to FOV) must be sampled with spacing:

$$\Delta k_x \le \frac{1}{\text{FOV}_x}, \qquad \Delta k_y \le \frac{1}{\text{FOV}_y}$$

For exact sampling:

$$\Delta k_x = \frac{1}{\text{FOV}_x}, \qquad \Delta k_y = \frac{1}{\text{FOV}_y}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum k-Space Value**
For a matrix of $N_x \times N_y$ points, the maximum k-space coordinates are:

$$k_{x,\max} = N_x \cdot \Delta k_x = \frac{N_x}{\text{FOV}_x}, \qquad k_{y,\max} = N_y \cdot \Delta k_y = \frac{N_y}{\text{FOV}_y}$$

**Step 4 — Pixel Size (Resolution)**
The pixel dimensions in image space are:

$$\Delta x = \frac{1}{2 k_{x,\max}} = \frac{\text{FOV}_x}{N_x}, \qquad \Delta y = \frac{\text{FOV}_y}{N_y}$$

**Step 5 — Worked Example (Likely KTU Problem)**
> *A scan uses FOV = 240 mm and matrix 256 × 256. Compute pixel size and required k-space extent.*

Given:
- $\text{FOV}_x = \text{FOV}_y = 240$ mm $= 0.24$ m
- $N_x = N_y = 256$

$$\Delta x = \Delta y = \frac{0.24}{256} = 9.375 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \approx 0.94 \text{ mm}$$

$$k_{\max} = \frac{256}{0.24} = 1066.67 \text{ cycles/m}$$

**Step 6 — Gradient Strength Required**
With readout time $T_{\text{readout}} = 5.12$ ms, the readout gradient must be:

$$G_x = \frac{2\pi k_{\max}}{\gamma \, T_{\text{readout}}} = \frac{2\pi \times 1066.67}{2\pi \times 42.58 \times 10^6 \times 5.12 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{\,G_x \approx 4.89 \text{ mT/m}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effect of a 180° Refocusing Pulse (Spin Echo)

A 180° RF pulse applied at $t = TE/2$ **reverses** the sign of the accumulated phase. This is captured mathematically as:

**Before refocusing** (dephasing): $\phi = 2\pi \vec{k}_{\text{pre}} \cdot \vec{r}$

**After refocusing** (rephasing): $\phi = 2\pi (\vec{k}_{\text{pre}} + \vec{k}_{\text{post}}) \cdot \vec{r}$

At the echo time $t = TE$, $\vec{k}_{\text{pre}} = -\vec{k}_{\text{post}}$, so $\vec{k}(TE) = 0$. The signal peaks at this instant — the **gradient echo / spin echo** event — and k-space traversal is symmetric.

> [!TIP]
> The 180° pulse does not change the *information content*; it only **delays** and **re-focuses** the signal so that it can be acquired when $T_2$ decay is still small.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Reconstruction Variants Summary

| Method | k-Space Trajectory | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spin Echo / GRE (Cartesian)** | Rectilinear rows | Simple, robust, FFT-friendly | Long scan time |
| **EPI (Echo Planar Imaging)** | Zig-zag raster | Very fast ($\sim$50 ms/slice) | Susceptible to off-resonance |
| **Spiral** | Spiral from origin | Motion robust, efficient | Requires gridding + NUFFT |
| **Radial (PROPELLER / BLADE)** | Rotating spokes | Robust to motion, undersampling | Streak artifacts |
| **Partial Fourier** | Half + conjugate symmetry | ~30–40 % time saving | Slightly lower SNR |
| **Parallel (SENSE / GRAPPA)** | Subsampled Cartesian | Faster, fewer artifacts | Coil geometry dependent |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

### **Q1. Define k-space. Why is it called the "spatial frequency domain"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

k-space is a temporary 2D (or 3D) matrix in which the MRI scanner stores raw signal data **before** Fourier reconstruction into the final image. **(1 mark)**

It is called the spatial frequency domain because the axes $k_x$ and $k_y$ represent **spatial frequencies** (in cycles per meter), not spatial positions. **(1 mark)**

Mathematically, k-space data $S(k_x, k_y)$ is the 2D Fourier Transform of the spin density $\rho(x,y)$ of the object, where $k_x = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi}\int G_x\,dt$ and $k_y = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi}\int G_y\,dt$. **(1 mark)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**

### **Q2. State and explain the Nyquist sampling condition in MRI k-space.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Nyquist condition requires that the spacing of k-space samples must satisfy: **(1 mark)**

$$\Delta k_x = \frac{1}{\text{FOV}_x}, \qquad \Delta k_y = \frac{1}{\text{FOV}_y}$$

If $\Delta k$ is too large (i.e., FOV is smaller than the object), **aliasing / wrap-around artifacts** occur, where signals from outside the FOV fold back into the image. **(1 mark)**

Conversely, the *maximum* k-space extent determines the **pixel size** (spatial resolution):

$$\Delta x = \frac{1}{2 k_{x,\max}} = \frac{\text{FOV}_x}{N_x}$$

Therefore, the matrix size $N_x \times N_y$ sets resolution, while the FOV sets the sample spacing. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main, Module 2 Choice 1]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**(a)** Derive the MR signal equation in k-space, starting from the rotating-frame Bloch equation. Show that the received signal is the 2D Fourier Transform of the spin density. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** With a 256 × 256 matrix and FOV of 250 mm, compute (i) pixel size, (ii) k-space extent $k_{\max}$, and (iii) the readout gradient amplitude required if readout time is 4 ms. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** In the rotating frame at Larmor frequency $\omega_0$, the static field is removed, leaving only gradient fields.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The local precession frequency for a proton at position $\vec{r}$ is:
$$\omega(\vec{r},t) = \gamma \vec{r}\cdot \vec{G}(t)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** The accumulated phase up to time $t$ is:
$$\phi(\vec{r},t) = \int_0^t \gamma \vec{r}\cdot \vec{G}(\tau)\, d\tau = 2\pi \vec{k}(t)\cdot \vec{r}$$

where $\vec{k}(t) = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi}\int_0^t \vec{G}(\tau)\, d\tau$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The transverse magnetization after a 90° pulse is $M_0$. The received signal from a small element $d\vec{r}$ is:
$$dS(t) = \rho(\vec{r})\, e^{-j2\pi \vec{k}(t)\cdot \vec{r}}\, d\vec{r}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Integrating over the object:
$$S(\vec{k}) = \int \rho(\vec{r})\, e^{-j2\pi \vec{k}\cdot \vec{r}}\, d\vec{r}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** This is recognized as the **2D Fourier Transform** of $\rho(\vec{r})$.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** The image is recovered by the **Inverse Fourier Transform**:
$$\rho(\vec{r}) = \int S(\vec{k})\, e^{+j2\pi \vec{k}\cdot \vec{r}}\, d\vec{k}$$

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $N_x = N_y = 256$, $\text{FOV} = 250$ mm $= 0.25$ m, $T_{\text{readout}} = 4$ ms, $\gamma = 2\pi \times 42.58 \times 10^6$ rad/s/T.

**(i) Pixel size (2 marks):**
$$\Delta x = \Delta y = \frac{\text{FOV}}{N} = \frac{0.25}{256} = 9.77 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \approx 0.98 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) k-space extent (2 marks):**
$$k_{\max} = \frac{N}{2\cdot \text{FOV}} = \frac{256}{2 \times 0.25} = 512 \text{ cycles/m}$$

(Equivalently $k_{x,\max} = N/FOV = 1024$ cycles/m depending on convention used; both accepted.)

**(iii) Readout gradient (3 marks):**
$$G_x = \frac{2\pi k_{\max}}{\gamma \, T_{\text{readout}}}$$

$$G_x = \frac{2\pi \times 512}{2\pi \times 42.58 \times 10^6 \times 4 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{G_x \approx 3.0 \text{ mT/m}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the role of **phase encoding** and **frequency encoding** gradients in populating k-space. Include a labeled timing diagram. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the consequences of (i) doubling the FOV while keeping matrix size fixed, (ii) doubling the matrix size while keeping FOV fixed, on spatial resolution, k-space extent, and scan time. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Frequency Encoding (4 marks):**
- The readout gradient $G_x$ is applied **continuously during signal acquisition**.
- It makes the Larmor frequency position-dependent: $\omega(x) = \omega_0 + \gamma G_x x$.
- A single ADC readout collects an entire *row* of k-space, with $k_x$ varying linearly in time.
- The bandwidth determines the k-space step $\Delta k_x = \gamma G_x \Delta t$.

**Phase Encoding (3 marks):**
- The phase-encode gradient $G_y$ is applied as a **short blip** between the excitation and readout.
- It imparts a position-dependent phase $\phi(y) = \gamma G_y y T_{PE}$ to spins along y.
- **Each TR uses a different $G_y$ amplitude**, sampling a different $k_y$ row.
- $N_y$ phase-encode steps are required to fill the entire k-space $\Rightarrow$ determines minimum scan time $= N_y \cdot \text{TR}$.

> [!NOTE]
> A **labeled timing diagram** is mandatory for full marks. Refer to **Section 4.4** in this module for the block schematic.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) FOV doubled, matrix fixed (3.5 marks):**

| Parameter | Effect |
|---|---|
| Pixel size | Doubles (resolution worsens) |
| $\Delta k$ | Halves |
| $k_{\max}$ | Unchanged |
| Scan time | Unchanged |

The image looks *more zoomed out* but with *less detail* per pixel.

**(ii) Matrix doubled, FOV fixed (3.5 marks):**

| Parameter | Effect |
|---|---|
| Pixel size | Halves (resolution improves 2×) |
| $\Delta k$ | Unchanged |
| $k_{\max}$ | Doubles |
| Scan time | Doubles (more phase-encode steps) |
| SNR | Falls by $\sqrt{2}$ (smaller voxels) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently write *"matrix size = resolution"* without distinguishing FOV effects. Always tie resolution to **pixel size = FOV / N**, not to N alone. Losing 1–2 marks per omission is common.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones in MRI Fourier Reconstruction Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to take the 2π factor** when converting $k$ to gradient integrals. The correct form is $k(t) = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi}\int G\,dt$, not $\gamma \int G\,dt$. (–1 mark typical)
> 2. **Mixing up "k-space" with "image space"** in conceptual answers. Always explicitly state: *"k-space is the Fourier domain of the image."* (–1 mark)
> 3. **Omitting units of $k_x$ and $k_y$.** Should be **cycles/m** or **m⁻¹**. (–0.5 mark)
> 4. **Confusing FOV with resolution.** FOV $\propto 1/\Delta k$; resolution $\propto 1/k_{\max}$. Examiners are strict on this distinction.
> 5. **Writing 2D FT without limits or explaining the inverse.** Always pair the forward and inverse relations for full credit.
> 6. **Skipping the rotating-frame justification** in the derivation. KTU expects at least one sentence mentioning why the rotating frame is used.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — MRI Fourier Reconstruction**

- ☐ MRI signal is the **2D Fourier Transform** of spin density; image is recovered by **inverse 2D FT**.
- ☐ **k-space** is the spatial-frequency domain; NOT the image.
- ☐ **k-space trajectory** is controlled by gradient integrals: $\vec{k}(t) = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi}\int_0^t \vec{G}(\tau)\, d\tau$.
- ☐ **Frequency encoding (readout)** $\rightarrow$ populates k-space along $k_x$ (rows).
- ☐ **Phase encoding (blipped)** $\rightarrow$ populates k-space along $k_y$ (one line per TR).
- ☐ **FOV** is set by k-space step: $\Delta k = 1/\text{FOV}$.
- ☐ **Resolution** is set by k-space extent: $\Delta x = 1/(2k_{\max}) = \text{FOV}/N$.
- ☐ **Nyquist** requires $\Delta k \le 1/\text{FOV}$; violation $\Rightarrow$ aliasing.
- ☐ **Matrix size $N$**: trade-off between resolution, scan time, and SNR.
- ☐ Modern scanners use **FFT** to perform reconstruction in $O(N^2 \log N)$ time.
- ☐ Specialized trajectories: **EPI** (fast), **Spiral** (motion robust), **Radial** (undersampling friendly).
- ☐ **Partial Fourier** and **parallel imaging** (SENSE/GRAPPA) reduce scan time.
- ☐ The **180° refocusing pulse** in spin-echo cancels dephasing from static inhomogeneities, leaving only $T_2$ decay.
- ☐ Constant reminder: $k_x$ and $k_y$ have units of **cycles per meter** (m⁻¹), not radians.
- ☐ The **gyromagnetic ratio** for protons: $\gamma = 2\pi \times 42.58$ MHz/T = **42.58 MHz/T** (angular form).

---

> [!TIP]
> **End-of-Module Mnemonic — "FRIDGE"**
> **F**ourier transform pairs the signal and image.
> **R**eadout (frequency) gradient fills rows of k-space.
> **I**nverse FT reconstructs the image.
> **D**istance between samples $\Rightarrow$ FOV.
> **G**radient integral $\Rightarrow$ k-space coordinate.
> **E**xtent of k-space $\Rightarrow$ Resolution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of X-Ray - Properties and Production - X-Ray Tube - Fixed Anode Type and Rotating Anode Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT723 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basics of X-Ray - Properties and Production - X-Ray Tube - Fixed Anode Type and Rotating Anode Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of X-Rays

**X-rays** are a form of **electromagnetic radiation** with wavelengths ranging from approximately **0.01 to 10 nanometers (nm)**, corresponding to frequencies between **$3 \times 10^{16}$ Hz and $3 \times 10^{19}$ Hz**, and photon energies between **100 eV and 100 keV**. They occupy the portion of the electromagnetic spectrum between ultraviolet light and gamma rays, discovered by **Wilhelm Conrad Röntgen** on **November 8, 1895**, for which he received the very first **Nobel Prize in Physics in 1901**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Definition):** X-rays are ionizing electromagnetic radiation produced by the sudden deceleration of high-speed electrons (Bremsstrahlung) or by electronic transitions in inner atomic shells (Characteristic radiation) when a high-energy electron beam strikes a metal target inside a vacuum tube.

### Key Physical Constants Used in X-Ray Physics

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | $3 \times 10^{8}$ | m/s |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | J·s |
| Electron charge | $e$ | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ | C |
| Electron rest mass | $m_e$ | $9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ | kg |
| Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0$ | $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ | F/m |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "X-Rays are Like an Invisible Flashlight":**
> Imagine you are in a completely dark room and you want to see the bones inside a sealed suitcase without opening it. An ordinary flashlight (visible light) cannot penetrate the suitcase. However, if you had a "super-flashlight" emitting waves so short and energetic that they pass right through the suitcase but get blocked by the dense metallic bones (or by dense bone tissue), you could outline the bones. **X-rays behave exactly like this super-flashlight.** They are produced by violently stopping fast-moving electrons (the "bullet") at a tungsten metal target (the "wall"). When the bullets hit, two things happen: (1) the bullets slow down and their kinetic energy converts into a continuous spray of X-rays (**Bremsstrahlung**, German for "braking radiation"), and (2) some bullets knock inner-shell electrons out of tungsten atoms, and when outer electrons fall in to fill the vacancy, they emit X-rays of very specific energies (**Characteristic X-rays**). This is exactly what happens inside every medical X-ray machine.

## 1.3 Fundamental Properties of X-Rays

X-rays exhibit the following clinically significant properties:

1. **Electromagnetic Wave Nature:** X-rays are transverse electromagnetic waves with no mass, no charge, and travel at the speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s in vacuum. They obey the wave equation:

$$c = f \lambda = \nu \lambda$$

where $f$ (or $\nu$) is the frequency in Hz and $\lambda$ is the wavelength in meters.

2. **Photon (Particle) Nature:** Despite being waves, X-rays also exhibit particle-like behavior. The energy of a single X-ray photon is given by the **Planck-Einstein relation**:

$$E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$$

3. **Ionizing Power:** X-rays have enough energy (> 10 eV) to eject electrons from atoms, creating ion pairs. This is the basis of biological damage and is also what allows them to be detected by gas-filled detectors and solid-state sensors.

4. **Penetrating Power:** X-rays pass through materials that absorb or reflect visible light. Penetration is inversely related to the **atomic number (Z)** and **density (ρ)** of the absorbing material and directly related to the X-ray photon energy.

5. **No Deflection by Electric or Magnetic Fields:** Being electrically neutral, X-rays travel in straight lines unaffected by electrostatic or magnetostatic fields.

6. **Photochemical Effect:** X-rays darken photographic films (the basis of film-based radiography) and produce latent images.

7. **Fluorescence Effect:** X-rays cause certain materials like **zinc sulfide (ZnS)** and **calcium tungstate (CaWO$_4$)** to emit visible light (the basis of intensifying screens).

8. **Biological Effect:** X-rays damage or destroy living tissue, especially rapidly dividing cells. This is the basis of radiotherapy and the reason for radiation safety protocols.

9. **Inverse Square Law:** The intensity of an X-ray beam decreases with the square of the distance from a point source:

$$I \propto \dfrac{1}{d^2}$$

10. **Blackening of Lead (Photographic Effect on Lead):** At high intensity, X-rays can darken lead salts, useful in industrial radiography NDT.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 X-Ray Production — The Two Fundamental Mechanisms

X-rays are produced inside a **Coolidge-type vacuum diode tube** (invented by **William D. Coolidge in 1913**) by accelerating electrons emitted from a heated cathode (filament) toward a high atomic-number target (anode) using a high potential difference (typically **30 kV to 150 kV** in diagnostic radiology).

When these high-velocity electrons strike the target, their kinetic energy is converted into X-ray photons via **two distinct physical processes**:

### 2.1.1 Bremsstrahlung Radiation (Braking / Continuous Spectrum)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Bremsstrahlung* (German: *bremsen* = to brake, *Strahlung* = radiation) refers to the X-ray photons produced when a high-speed electron is decelerated or deflected by the strong electric field of a target atom's nucleus. Because the electron can lose **any** fraction of its kinetic energy in a single interaction, the resulting X-ray spectrum is **continuous** from zero up to a maximum photon energy equal to the entire kinetic energy of the incident electron.

**Step-by-step logic of Bremsstrahlung production:**

1. The thermionic cathode emits electrons (filament heated to ~2400 °C).
2. A high voltage $V$ (in volts) accelerates each electron to kinetic energy $E_k = eV$.
3. The electron enters the target material (typically tungsten, Z = 74).
4. The electron passes near the nucleus of a target atom and is decelerated by the **Coulomb field** of the nucleus.
5. According to classical electromagnetism, a decelerating charged particle emits electromagnetic radiation.
6. The electron may lose 0% to 100% of its kinetic energy in a **single interaction**, producing photons of all possible energies from 0 to $E_k$.
7. The result is a **continuous X-ray spectrum** with a **short-wavelength cutoff** $\lambda_{min}$.

### 2.1.2 Characteristic X-Ray Radiation (Line Spectrum)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Characteristic X-rays are produced when an inner-shell (K-shell or L-shell) electron of the target atom is ejected by an incident high-energy electron, and an electron from a higher shell drops down to fill the vacancy, releasing a photon whose energy equals the difference in binding energies of the two shells.

**Step-by-step logic of Characteristic radiation:**

1. The incident electron strikes an inner-shell (K-shell) electron of the target atom.
2. The K-shell electron is ejected from the atom (provided the incident energy exceeds the K-edge binding energy).
3. This creates a vacancy in the K-shell, leaving the atom in an excited, ionized state.
4. An electron from a higher shell (L, M, or N) drops down to fill the K-shell vacancy.
5. The energy difference is emitted as a photon of very specific, discrete energy.
6. Transitions from L → K produce **K$_\alpha$ X-rays**, M → K produce **K$_\beta$ X-rays**, etc.
7. For tungsten target: $K_\alpha = 59.3$ keV, $K_\beta = 67.2$ keV (these are characteristic of the target material, hence the name).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description / Boundary Conditions |
|---|--------------|----------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | Photon energy (Planck-Einstein) | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | $E$ in J or eV; $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s |
| 2 | Minimum wavelength (Duane-Hunt Law) | $\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV} = \dfrac{12400}{V(\text{in V})} \text{ nm}$ | $V$ in volts; $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C |
| 3 | Maximum photon energy | $E_{max} = eV \text{ (in eV)} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{min}}$ | $E_{max}$ in keV when $V$ is in kV |
| 4 | Wave equation | $c = f\lambda$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 5 | Intensity (point source) | $I = \dfrac{P}{4\pi d^2}$ | $P$ = power of source; $d$ = distance |
| 6 | HVL (Half-Value Layer) relation | $I = I_0 \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{x/\text{HVL}}$ | $x$ = thickness of absorber |
| 7 | Linear attenuation coefficient | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | $\mu$ depends on material and photon energy |
| 8 | Mass attenuation coefficient | $\dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | $\rho$ = density of material (kg/m³) |
| 9 | Tungsten K-edge | $E_{K} = 69.5$ keV | For Z = 74 (tungsten) |
| 10 | Electromagnetic spectrum position | $0.01 \text{ nm} \le \lambda \le 10 \text{ nm}$ | Diagnostic X-ray range is ~0.01–0.1 nm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion trick for KTU exams:** $\lambda_{min} \text{ (in Å)} = \dfrac{12.4}{V \text{ (in kV)}}$, where 1 Å = 0.1 nm. This is a much faster form to use in numerical problems and is **frequently asked** in KTU university examinations.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The X-ray tube and its production physics form the foundation of:

- **Diagnostic Radiography** (chest X-rays, dental X-rays, mammography)
- **Computed Tomography (CT)** — uses rotating-anode X-ray tubes
- **Fluoroscopy** — real-time X-ray imaging using image intensifiers
- **Industrial Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** — finding cracks in welds and pipelines
- **Airport Security Scanning** — baggage inspection systems
- **Crystallography** — X-ray diffraction to determine crystal structures (Nobel Prize-winning work)
- **Radiotherapy** — using high-energy (>1 MeV) X-rays to destroy tumors

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Duane-Hunt Law (Minimum Wavelength)

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU university exams for Medical Imaging Techniques.

> **Problem Statement:** An electron of charge $e$ is accelerated from rest through a potential difference $V$. On striking the target, it is completely stopped in a single interaction. Derive the expression for the minimum wavelength of the emitted X-ray photon.

### Derivation (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):

**Step 1 — Energy gained by the electron from the accelerating voltage:**

When an electron (charge $e$) is accelerated from rest through a potential difference $V$, the work done on the electron by the electric field becomes its kinetic energy. By the work-energy theorem:

$$W = eV = E_k$$

**Step 2 — Maximum kinetic energy of the incident electron:**

Since the electron starts essentially from rest at the cathode, its kinetic energy just before striking the target is:

$$E_k = eV$$

**Step 3 — Condition for maximum photon energy (X-ray photon with minimum wavelength):**

By the principle of conservation of energy, the **maximum possible photon energy** is emitted when the **entire** kinetic energy of the electron is converted into a **single** X-ray photon. (This is the upper limit — it happens very rarely.)

$$E_{photon,max} = E_{electron} = eV$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Planck-Einstein relation to the photon:**

The energy of a photon is given by:

$$E_{photon} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$$

For the maximum-energy photon (which has the minimum wavelength $\lambda_{min}$):

$$E_{photon,max} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{min}}$$

**Step 5 — Equate the two expressions for energy:**

Using conservation of energy, set the kinetic energy of the electron equal to the energy of the maximum-energy photon:

$$\dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{min}} = eV$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\lambda_{min}$:**

$$\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the numerical values of the constants:**

Using $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, and $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C:

$$\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{(1.602 \times 10^{-19}) V}$$

$$\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{V}$$

$$\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{1.2406 \times 10^{-6}}{V} \text{ meters}$$

**Step 8 — Express the result in convenient units (nanometers and volts):**

$$\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{1240 \text{ nm·V}}{V} = \dfrac{12400 \text{ Å·V}}{V}$$

Or, equivalently, in Angstroms with kV:

$$\lambda_{min} \text{ (in Å)} = \dfrac{12.4}{V \text{ (in kV)}}$$

**Final Result (Duane-Hunt Law):**

$$\boxed{\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV} = \dfrac{12400}{V} \text{ Å} = \dfrac{1.24}{V} \text{ nm}}$$

where $V$ is the accelerating voltage in volts.

## 3.2 Worked Example (Frequently Asked KTU Numerical)

> **Question:** An X-ray tube operates at a tube voltage of 100 kV. Calculate (a) the maximum kinetic energy of the electrons striking the target, (b) the minimum wavelength of the emitted X-rays, and (c) the maximum frequency of the emitted X-rays.

### Solution:

**Part (a) — Maximum kinetic energy of the electron:**

$$E_{k,max} = eV = (1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}) \times (100 \times 10^{3} \text{ V})$$

$$E_{k,max} = 1.602 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J} = 100 \text{ keV}$$

**Part (b) — Minimum wavelength (Duane-Hunt Law):**

Using $\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{12400 \text{ Å·V}}{V}$:

$$\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{12400}{100000} = 0.124 \text{ Å} = 0.0124 \text{ nm}$$

**Part (c) — Maximum frequency:**

Using $c = f_{max} \lambda_{min}$:

$$f_{max} = \dfrac{c}{\lambda_{min}} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}}{0.0124 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}}$$

$$f_{max} = 2.42 \times 10^{19} \text{ Hz}$$

## 3.3 X-Ray Intensity Distribution

The total X-ray intensity emitted from a thick target is given by **Kramers' Law** (semi-empirical):

$$I = K \cdot Z \cdot I_e \cdot V^2$$

where:
- $K$ = constant (≈ $1.1 \times 10^{-9}$ to $1.4 \times 10^{-9}$ V$^{-1}$ for tungsten)
- $Z$ = atomic number of the target
- $I_e$ = electron beam current (in amperes)
- $V$ = tube voltage (in volts)

**Key takeaway:** The X-ray intensity is **proportional to $Z$** (which is why high-Z targets like tungsten are used) and **proportional to $V^2$** (which is why higher kV gives more X-rays but also shifts the spectrum to higher energies).

## 3.4 Efficiency of X-Ray Production

The efficiency $\eta$ of X-ray production is extremely small (about 1% even at 100 kV):

$$\eta = \dfrac{\text{X-ray power output}}{\text{Electron beam power input}} = K \cdot Z \cdot V$$

For a tungsten target at 100 kV: $\eta \approx 0.8\%$. The remaining 99.2% of the electron energy is converted to **heat**, which is why the anode must be made of high-melting-point material and rotated to dissipate heat (this directly leads us to the rotating-anode tube design).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: X-Ray Production System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HVPS["High Voltage Power Supply 30 to 150 kV DC"]
    FIL["Filament Heating Supply 3 to 5 V AC 3 to 5 A"]
    XRT["X-Ray Tube Vacuum Enclosure"]
    CAT["Cathode Filament Tungsten Wire"]
    ANS["Anode Tungsten Target Embedded in Copper"]
    WIN["Beryllium Window 0.5 to 1 mm"]
    PAT["Patient Body Tissue Bone Soft Tissue"]
    DET["Image Detector Film or Digital Panel"]
    HVPS -->|High Voltage| XRT
    FIL -->|Low Voltage AC| CAT
    CAT -->|Thermionic Electrons| ANS
    ANS -->|Bremsstrahlung Photons| WIN
    WIN -->|Useful X-Ray Beam| PAT
    PAT -->|Attenuated Beam| DET
```

## 4.2 Fixed Anode X-Ray Tube — Cross-Sectional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GLASS["Glass or Metal Envelope Vacuum 10 to the power of minus 6 Torr"]
    CATHODE["Cathode Assembly"]
    FILH["Filament Helical Tungsten Wire"]
    CUP["Focusing Cup Nickel"]
    ANODE["Anode Tungsten Target"]
    COPPER["Copper Stem Heat Sink"]
    WINDOW["Beryllium Exit Window"]
    CATHODE --> FILH
    CATHODE --> CUP
    ANODE --> COPPER
    GLASS --> CATHODE
    GLASS --> ANODE
    GLASS --> WINDOW
    FILH -->|emits electrons| ANODE
    ANODE -->|X-ray photons emitted backward| WINDOW
    WINDOW -->|useful beam exits| PAT["Patient Image Plane"]
```

## 4.3 Rotating Anode X-Ray Tube — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    STATOR["Stator External Induction Motor Windings"]
    ROTOR["Rotor Inside Vacuum Attached to Anode"]
    MOLYBDENUM["Molybdenum Stem Low Thermal Conductance"]
    ANODE["Tungsten Target Disc Diameter 70 to 200 mm"]
    BEARING["Ball Bearings High Temperature Lubricated"]
    CATHODE["Cathode Assembly with Focusing Cup"]
    BERYL["Beryllium Window"]
    STATOR -->|induces rotation| ROTOR
    ROTOR -->|carries| ANODE
    ANODE -->|connected via| MOLYBDENUM
    MOLYBDENUM -->|supported by| BEARING
    CATHODE -->|emits electron beam onto track| ANODE
    ANODE -->|X-ray photons through beryl window| BERYL
```
