# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Bremsstrahlung radiation. Why is it called "braking radiation"?**

**Model Answer:**

Bremsstrahlung (German for "braking" radiation) is the continuous-spectrum X-ray emission that occurs when a high-speed electron is decelerated or deflected by the strong Coulomb field of a target atom's nucleus inside an X-ray tube. It is called "braking radiation" because the X-rays are produced precisely by the **braking action** of the target nuclei on the incident electrons. The electron loses kinetic energy during this braking, and that energy is converted into electromagnetic radiation. Because the electron can lose **any** fraction of its energy (from 0 to 100%) in a single interaction, the resulting X-ray spectrum is **continuous** from zero intensity up to a maximum photon energy equal to the incident electron's full kinetic energy $E_k = eV$. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**State and explain the Duane-Hunt Law for the minimum wavelength of X-rays.**

**Model Answer:**

The Duane-Hunt Law states that the **minimum wavelength** $\lambda_{min}$ of X-rays produced in an X-ray tube is **inversely proportional to the accelerating voltage** $V$ applied across the tube, and is given by:

$$\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$$

**Explanation:** When an electron accelerated through potential difference $V$ strikes the target, its maximum kinetic energy is $eV$. If the electron loses all this energy in a single interaction, it produces a single X-ray photon of maximum energy $E_{max} = eV$. Using $E = hc/\lambda$, this photon has the **minimum wavelength** $\lambda_{min} = hc/eV$. Substituting constants, $\lambda_{min} \text{ (in Å)} = 12400/V$. For example, at $V = 50$ kV, $\lambda_{min} = 0.248$ Å. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Following KTU ESE 2024 Scheme, answer **ONE** full question from the two choices below. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **PART B — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **fixed anode X-ray tube**. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A fixed anode X-ray tube (also called a stationary anode tube) is the simplest design used in low-to-medium power applications such as dental radiography and basic diagnostic units.

**Construction:**

1. **Glass/Metal Envelope:** A vacuum-sealed envelope (pressure $\sim 10^{-6}$ Torr) provides electrical insulation between the anode and cathode and prevents oxidation of the filament.
2. **Cathode Assembly:** Consists of a **helical tungsten filament** (acting as the source of electrons by thermionic emission) mounted inside a **nickel focusing cup** that directs the electron beam toward a small area on the anode.
3. **Anode Assembly:** A **tungsten target** (Z = 74, melting point 3422 °C) embedded in a **copper stem**, which acts as a heat sink. Tungsten is chosen because of its high atomic number (efficient X-ray production) and high melting point.
4. **Beryllium Window:** A thin (~0.5 mm) beryllium exit window allows the X-ray beam to leave the tube with minimal attenuation.

**Working:**

1. The filament is heated by a low-voltage AC supply (~3–5 V, 3–5 A) to ~2400 °C, releasing electrons by thermionic emission.
2. A high DC voltage (30–150 kV) applied between cathode and anode accelerates the electrons.
3. The accelerated electrons strike the tungsten target at very high velocity.
4. At the target, ~99% of the electron energy is converted to **heat** and ~1% to **X-rays** (Bremsstrahlung + Characteristic).
5. X-rays exit through the beryllium window, while heat is conducted away through the copper anode stem.

**Advantages:**
- Simple, compact, and inexpensive design.
- Reliable with long operational life.
- Suitable for low-power, low-duty-cycle applications (dental, portable units).

**Limitations:**
- Small focal spot limits X-ray output power.
- Heat dissipation is poor — the same spot is hit by all electrons continuously, leading to target damage (pitting, cracking).
- Cannot be used for high-power applications like CT.

**(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Derive the **Duane-Hunt Law** for the minimum wavelength of X-rays emitted from an X-ray tube. A diagnostic X-ray tube operates at **80 kV**. Calculate the minimum wavelength and maximum frequency of the emitted X-rays. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:** (Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for full step-by-step derivation)

The energy of an electron accelerated through potential $V$ is $E_k = eV$. The maximum photon energy equals the electron's kinetic energy (when all energy is converted in a single interaction):

$$\dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{min}} = eV \implies \lambda_{min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given $V = 80 \text{ kV} = 80{,}000 \text{ V}$.

**Minimum wavelength:**

$$\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{12400 \text{ Å·V}}{80000 \text{ V}} = 0.155 \text{ Å} = 0.0155 \text{ nm}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(80000)} = 1.55 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}$$

**Maximum frequency:**

$$f_{max} = \dfrac{c}{\lambda_{min}} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{1.55 \times 10^{-11}} = 1.935 \times 10^{19} \text{ Hz}$$

**(7 marks)**

---

### **PART B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with a block diagram the construction and working of a **rotating anode X-ray tube**. Why is it preferred over a fixed anode tube in CT scanners? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A rotating anode X-ray tube is a high-power tube design in which the tungsten target is mounted on a rotor that spins at high speed (3000–10,000 RPM), allowing the electron beam to be distributed over a much larger annular area of the target (the "track") rather than a single stationary spot.

**Construction:**

1. **Anode Disc:** A large tungsten-rhenium alloy disc (70–200 mm diameter) is mounted on a rotor shaft.
2. **Molybdenum Stem:** Connects the disc to the rotor; molybdenum is used because of its **low thermal conductivity** (it allows heat to radiate outward rather than conduct to the bearings).
3. **Rotor and Stator:** The rotor is inside the vacuum, the stator is outside. Electromagnetic induction spins the rotor without mechanical contact.
4. **Ball Bearings:** High-temperature, silver-lead lubricated bearings support the rotor.
5. **Cathode Assembly:** Two filaments (small and large focal spot) with focusing cups.
6. **Beryllium Window and Metal Housing:** Oil-cooled, lead-lined housing for safety.

**Working:**

1. The stator windings energize first, accelerating the rotor to operating speed.
2. The filament is heated to emit electrons.
3. High voltage accelerates the electrons to the anode.
4. The electron beam strikes the **annular track** of the rotating disc.
5. The heat generated at any one point on the track is spread over a much larger area as the disc rotates — drastically improving heat dissipation.
6. X-rays are emitted at a 90° angle (or ~7–20° for some designs) from the focal track and exit through the beryllium window.

**Why Preferred Over Fixed Anode in CT Scanners:**

| Feature | Fixed Anode | Rotating Anode |
|---------|-------------|----------------|
| Focal spot | Large (1–2 mm) | Small (0.3–1 mm) |
| Power rating | 1–3 kW | 30–100 kW |
| Heat capacity | Low | ~10–20× higher |
| Spatial resolution | Lower | Higher (small focal spot) |
| CT suitability | Poor — would melt | Excellent |

In CT, the tube must rotate around the patient and produce thousands of high-resolution slices in seconds. This requires very high instantaneous power and excellent heat dissipation — both provided by the rotating anode. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Differentiate between **Bremsstrahlung** and **Characteristic X-ray** production mechanisms. For a tungsten target, calculate the energy of the K$_\alpha$ photon given that the K-shell binding energy is 69.5 keV and the L-shell binding energy is 10.2 keV. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Bremsstrahlung | Characteristic X-ray |
|-----------|----------------|----------------------|
| Origin | Deceleration of electron in nuclear Coulomb field | Electronic transition between atomic shells |
| Spectrum | Continuous (0 to $E_{max}$) | Line spectrum (discrete peaks) |
| Energy range | Variable, depends on $V$ | Fixed by shell binding energies |
| Depends on $V$? | Yes — $\lambda_{min} \propto 1/V$ | No — depends only on target material |
| Depends on target Z? | Yes — $I \propto Z$ | Yes — energies increase with Z (Moseley's law) |
| Photon energy relation | $E \le eV$ | $E = E_K - E_L$ for K$_\alpha$ |
| Diagnostic use | Forms the bulk of useful beam | Adds discrete peaks to spectrum |

**Calculation of K$_\alpha$ photon energy for tungsten:**

Given:
- K-shell binding energy: $E_K = 69.5$ keV
- L-shell binding energy: $E_L = 10.2$ keV

The K$_\alpha$ photon is emitted when an L-shell electron drops into the K-shell vacancy:

$$E_{K_\alpha} = E_K - E_L = 69.5 - 10.2 = 59.3 \text{ keV}$$

Converting to Joules:

$$E_{K_\alpha} = 59.3 \times 10^3 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 9.50 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J}$$

The corresponding wavelength is:

$$\lambda_{K_\alpha} = \dfrac{hc}{E_{K_\alpha}} = \dfrac{12400 \text{ Å·V}}{59300 \text{ V}} = 0.209 \text{ Å}$$

**(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mixing:** Students frequently write $V$ in kV but then use $eV$ directly in Joules — losing 1–2 marks. Always convert kV → V before multiplying by $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C.
> 2. **Forgetting constants:** The numerical constant $\lambda_{min} = 12400/V$ is in **Ångstroms**, not nanometers. KTU examiners explicitly check unit consistency.
> 3. **Confusing X-ray types:** Many students write "Characteristic X-rays are produced by deceleration" — this is wrong. Bremsstrahlung is by deceleration, Characteristic is by electronic transition. Deduct 2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping derivation steps:** For 7-mark Duane-Hunt derivations, students must show **at least 6 explicit steps**. Skipping "let $E_{photon} = hc/\lambda$" loses a mark.
> 5. **Drawing a Coolidge tube but labeling a Crookes tube:** Examiners check whether your diagram labels include **vacuum**, **focusing cup**, **beryllium window**, and **cooling fins** — missing two of these = 1 mark deduction.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **X-rays are EM waves** with $\lambda \in [0.01, 10]$ nm, produced by stopping fast electrons on a high-Z target.
- ✅ **Two production mechanisms:** **Bremsstrahlung** (continuous spectrum, braking) and **Characteristic** (line spectrum, shell transitions).
- ✅ **Duane-Hunt Law (memorize):** $\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{12400 \text{ Å·V}}{V \text{ (in V)}}$ or $\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{12.4}{V \text{ (in kV)}}$ Å.
- ✅ **Maximum photon energy:** $E_{max} = eV$ in eV (numerically equal to $V$ in volts).
- ✅ **Only ~1% of electron energy** becomes X-rays — the rest is **heat** (this drives the rotating-anode design).
- ✅ **Tungsten** is the standard target because of its high Z (=74) and melting point (3422 °C).
- ✅ **Fixed anode tube:** Simple, cheap, low power (1–3 kW), used in dental/portable units.
- ✅ **Rotating anode tube:** Disc spins at 3000–10,000 RPM, distributes heat over an annular track, allows 30–100 kW operation, used in CT and angiography.
- ✅ **Beryllium window** is used (not glass) because of its low Z and high X-ray transmission.
- ✅ **Focal spot size** is critical: smaller focal spot = better spatial resolution but lower heat tolerance.
- ✅ **Tube housing** is lead-lined and oil-cooled for radiation safety and heat management.
- ✅ **Heel effect:** X-ray intensity is non-uniform across the field — stronger on the cathode side (use in mammography positioning).
- ✅ **Coolidge tube (1913)** is the basis of all modern X-ray tubes — features thermionic emission and high-vacuum operation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Sectional Imaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT723 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principles of Sectional Imaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Sectional Imaging (Tomography)** is the process of generating two-dimensional (2D) cross-sectional images of a three-dimensional (3D) anatomical object without physically cutting it, by using mathematical reconstruction algorithms applied to a series of one-dimensional (1D) projection measurements acquired from multiple angular orientations around the object.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, sectional imaging primarily encompasses two pillar technologies:
- **Computed Tomography (CT)** — based on X-ray attenuation profiles (Cormack & Hounsfield, Nobel Prize 1979).
- **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** — based on Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) of hydrogen protons (Lauterbur & Mansfield, Nobel Prize 2003).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The principle revolves around (a) acquiring *projections* of the slice, (b) transforming projection data into image space using the *Radon Transform / Fourier Slice Theorem*, and (c) reconstructing a tomographic map of the physical property (linear attenuation coefficient $\mu$ for CT, proton density / relaxation times for MRI).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you are standing inside a dark room holding a flashlight. You cannot see the room's full contents, but if you walk around the room and shine the flashlight from many directions, recording the *shadow* (silhouette) the objects cast on the opposite wall each time, you could mathematically *reconstruct* the shape and density of every object inside — even those hidden behind others. This is exactly what sectional imaging does:

| Real-World Analogy | Sectional Imaging Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Flashlight (light source) | X-ray tube (CT) or RF coil (MRI) |
| Shadow on the wall | 1D Projection / Sinogram row |
| Walking around the room | Rotating gantry at angle $\theta$ |
| Mathematical reconstruction of shapes | Inverse Radon / Filtered Back Projection |
| Final 2D map of the room | Cross-sectional image (slice) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Sectional imaging is fundamentally an **inverse problem** — we measure *effects* (attenuated X-rays or emitted RF signals) and mathematically invert them to recover the *cause* (the spatial distribution of tissue properties).

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of light | $c$ | $3 \times 10^{8}$ | m/s |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | J·s |
| Gyromagnetic ratio of Hydrogen | $\gamma$ | $42.58$ | MHz/T |
| Water linear attenuation (at 73 keV) | $\mu_{water}$ | ~0.195 | cm⁻¹ |
| Larmor frequency (at 1.5 T) | $\omega_0$ | **63.87** | MHz |
| Standard CT number for water | $HU_{water}$ | **0** | Hounsfield Units |
| Standard CT number for air | $HU_{air}$ | **−1000** | Hounsfield Units |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Fundamental Steps of Sectional Imaging
The workflow of every sectional imaging modality is identical in philosophy, differing only in the *physics* of the projection measurement:

**Step 1 — Data Acquisition (Forward Projection):**
The imaging system measures how an external energy probe (X-ray photon, RF excitation) is modified as it traverses the object along many straight-line paths (rays) at many angles $\theta \in [0°, 180°]$ (or $360°$ in modern helical CT).

**Step 2 — Image Reconstruction (Inverse Problem):**
A mathematical operator (Inverse Radon Transform, Filtered Back Projection, or iterative reconstruction) is applied to the projection dataset (called a **sinogram**) to recover the spatial map $f(x, y)$ of the tissue property.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Tomography
Conventional radiography superimposes all anatomical structures along the X-ray path onto a single 2D detector plane, causing **anatomical structure overlap**. A 2D projection $P$ of a 3D object is mathematically a *lossy compression* of information. By acquiring projections at multiple angles and exploiting the fact that different tissues have *different* linear attenuation coefficients $\mu(x, y, z)$, the inverse problem becomes *uniquely solvable*.

### 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula bank** for KTU 2024 ESE questions on Principles of Sectional Imaging. Memorize every row.

| # | Formula / Equation | Name | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$ | Lambert-Beer Law (Attenuation) | CT: intensity after thickness $x$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | $I = I_0 \, \exp\!\left[-\int_L \mu(x,y)\, ds\right]$ | Line integral of attenuation | CT: ray-sum / projection $p$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | $p(\rho, \theta) = \int_L f(x,y)\, ds$ | Radon Transform definition | Forward projection operator | varies |
| 4 | $f(x,y) = \int_0^{\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} P(\omega, \theta) \cdot \vert\omega\vert \cdot e^{j 2\pi \omega \rho}\, d\omega\, d\theta$ | Filtered Back Projection (FBP) | CT/MRI reconstruction | varies |
| 5 | $HU = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{\mu_{tissue} - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}}$ | Hounsfield Unit (CT Number) | CT: image intensity scaling | HU |
| 6 | $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ | Larmor Frequency | MRI: precession frequency | rad/s or Hz |
| 7 | $M_{xy}(t) = M_{xy}(0)\, e^{-t/T_2}$ | T₂ Transverse Decay | MRI: spin–spin relaxation | seconds |
| 8 | $M_z(t) = M_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/T_1}\right)$ | T₁ Longitudinal Recovery | MRI: spin–lattice relaxation | seconds |
| 9 | $\Delta\omega = \gamma \cdot G \cdot x$ | Frequency Encoding Relation | MRI: spatial localization | Hz |
| 10 | $\text{SNR} \propto B_0^{7/4}$ | MRI Signal-to-Noise | MRI: field-strength scaling | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\text{CTDI} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} D_i$ | CT Dose Index | CT: radiation dose metric | mGy |
| 12 | $F(k) = \int f(x)\, e^{-j 2\pi k x}\, dx$ | Fourier Transform | Slice theorem bridge | varies |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** When evaluating the line integral along a ray at angle $\theta$ and offset $\rho$, the substitution $x = \rho \cos\theta - s \sin\theta$ and $y = \rho \sin\theta + s \cos\theta$ is used. KTU questions frequently test this geometric mapping.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Radiation Therapy Planning:** CT reconstructions provide electron density maps required for dose calculation in linear accelerators.
- **Image-Guided Surgery:** MRI's superior soft-tissue contrast enables neurosurgical navigation without ionizing radiation.
- **Functional Imaging Extensions:** The sectional imaging principle extends to **PET** (Positron Emission Tomography) and **SPECT** (Single Photon Emission CT) by replacing X-ray transmission with gamma-ray emission detection.
- **Industrial Non-Destructive Testing (NDT):** The same Radon-based reconstruction is used to inspect turbine blades, rocket fuel rods, and pipeline welds.
- **Geophysics & Seismology:** CT reconstruction algorithms are used in seismic tomography to image the Earth's subsurface.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Lambert-Beer Law to the Radon Transform
This derivation shows how a single X-ray measurement along a straight line becomes one *sample* of the Radon transform.

**Starting Point — Lambert-Beer Law for a homogeneous medium:**

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

**Step 1 — Generalize to a heterogeneous medium (piecewise constant assumption):**
The object is divided into infinitesimal segments of thickness $ds$, each with its own attenuation $\mu(s)$:

$$I = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[-\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mu(s_i)\, \Delta s\right]$$

**Step 2 — Take the continuum limit (let $\Delta s \to 0$):**

$$I = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[-\int_L \mu(s)\, ds\right]$$

**Step 3 — Define the projection (ray-sum) $p$ as the negative logarithm of the intensity ratio:**
This converts the exponential relationship into a linear integral — the key step enabling linear reconstruction:

$$p = -\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = \int_L \mu(s)\, ds$$

**Step 4 — Express the line $L$ in terms of projection geometry (offset $\rho$ and angle $\theta$):**
A ray passing through the object at angle $\theta$ and perpendicular distance $\rho$ from the origin satisfies the equation:

$$x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta = \rho$$

**Step 5 — Parameterize the line using a running coordinate $s$ along the ray:**

$$x(s) = \rho \cos\theta - s \sin\theta$$
$$y(s) = \rho \sin\theta + s \cos\theta$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into the integral to obtain the formal Radon Transform:**

$$p(\rho, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f\!\left(\rho \cos\theta - s \sin\theta,\ \rho \sin\theta + s \cos\theta\right) ds$$

where $f(x, y) = \mu(x, y)$ is the unknown 2D attenuation map we wish to reconstruct.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Radon Transform is the foundation of all CT mathematics.** Memorizing its definition and the geometric meaning of $(\rho, \theta)$ is essential for KTU Part B derivations.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: The Fourier Slice Theorem (Central Slice Theorem)
The Fourier Slice Theorem is the *bridge* between projection space and image space and is the basis of the Filtered Back Projection algorithm.

**Step 1 — Take the 1D Fourier Transform of the projection $p(\rho, \theta)$ with respect to $\rho$:**

$$P(\omega, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} p(\rho, \theta)\, e^{-j 2\pi \omega \rho}\, d\rho$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Radon Transform expression for $p(\rho, \theta)$:**

$$P(\omega, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x,y)\, ds\right] e^{-j 2\pi \omega \rho}\, d\rho$$

**Step 3 — Change integration order and apply coordinate rotation:**
Define the rotated Fourier-space variables $u = \omega \cos\theta$ and $v = \omega \sin\theta$. The line integral combined with the Fourier kernel collapses elegantly to:

$$P(\omega, \theta) = F(u, v) = F(\omega \cos\theta,\ \omega \sin\theta)$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result geometrically:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 1D Fourier Transform of a projection at angle $\theta$ equals a 1D slice through the 2D Fourier transform of the object, taken at the same angle $\theta$.** Acquiring projections at all angles fills the entire 2D Fourier space (called *k-space* in MRI).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Hounsfield Unit (CT Number)
**Step 1 — Define a normalized attenuation difference relative to water:**

$$\Delta\mu = \mu_{tissue} - \mu_{water}$$

**Step 2 — Normalize by the attenuation of water to make the scale dimensionless:**

$$\text{Normalized } \Delta\mu = \frac{\mu_{tissue} - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by 1000 to obtain integer-scaled Hounsfield Units:**

$$HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu_{tissue} - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}}$$

**Step 4 — Verification using boundary cases:**
- For water: $HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu_{water} - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}} = 0$ ✓
- For air: $HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{0 - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}} = -1000$ ✓
- For dense bone ($\mu_{bone} \approx 2 \mu_{water}$): $HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{2\mu_{water} - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}} = +1000$ ✓

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Larmor Frequency for MRI
**Step 1 — A proton possesses an intrinsic nuclear spin angular momentum $I = \tfrac{1}{2}\hbar$.**
**Step 2 — Placed in static magnetic field $B_0$, the spin experiences a torque causing precession.**
**Step 3 — The precession (Larmor) angular frequency is given by:**

$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$$

where $\gamma$ is the gyromagnetic ratio.

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation at $B_0 = 1.5$ T:**

$$\omega_0 = (42.58 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz/T}) \times 1.5 \text{ T} = 63.87 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 5 — Frequency encoding: applying a linear gradient $G$ along $x$ gives a spatially varying field $B(x) = B_0 + G \cdot x$, so each spatial location has a unique frequency $\omega(x) = \gamma(B_0 + Gx)$.** This is the physical basis of MRI's spatial encoding.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)
**Problem:** A CT projection measures $I_0 = 10{,}000$ photons and $I = 2{,}000$ photons through a uniform tissue of thickness $x = 5$ cm. Calculate the linear attenuation coefficient $\mu$.

**Solution:**

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x} \implies 2000 = 10000 \cdot e^{-\mu \cdot 5}$$

$$\frac{2000}{10000} = e^{-5\mu} \implies 0.2 = e^{-5\mu}$$

$$\ln(0.2) = -5\mu \implies -1.6094 = -5\mu$$

$$\mu = 0.3219 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

The CT number relative to water: $HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{0.3219 - 0.195}{0.195} \approx 650$ HU (consistent with dense bone or contrast-enhanced tissue).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Sectional Imaging Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Object Slice 3D Tissue] --> B[Energy Probe]
    B --> B1[X-ray Photons CT]
    B --> B2[RF Pulses MRI]
    B1 --> C[Attenuation Along Ray]
    B2 --> C1[Precession Signal]
    C --> D[Detector Array 1D]
    C1 --> D
    D --> E[Sinogram Projection Data]
    E --> F[Inverse Radon Transform]
    F --> G[Filtered Back Projection]
    G --> H[2D Cross Sectional Image]
    H --> I[Hounsfield Map CT]
    H --> J[Relaxation Map MRI]
    I --> K[Display and Diagnosis]
    J --> K
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — CT Geometry and Projection Acquisition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[X-ray Source] -->|Rotates around| B[Object F xy]
    B -->|Attenuates| C[Detector Array]
    C --> D[Projection p rho theta]
    D --> E[Sinogram Storage]
    E --> F[Reconstruction CPU]
    F --> G[CT Image HU Map]
    subgraph ANG[Angle Sweep]
    H[theta 0 deg] --> I[theta 45 deg]
    I --> J[theta 90 deg]
    J --> K[theta 135 deg]
    K --> L[theta 180 deg]
    end
    ANG --> E
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — MRI Sequential Acquisition Blocks

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Magnet B0 Polarization] --> B[RF Excitation 90 deg Pulse]
    B --> C[Net Magnetization Tilted to xy Plane]
    C --> D[Gradient Gx Frequency Encoding x axis]
    D --> E[Gradient Gy Phase Encoding y axis]
    E --> F[Free Induction Decay FID Signal]
    F --> G[Analog to Digital Conversion]
    G --> H[k-space Filling Line by Line]
    H --> I[2D Inverse Fourier Transform]
    I --> J[MRI Magnitude Image]
    J --> K[T1 weighted or T2 weighted Display]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Fourier Slice Theorem Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Object f xy in Image Space] --> B[Take Projection at angle theta]
    B --> C[p rho theta in Sinogram]
    C --> D[1D Fourier Transform along rho]
    D --> E[P omega theta equals 1D slice of F uv at angle theta]
    E --> F[Acquire Projections for all theta 0 to 180]
    F --> G[2D Fourier Space F uv Filled Completely]
    G --> H[2D Inverse Fourier Transform]
    H --> I[Reconstructed f xy Slice]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visual Tip:** The Mermaid diagram above represents the **Fourier Slice Theorem**, which is the conceptual heart of CT/MRI reconstruction. In exams, draw a 2D Fourier plane with radial lines (each line = one projection angle) and label the slice theorem relationship clearly.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
*Define the Radon Transform. Explain the geometric meaning of the parameters $\rho$ and $\theta$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Radon Transform of a 2D function $f(x, y)$ is the line integral of $f$ along a straight line $L$ parameterized by its perpendicular distance $\rho$ from the origin and its angle $\theta$ with respect to the x-axis:

$$p(\rho, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(\rho \cos\theta - s \sin\theta,\ \rho \sin\theta + s \cos\theta)\, ds$$

- $\rho$ (rho): the **perpendicular offset** of the ray from the origin, measured along the detector axis. It can take values in $(-\infty, \infty)$ but is bounded by the object diameter.
- $\theta$ (theta): the **angular orientation** of the ray bundle, ranging over $[0°, 180°)$ for a parallel-beam CT. At $\theta = 0°$ the ray travels along the y-axis; at $\theta = 90°$ along the x-axis.

The set of all $p(\rho, \theta)$ for one slice is stored in a 2D array called the **sinogram**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*What is the Larmor frequency? Calculate the Larmor frequency of a hydrogen proton in a 1.5 T MRI scanner.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Larmor frequency** $\omega_0$ is the precession frequency of a nuclear magnetic moment (e.g., a hydrogen proton) when placed in a static external magnetic field $B_0$. It is given by:

$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$$

where $\gamma = 42.58$ MHz/T is the gyromagnetic ratio of hydrogen.

**Calculation for 1.5 T:**

$$\omega_0 = 42.58 \text{ MHz/T} \times 1.5 \text{ T} = 63.87 \text{ MHz}$$

This frequency lies in the **radio-frequency (RF) range** of the electromagnetic spectrum, which is why MRI uses RF coils (and not X-ray tubes) for signal generation.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Question with Internal Choice

**Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the Radon Transform of a 2D function $f(x, y)$ from the basic principle of X-ray attenuation through a heterogeneous medium. Clearly state the geometric meaning of each variable used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Fourier Slice Theorem. Using this theorem, describe how the Filtered Back Projection (FBP) algorithm reconstructs a CT image from its projections. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation of the Radon Transform (7 marks):**
- **Step 1 (1 mark):** Begin with Lambert-Beer law for homogeneous medium: $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$.
- **Step 2 (1 mark):** Extend to heterogeneous medium using a line integral: $I = I_0 \exp\left[-\int_L \mu(s)\, ds\right]$.
- **Step 3 (1 mark):** Define projection $p = -\ln(I/I_0) = \int_L \mu(s)\, ds$.
- **Step 4 (1 mark):** Introduce geometric parameters $\rho$ and $\theta$, with the ray equation $x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta = \rho$.
- **Step 5 (1 mark):** Parameterize the ray using $s$ (running coordinate along the ray) and apply the rotation substitution.
- **Step 6 (1 mark):** Write the final Radon Transform expression and explain that $f(x, y) = \mu(x, y)$ is the unknown to be reconstructed.
- **Step 7 (1 mark):** Mention that the set of all projections for one slice forms a *sinogram*.

**(b) Fourier Slice Theorem and FBP (7 marks):**
- **Step 1 (1.5 marks):** State the theorem: the 1D Fourier transform of a projection at angle $\theta$ equals a 1D radial slice through the 2D Fourier transform $F(u, v)$ of the object at the same angle.
- **Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Write the mathematical relationship: $P(\omega, \theta) = F(\omega \cos\theta, \omega \sin\theta)$.
- **Step 3 (1 mark):** Explain that acquiring projections for $\theta \in [0°, 180°]$ fills the 2D Fourier space radially.
- **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Describe FBP algorithm: (i) take 1D FT of each projection, (ii) multiply by ramp filter $\vert \omega \vert$ in frequency domain to compensate for $1/\vert\omega\vert$ density of radial sampling, (iii) take inverse 1D FT, (iv) back-project (smear) each filtered projection back across the image grid, (v) sum all back-projected images.
- **Step 5 (1 mark):** Write the FBP integral equation: $f(x, y) = \int_0^{\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} P(\omega, \theta) \vert\omega\vert e^{j2\pi\omega(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)}\, d\omega\, d\theta$.
- **Step 6 (0.5 marks):** Mention the role of the ramp filter (sharpens edges, removes blur from simple back-projection).

---

**Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the basic principle of Computed Tomography. Derive an expression for the CT number (Hounsfield Unit) and compute the HU of muscle ($\mu_{muscle} \approx 0.215$ cm⁻¹) and fat ($\mu_{fat} \approx 0.165$ cm⁻¹) relative to water ($\mu_{water} = 0.195$ cm⁻¹). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the basic principle of MRI. Derive the Larmor frequency equation and briefly describe T₁ and T₂ relaxation mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) CT Principle and Hounsfield Unit (7 marks):**
- **Step 1 (1 mark):** State CT principle: an X-ray tube rotates around the patient, acquiring 1D projections at many angles; an inverse Radon transform reconstructs a 2D slice of attenuation coefficient $\mu(x, y)$.
- **Step 2 (1 mark):** Write the Hounsfield formula: $HU = 1000 \cdot (\mu_{tissue} - \mu_{water})/\mu_{water}$.
- **Step 3 (1 mark):** State boundary cases: water = 0 HU, air = −1000 HU.
- **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Compute HU for muscle: $HU = 1000 \cdot (0.215 - 0.195)/0.195 = 1000 \cdot 0.1026 = +102.6$ HU.
- **Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Compute HU for fat: $HU = 1000 \cdot (0.165 - 0.195)/0.195 = 1000 \cdot (-0.1538) = -153.8$ HU.
- **Step 6 (1 mark):** Interpret: muscle has higher attenuation than water (positive HU); fat has lower attenuation (negative HU). This differential is what enables tissue contrast in CT.

**(b) MRI Principle, Larmor Frequency, Relaxation (7 marks):**
- **Step 1 (1 mark):** State MRI principle: hydrogen protons in the body are placed in a strong $B_0$ field, excited by a 90° RF pulse at the Larmor frequency, and the resulting Free Induction Decay (FID) signal is measured and spatially encoded using gradients.
- **Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Derive Larmor frequency: $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$, where $\gamma = 42.58$ MHz/T for hydrogen. Precession arises from the torque $\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}$ on the magnetic moment.
- **Step 3 (1 mark):** Mention that at 1.5 T, $\omega_0 = 63.87$ MHz; at 3 T, $\omega_0 = 127.74$ MHz.
- **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Explain T₁ (longitudinal / spin–lattice) relaxation: the excited spins transfer energy to the surrounding lattice (molecular environment), causing $M_z$ to recover toward $M_0$ as $M_z(t) = M_0(1 - e^{-t/T_1})$.
- **Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Explain T₂ (transverse / spin–spin) relaxation: dephasing among spins due to local magnetic field inhomogeneities causes $M_{xy}$ to decay as $M_{xy}(t) = M_{xy}(0) e^{-t/T_2}$. Note $T_2 \le T_1$.
- **Step 6 (0.5 marks):** Mention that different tissues have different $T_1$ and $T_2$ values, providing the *soft-tissue contrast* that MRI is famous for.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Units Mix-up (–1 mark):** Many students write Larmor frequency in Hz when the equation $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ gives angular frequency in rad/s. Use $f_0 = \gamma B_0 / (2\pi)$ for Hz.
> 2. **Sign Error in HU (–1 mark):** Students often forget the sign convention. Fat has *negative* HU, not positive.
> 3. **Skipping Geometric Substitution (–2 marks):** In Radon Transform derivations, students frequently write the integral without parameterizing the line $L$ in terms of $\rho, \theta, s$. Always show the substitution.
> 4. **Confusing CT and MRI Principles (–2 marks):** CT uses *ionizing X-rays* and measures *attenuation*; MRI uses *non-ionizing RF* and measures *precession signals*. Do not interchange them.
> 5. **Forgetting the Ramp Filter in FBP (–1 mark):** The FBP formula *must* include the $\vert\omega\vert$ filter term; otherwise it is just simple back-projection (which produces a blurred image).
> 6. **Gyromagnetic Ratio Value (–1 mark):** Use $\gamma = 42.58$ MHz/T for hydrogen, not 42.58 Hz/T (a common unit error).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Sectional imaging** = 2D cross-sectional reconstruction from multi-angle 1D projections.
- ✅ The **Radon Transform** $p(\rho, \theta) = \int_L f(x, y)\, ds$ is the forward model; the **Inverse Radon Transform** is the reconstruction.
- ✅ The **Fourier Slice Theorem** links projection-space (sinogram) to image-space via 1D FT of each projection = radial slice of 2D FT.
- ✅ **Filtered Back Projection (FBP)** = filter each 1D projection with ramp $\vert\omega\vert$, back-project, and sum.
- ✅ **Lambert-Beer Law** in differential form: $I = I_0 \exp\left[-\int_L \mu\, ds\right]$.
- ✅ **Hounsfield Unit:** $HU = 1000 \cdot (\mu - \mu_{water})/\mu_{water}$; water = 0 HU, air = −1000 HU, dense bone ≈ +1000 HU.
- ✅ **Larmor frequency** for hydrogen: $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ with $\gamma = 42.58$ MHz/T → 63.87 MHz at 1.5 T.
- ✅ **T₁ relaxation** = spin–lattice energy exchange → recovery of $M_z$ (longitudinal).
- ✅ **T₂ relaxation** = spin–spin dephasing → decay of $M_{xy}$ (transverse); $T_2 \le T_1$ always.
- ✅ CT uses **ionizing X-rays**; MRI uses **non-ionizing RF**; this is a fundamental KTU distinction.
- ✅ **MRI spatial encoding** uses frequency-encoding gradient $G_x$ (during read-out) and phase-encoding gradient $G_y$ (between repetitions).
- ✅ Modern CT scanners use **helical (spiral) acquisition** with slip-ring technology and interpolate projections onto planar slices before FBP.
- ✅ Modern MRI uses **parallel imaging (SENSE, GRAPPA)** and **compressed sensing** to accelerate k-space acquisition.
- ✅ The **k-space** in MRI is the direct analog of the 2D Fourier space in CT's Fourier Slice Theorem.
- ✅ Image quality metrics: **spatial resolution** (mm), **contrast resolution** (%), **SNR**, **CNR**, and for CT also **CTDI** (dose).
- ✅ **Sinogram** = 2D storage of all 1D projections for a slice; rows = detector channels, columns = projection angle $\theta$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generations of CT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBMT723 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Generations of CT # 📘 Generations of CT — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **CT (Computed Tomography) Generations** refer to the chronological evolution of CT scanner architecture, classified by the geometry of the X-ray beam, the motion pattern of the X-ray source and detectors, and the resulting improvements in acquisition speed, image quality, and clinical throughput. The classification spans from the **1st Generation (Hounsfield, 1971)** to the **7th Generation (Multi-Slice / Volumetric CT)**. Each generation represents a distinct engineering solution to the fundamental problem of acquiring a complete 2D projection dataset (Radon space) of a patient within a clinically acceptable scan time while minimizing **motion blur**, **patient dose**, and **artifacts**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Flashlight in a Dark Room" Analogy Imagine you are in a completely dark room with a sculpture inside, holding only a narrow flashlight: - **1st Generation — One Ray at a Time:** You can only see one pencil-thin beam of light. To map the entire sculpture, you must sweep the beam across it, rotate a tiny bit, sweep again, rotate again... (very slow but very precise). - **2nd Generation — A Narrow Flashlight:** You upgrade to a slightly wider beam (a small fan of light), needing fewer sweeps and rotations to complete the picture. - **3rd Generation — A Wide Stage Light:** You install a wide fan-beam light on a rotating arm. The light and a curved mirror (detector) rotate *together* around the sculpture in one continuous spin — no sweeping required. - **4th Generation — A Ring of Mirrors:** You place a full ring of light sensors (detectors) all around the sculpture. Only the flashlight now rotates — the detectors are stationary. - **5th Generation — No Moving Parts:** Instead of a flashlight, you use a beam of electrons steered magnetically to strike a stationary tungsten target surrounding the patient. The "light" is created electronically. - **6th Generation — A Moving Conveyor Belt:** The flashlight keeps spinning continuously while the sculpture (patient bed) slides smoothly through the ring — creating a *spiral/helical* data set. - **7th Generation — A 3D Scanner:** Instead of a single flat slice, you capture *many parallel slices* simultaneously using multiple detector rows, reconstructing full **volumetric (3D)** images of entire organs in a single breath-hold. > [!NOTE] > **Key Engineering Driver:** Every generational step trades off **scan time, mechanical complexity, cost, and dose** to approach the ideal: *fastest possible acquisition of the cleanest possible volumetric dataset with the lowest patient dose.* ## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in CT Generation Classification | Metric | Typical Values Across Generations | |---|---| | **Beam Geometry** | Pencil → Narrow Fan → Wide Fan → Cone | | **Acquisition Time / Slice** | **~4–5 min** → **~20–30 s** → **~1–5 s** → **~50 ms** | | **Detector Count** | **1** → **~30** → **~600–1000** → **~4800** (ring) | | **Mechanical Motion** | Translate–Rotate → Rotate–Rotate → Stationary Ring → None (electronic) | | **Detector Shape** | Single Point → Linear Array → Curved Array → Full Ring → Multi-Row Array | # 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 First Generation (Translate–Rotate, Pencil Beam) - **Beam Geometry:** Highly collimated **pencil beam** (parallel rays). - **Motion:** The X-ray tube and a *single* detector perform a **linear translation** across the patient, followed by a small angular **rotation** (typically **1°**). This pair of motions is repeated **180 times** to cover 180°. - **Number of Projections:** Approximately **180 parallel projections** (each at a unique angle). - **Data Acquired:** A single 1D line of attenuation data per translation. The full 2D Radon transform of the slice is built up after 180 translations and 180 rotations. - **Scan Time:** ~**4–5 minutes per slice** (clinically impractical for modern use; the original EMI Mark I scanner, 1971). - **Advantage:** Excellent geometric accuracy; minimal scatter due to narrow beam. - **Disadvantage:** Extremely slow → highly sensitive to **patient motion artifacts**. **Total scan time equation:** $$ T_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{trans}} \cdot T_{\text{trans}} + N_{\text{rot}} \cdot T_{\text{rot}} $$ where $N_{\text{trans}} = N_{\text{rot}} = 180$, $T_{\text{trans}}$ = time for one linear pass, $T_{\text{rot}}$ = time for one rotation step. ## 2.2 Second Generation (Translate–Rotate, Narrow Fan Beam) - **Beam Geometry:** A **narrow fan beam** of angular width $\beta \approx 5^\circ - 10^\circ$. - **Motion:** Same translate–rotate pattern, but the detector is now a **linear array of $\sim$ 30 detectors**. - **Angular Increment:** Larger rotation step, typically $\sim 5^\circ$–$6^\circ$, because each fan covers more angles simultaneously. - **Total Rotations:** Reduced from 180 to $\sim 30$–$36$. - **Scan Time:** ~**20–30 seconds per slice** (~10× faster than 1st Gen). - **Trade-off:** Wider beam increases **scattered radiation** reaching the detectors, slightly degrading contrast. **Rotation count relation:** $$ N_{\text{rot}} = \left\lceil \frac{180^\circ}{\Delta\phi} \right\rceil $$ ## 2.3 Third Generation (Rotate–Rotate, Wide Fan Beam) ⭐ Most Clinically Iconic - **Beam Geometry:** **Wide fan beam** of angular width $\beta \approx 30^\circ - 60^\circ$ covering the entire patient cross-section. - **Motion:** **Pure rotation** of X-ray source and a *curved* detector array. **No translation required.** - **Detector Array:** Curved (focused) array of $\sim 600 - 1200$ detectors, all rotating with the tube. - **Scan Time:** **~1–5 seconds per slice** (breath-hold compatible). - **Sampling Condition** (critical for image fidelity): $$ N_{\text{views}} \cdot N_{\text{detectors}} \geq \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot N_{\text{res}} $$ where $N_{\text{res}}$ = number of resolution elements across the FOV. Equivalently, the angular sampling per view must satisfy the Nyquist criterion for the fan-beam geometry. - **Artifactual Concern:** Susceptible to **ring artifacts** if any single detector drifts or malfunctions, because each detector is responsible for a specific annular region in the reconstructed image. ## 2.4 Fourth Generation (Rotate–Stationary) - **Beam Geometry:** Wide fan beam, same as 3rd Gen. - **Motion:** Only the **X-ray tube rotates**; detectors form a **complete stationary ring** of $\sim 1200 - 4800$ detectors surrounding the patient. - **Scan Time:** **~1–5 seconds per slice** (similar to 3rd Gen). - **Detector Type:** Fixed solid-state detectors (e.g., CdWO₄ or Gd₂O₂S scintillators with photodiodes) covering a full 360° ring. - **Advantage over 3rd Gen:** No **ring artifacts** (each view uses a different detector) → more robust image quality. - **Disadvantage:** Very expensive due to thousands of detectors; **scatter** is higher because wider solid angle is collected. ## 2.5 Fifth Generation (Electron Beam CT — EBCT) - **Beam Geometry:** Electron beam strikes one of **4 stationary tungsten target rings** (210° arcs) surrounding the patient. - **Motion:** **No mechanical motion at all.** The electron beam is **electromagnetically steered** to sequentially strike different target rings, generating X-rays at different locations. - **Scan Time:** **~50–100 ms per slice** — fast enough to image the **beating heart** without gating. - **Detector Array:** Two stationary detector rings above the targets. - **Clinical Niche:** Cardiac CT, coronary calcium scoring. - **Current Status:** Largely discontinued due to high cost; superseded by fast multi-slice scanners. ## 2.6 Sixth Generation (Helical / Spiral CT) - **Key Innovation:** **Slip-ring technology** — the X-ray tube can rotate *continuously* without the need to unwind cables between rotations. - **Motion:** Tube rotates **continuously** (1–2 rotations per second) while the **patient table translates** at a constant speed through the gantry. - **Acquisition Path:** The X-ray source traces a **helical (spiral) trajectory** around the patient, not a series of discrete circular slices. - **Data Handling:** Requires **interpolation algorithms** (e.g., 360° LI, 180° LI) to convert helical data into equivalent planar slices for reconstruction. - **Key Parameter — Pitch Factor:** $$ \text{Pitch} = \frac{\text{Table travel per rotation}}{\text{Beam collimation (slice width)}} $$ * Pitch = 1 → contiguous slices * Pitch < 1 → overlapping slices (better 3D reconstruction, higher dose) * Pitch > 1 → gaps in coverage (faster, lower dose, but may miss small lesions) - **Scan Time:** **Entire thorax / abdomen in < 30 seconds**; whole-body CT in < 60 seconds. ## 2.7 Seventh Generation (Multi-Slice / Multi-Detector CT — MDCT) - **Beam Geometry:** **Cone beam** (2D divergent beam in both x and z). - **Detectors:** **Multiple parallel detector rows** (4, 8, 16, 64, 128, 256, 320 rows) along the z-axis. - **Acquisition:** Acquires **multiple slices per tube rotation** → true **volumetric (3D) imaging**. - **Scan Time:** Whole-organ (liver, heart, brain) coverage in **single breath-hold (5–10 s)**. - **Reconstruction:** Requires **cone-beam reconstruction algorithms** (e.g., FDK — Feldkamp–Davis–Kress) to handle the 2D divergent geometry. - **Clinical Impact:** Enables **CT angiography**, **perfusion imaging**, **virtual colonoscopy**, **4D cardiac imaging**. ## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 1st Gen total time | $T = 180(T_t + T_r)$ | seconds; $T_t$, $T_r$ = translate / rotate step times | | 2 | Number of rotations (Gen 2) | $N_{\text{rot}} = \lceil 180/\Delta\phi \rceil$ | dimensionless | | 3 | Fan-beam sampling criterion | $N_v \cdot N_d \geq \frac{\pi}{2} N_{\text{res}}$ | dimensionless; Nyquist-like | | 4 | Pitch factor (Helical CT) | $P = \dfrac{d_{\text{table}}}{N \cdot h}$ | $h$ = slice collimation, $N$ = rows active | | 5 | Cone-beam reconstruction | FDK algorithm (Feldkamp–Davis–Kress) | algorithm name | | 6 | CT dose index | $\text{CTDI}_{100} = \dfrac{1}{N \cdot T} \int_{-50}^{+50} D(z)\, dz$ | mGy | | 7 | Dose-length product | $\text{DLP} = \text{CTDI}_{\text{vol}} \cdot L$ | mGy·cm | | 8 | Effective dose | $E = \sum w_T \cdot H_T$ | mSv; $w_T$ = tissue weighting factor | > [!NOTE] > **CTDI (CT Dose Index)** is the standard metric quantifying patient dose per slice; **DLP** aggregates dose over an entire scan length. These are *not* asked to derive on exams but are common 2–3 mark definitions. ## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cardiac CT (5th–7th Gen):** Sub-second imaging eliminates the need for beta-blockers to slow heart rate. - **Trauma & Stroke Imaging (6th–7th Gen):** Whole-body CT in < 60 s is now the standard of care in Level-1 trauma centers. - **Image-Guided Radiotherapy:** 4D-CT (helical + respiratory gating) directly maps tumor motion for precise dose planning. - **Industrial CT (Non-medical):** Same 3rd/4th Gen architecture used in aerospace, additive manufacturing, and security scanning. # 📐 Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Problems ## 3.1 Derivation — Sampling Requirement for 3rd Generation Fan-Beam CT **Goal:** Show why 3rd Generation CT requires a minimum number of views and detectors per view to satisfy the Nyquist sampling criterion. ### Step 1 — Define the Field of View (FOV) Let $D$ = patient diameter, and $N_{\text{res}}$ = number of resolvable elements (pixels) across the FOV. Then: $$ \Delta x = \frac{D}{N_{\text{res}}} $$ This is the smallest spatial detail we wish to resolve. Text row: This $\Delta x$ is the fundamental resolution cell — anything smaller than this cannot be reconstructed reliably. ### Step 2 — Detector Spacing Requirement For 3rd generation geometry, the curved detector array subtends a fan angle $\beta$. With $N_d$ detectors in the array, the spacing at the patient center is approximately: $$ \Delta d \approx \frac{D}{N_d} $$ Text row: A larger number of detectors packed into the array gives a finer sampling grid at the isocenter. ### Step 3 — Angular Sampling Requirement Per Nyquist, the angular sampling interval $\Delta\phi$ (in radians) must satisfy: $$ \Delta\phi \leq \frac{2}{N_{\text{res}}} $$ Equivalently, the number of views over 180° must be: $$ N_v \geq \frac{\pi N_{\text{res}}}{2} $$ Text row: This is the standard result that the Radon space must be sampled finely enough in the angular direction to avoid aliasing (streak artifacts) in the reconstructed image. ### Step 4 — Combine the Two Constraints Multiplying the two requirements (detector sampling × angular sampling), the **total number of independent samples** must satisfy: $$ N_v \cdot N_d \;\geq\; \left(\frac{\pi N_{\text{res}}}{2}\right) \cdot N_{\text{res}} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{2} N_{\text{res}}^{2} $$ Text row: This is the famous $N_v \cdot N_d \geq \frac{\pi}{2} N_{\text{res}}^{2}$ sampling law. For a 512×512 image ($N_{\text{res}} = 512$): $$ N_v \cdot N_d \;\geq\; \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot (512)^2 \;\approx\; 4.12 \times 10^{5} $$ If we choose $N_v = 1000$ views (a typical clinical value), then $N_d \geq 412$ detectors. Real 3rd Gen scanners use $N_d = 600 - 1200$, comfortably exceeding this. ## 3.2 Worked Problem 1 — Pitch Factor Calculation **Question:** A 6th generation helical CT scanner uses a 16-row detector array. Each row has a collimation of 1.25 mm. The table moves 20 mm per tube rotation. Calculate the **pitch factor** and comment on coverage. ### Solution The total beam collimation (effective slice width along z) is: $$ N \cdot h = 16 \times 1.25 \text{ mm} = 20 \text{ mm} $$ The pitch is: $$ P = \frac{d_{\text{table}}}{N \cdot h} = \frac{20 \text{ mm}}{20 \text{ mm}} = 1.0 $$ **Interpretation:** $P = 1$ means **contiguous (gapless) coverage** of the scanned volume. There is no overlap and no gap. This is the standard clinical choice balancing dose and coverage. If the table moved 30 mm per rotation, then $P = 30/20 = 1.5$ — faster scan, lower dose, but **gaps in z-coverage** may cause small lesions to be missed. ## 3.3 Worked Problem 2 — Scan Time Comparison: 1st vs 3rd Generation **Question:** Calculate the scan time per slice for a 1st Generation CT with $T_t = 0.8$ s, $T_r = 0.5$ s, and 180 projections. Compare to a 3rd Generation CT with a 2 s rotation. ### Solution **1st Generation:** $$ T_{1G} = 180 \times (0.8 + 0.5) = 180 \times 1.3 = 234 \text{ s} \approx 3.9 \text{ min} $$ **3rd Generation:** $$ T_{3G} = 2 \text{ s (single rotation acquires full slice)} $$ **Speed-up factor:** $$ \frac{T_{1G}}{T_{3G}} = \frac{234}{2} \approx 117 \times $$ Text row: This explains why 1st Generation CT was clinically impractical for *thoracic* and *abdominal* imaging — the patient could not hold their breath for nearly 4 minutes per slice. The 3rd Generation's ~2 s acquisition made breath-hold imaging feasible for the first time. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Generation-by-Generation Comparison | Parameter | 1G | 2G | 3G | 4G | 5G (EBCT) | 6G (Helical) | 7G (MDCT) | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Beam** | Pencil | Narrow Fan | Wide Fan | Wide Fan | Fan (electronic) | Fan + moving table | Cone | | **Source motion** | T+R | T+R | R+R | R (only) | None (e-mag) | R (continuous) | R (continuous) | | **Detector motion** | T+R | T+R | R+R | Stationary | Stationary | R (continuous) | R (continuous) | | **Detector count** | 1 | ~30 | 600–1200 | 1200–4800 | 2 rings | 600–1200 | 600–1200 per row × N rows | | **Det shape** | Point | Linear | Curved arc | Full ring | Rings | Curved | 2D multi-row | | **Time / slice** | 4–5 min | 20–30 s | 1–5 s | 1–5 s | 50–100 ms | <1 s effective | <1 s for full volume | | **Ring artifacts** | No | No | **Yes** (major) | No | No | No | No | | **Cardiac capable** | No | No | Limited | Limited | **Yes** | Yes | **Yes** (4D) | | **Year (approx)** | 1971 | 1974 | 1975 | 1976 | 1984 | 1989 | 1998+ | # 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 CT Generation Evolution — Mermaid Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Gen 1: 1971

Wide Fan Beam

[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model] **List the four key engineering parameters used to classify CT scanner generations. State the beam geometry and motion pattern of 3rd Generation CT.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The four classification parameters are: 1. **X-ray beam geometry** (pencil / narrow fan / wide fan / cone) — **(1 mark)** 2. **Motion pattern of source and detectors** (translate–rotate, rotate–rotate, rotate–stationary, none) — **(1 mark)** 3. **Number, shape, and arrangement of detectors** (single, linear, curved, ring, multi-row) — **(0.5 marks)** 4. **Acquisition time per slice / per volume** — **(0.5 marks)** **3rd Generation CT specifics:** Wide fan beam (30°–60°), **rotate–rotate** motion (both tube and curved detector array rotate together around the patient), no translation. Scan time ~1–5 s per slice. — **(Included in explanation, not separately graded)** --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] **Explain the principle of "Slip-ring technology" and state the generation in which it was introduced.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Slip-ring technology replaces the old **cable-based power and data transmission** to the rotating gantry with **continuous sliding electrical contacts** (brushes sliding on concentric metallic rings). — **(1.5 marks)** This allows the X-ray tube to **rotate continuously in one direction** (no need to stop and unwind cables), which in turn enables **helical (spiral) acquisition** where the table translates simultaneously. — **(1 mark)** **Introduced in:** 6th Generation CT scanners (1989 onwards). — **(0.5 marks)** --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the construction and working of **3rd Generation CT**. Compare it with **4th Generation CT** in terms of detector arrangement, motion, artifacts, and scan time. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain **Helical (Spiral) CT** acquisition. Define the **pitch factor** and discuss the clinical implications of using **pitch = 0.5, 1.0, and 1.5**. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution **Part (a) — 3rd Gen vs 4th Gen CT (7 marks):** **3rd Generation CT Construction & Working:** ``` [Block diagram description for answer script] X-ray tube + curved detector array → mounted on opposite ends of a common rotatable C-arm ↓ Both rotate together (rotate-rotate) around the patient ↓ Wide fan beam (~30°–60°) covers entire patient cross-section ↓ ~600–1200 detectors in the curved array sample the fan simultaneously ↓ Data is digitized (16-bit ADC), log-amplified ↓ Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) reconstructs the cross-sectional image ↓ Displayed on console / archived in DICOM format ``` **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Feature | 3rd Gen | 4th Gen | |---|---|---| | Detector arrangement | Curved array, rotates with tube | **Full 360° stationary ring** | | Motion | Rotate–rotate | **Rotate–stationary** (only tube rotates) | | Number of detectors | 600–1200 | **1200–4800** | | Scan time | 1–5 s | 1–5 s | | Ring artifacts | **Yes — critical problem** | **Eliminated** | | Scatter | Lower | **Higher** (wider solid angle) | | Cost | Moderate | **Higher** | **Part (b) — Helical CT & Pitch Factor (7 marks):** Helical CT uses **slip-ring technology** to allow the X-ray tube to **rotate continuously** (typically 0.3–1.0 s per rotation) while the **patient table translates at a constant velocity** through the gantry. The X-ray source thus traces a **helical path** around the patient. — **(2 marks)** Z-interpolation (360° LI or 180° LI) is applied to the raw helical data to synthesize planar slices equivalent to conventional step-and-shoot acquisitions. — **(1 mark)** **Pitch Factor Definition:** $$ P = \frac{\text{Table travel per tube rotation (mm)}}{\text{Total beam collimation (mm)}} $$ **Clinical implications (3 marks):** - **P = 0.5:** Heavy overlap; **higher patient dose**, but excellent 3D / multi-planar reconstruction quality. Used for small lesion detection. - **P = 1.0:** Contiguous coverage; **standard clinical choice**; balance between dose and image quality. - **P = 1.5:** Gaps in z-coverage; **lower dose, faster scan**; risk of missing small lesions; used in screening or pediatric CT to minimize dose. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]** **(a)** Describe the **1st Generation and 2nd Generation CT scanners** in detail. Explain why 2nd Generation is faster than 1st Generation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is **Electron Beam CT (EBCT)**? Explain its principle, construction, and the clinical application that justifies its design. State why it is largely obsolete today. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution **Part (a) — 1st Gen and 2nd Gen CT (7 marks):** **1st Generation CT (Hounsfield, EMI Mark I, 1971):** - **Pencil beam** (highly collimated) + **single detector** — **(1 mark)** - Motion: **Translate–rotate** sequence. 180 linear translations × 180 rotations of 1° each. — **(1 mark)** - Each translation gives one parallel projection; 180 projections cover 180° (sufficient due to parallel-beam symmetry). — **(0.5 marks)** - Scan time: **4–5 minutes per slice** — clinically impractical. — **(0.5 marks)** **2nd Generation CT:** - **Narrow fan beam** (~5°–10°) + **linear detector array of ~30 elements** — **(1 mark)** - Same translate–rotate motion, but **larger angular step (~5°–6°)** because each fan covers multiple angles at once. — **(1 mark)** - Total rotations reduced from 180 to **~30–36**. — **(0.5 marks)** - Scan time: **20–30 seconds per slice** (~10× faster). — **(0.5 marks)** **Why 2nd Gen is faster:** The narrow fan beam with a linear detector array collects data equivalent to **multiple parallel-beam translations in a single pass**, drastically reducing the number of mechanical motions required to complete the 180° angular sweep. — **(1 mark)** **Part (b) — Electron Beam CT (7 marks):** **Principle:** EBCT replaces the mechanical rotation of an X-ray tube with an **electron beam** that is **electromagnetically focused, steered, and swept** across a set of **stationary tungsten target rings** (typically 4 rings covering 210° arcs) surrounding the patient. — **(2 marks)** **Construction:** - Large vacuum chamber houses the electron gun, focusing coils, steering coils, and tungsten target rings. — **(1 mark)** - Two stationary detector rings are positioned above the targets. — **(0.5 marks)** - No mechanical gantry rotation → scan time per slice is **~50–100 ms**. — **(0.5 marks)** **Clinical application:** **Cardiac imaging.** The 50–100 ms acquisition is faster than a single cardiac cycle, allowing **motion-free imaging of the beating heart** without ECG gating artifacts and without requiring beta-blockers to slow the heart. Particularly used for **coronary artery calcium scoring** and congenital heart disease. — **(2 marks)** **Obsolescence (1 mark):** EBCT is now largely replaced by **7th Generation Multi-Slice CT (MDCT)** with 64–320 detector rows and rotation times of ~0.24 s, which deliver comparable cardiac image quality at lower cost and with greater clinical versatility. --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing 3rd and 4th Gen motion pattern** — many students write "4th Gen detectors rotate with tube" — this is wrong. In 4th Gen, only the **tube rotates**; the detectors form a **stationary ring**. This single error costs **2 marks** in 7-mark sub-parts. > 2. **Forgetting the pitch implications** — defining pitch without discussing **dose, coverage, and lesion-miss risk** at non-unity values loses up to **1.5 marks**. > 3. **Conflating "Slip-ring" with "Helical"** — slip-ring is the *enabling hardware*; helical is the *acquisition geometry*. Examiners expect this distinction. > 4. **Omitting year / inventor / scan time** in long answers — even if not explicitly asked, KTU examiners award partial marks for these standard technical details. > 5. **In Helical CT questions, students often forget that raw data must be interpolated to form planar slices** — this is a tested concept worth 1–2 marks. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - 🔑 **1st Gen** = Pencil beam, T+R, single detector, **4–5 min/slice**. - 🔑 **2nd Gen** = Narrow fan, T+R, ~30 detectors, **20–30 s/slice**, ~10× faster than 1G. - 🔑 **3rd Gen** = Wide fan, R+R, curved array, **1–5 s/slice**, prone to **ring artifacts**. - 🔑 **4th Gen** = Wide fan, R (tube) + **stationary detector ring**, no ring artifacts, higher cost. - 🔑 **5th Gen (EBCT)** = Electron beam steered electromagnetically, **no moving parts**, **~50 ms/slice**, cardiac CT. - 🔑 **6th Gen (Helical)** = **Slip-ring** enables continuous rotation + table translation; data is acquired in **helical geometry** and **interpolated** to form slices; **Pitch = table travel / beam collimation**. - 🔑 **7th Gen (MDCT)** = **Cone beam + multi-row detectors** (up to 320 rows); enables **volumetric 3D imaging** in a single breath-hold; uses **FDK cone-beam reconstruction**. - 🔑 **Sampling criterion:** $N_v \cdot N_d \geq \frac{\pi}{2} N_{\text{res}}^{2}$. - 🔑 **Pitch = 1** is the standard clinical compromise; **P < 1** = better 3D quality, higher dose; **P > 1** = faster, lower dose, possible miss of small lesions. - 🔑 **Hounsfield** invented CT (1971, EMI Mark I, 1st Generation) and received the **Nobel Prize in Medicine (1979)**. - 🔑 Standard metrics to memorize: **CTDI (mGy)**, **DLP (mGy·cm)**, **Effective dose E (mSv)**, **Pitch (dimensionless)**.

topics.title: Spiral and Multi-Slice CT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT723 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Spiral and Multi-Slice CT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Spiral CT (Helical CT) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spiral CT (Helical CT)** is a third-generation Computed Tomography acquisition technique in which the X-ray tube continuously rotates around the patient while the patient table translates smoothly and continuously through the gantry at a constant velocity. The combined motion of continuous gantry rotation and continuous table translation traces a **helical (spiral) trajectory** of the X-ray focal spot relative to the patient, replacing the discrete "step-and-shoot" motion of conventional (sequential) CT.

The enabling hardware component is the **slip-ring technology**, which transfers electrical power and high-frequency control/data signals to the rotating gantry through stationary graphite brushes and concentric metallic rings. This eliminates the need to unwind cables between rotations, permitting **continuous rotation in a single direction**.

**Key operational metric — Pitch:**
$$P = \frac{\text{Table travel per gantry rotation }(d)}{\text{Total X-ray beam collimation }(N \cdot T)}$$

where $N$ is the number of active detector rows and $T$ is the nominal slice thickness per row.

## 1.2 Multi-Slice CT (MSCT / MDCT) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multi-Slice CT (MSCT)**, also called **Multi-Detector CT (MDCT)**, is a CT architecture in which multiple parallel detector rows (typically **4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256, or 320 rows**) are placed along the patient long axis (z-axis). The X-ray beam is widened into a **cone beam** (rather than a thin fan beam) so that several adjacent slices are acquired simultaneously during a single gantry rotation, dramatically improving z-axis coverage and reducing scan time.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — The Rotating Helix (Spiral CT):**
Imagine a **rotating ceiling fan** whose blades carry a small torch (the X-ray source). If a person walks steadily underneath the fan while it rotates, the torch beam does not sweep a flat circle but rather a **helix wrapping around the person**. Data is therefore collected continuously along the entire length of the body in one breath-hold, instead of stopping, scanning a thin disc, stopping, advancing, scanning again (like peeling a banana one coin-shaped slice at a time).

**Analogy 2 — The Bread Slicer (Multi-Slice CT):**
Conventional CT is like a kitchen knife slicing **one bread slice at a time**. Multi-Slice CT is like a **slicing machine with 64, 128 or 320 parallel blades** that descends through the loaf — you get the entire loaf neatly sectioned in a single downward pass. Each "blade" corresponds to a detector row.

**Analogy 3 — Helicopter Searchlights (Cone Beam):**
A conventional CT fan beam is like a **wall of light** that is thin in height. A multi-slice cone beam is like a **flashlight pointed downward** — a wide cone of light illuminating multiple floors of a building at once, with each detector row "seeing" a different floor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters clinically:** Spiral + multi-slice acquisition is the **foundation of modern CT angiography, cardiac CT, trauma whole-body scanning, and CT perfusion imaging** because it removes the slice-to-slice misregistration (breathing, peristalsis) of step-and-shoot CT and allows isotropic voxels (~0.5 mm in all three axes).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Parameter | Conventional CT | Single-Slice Spiral | Multi-Slice Spiral (4–320 row) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rotation time | 1–2 s | 0.75–1 s | **0.27–0.35 s** |
| Table motion | Step-and-shoot | Continuous | Continuous |
| Detectors per rotation | 1 row | 1 row | **4 to 320 rows** |
| Beam shape | Fan | Fan | **Cone** |
| z-coverage/rotation | 1 × T | 1 × T | **N × T** |
| Typical helical pitch | N/A | 1.0–2.0 | 0.5–1.5 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Slip-Ring Technology — The Heart of Spiral CT

In conventional (third-generation) CT, the high-voltage cable, control signals, and data lines were physically attached to the rotating gantry. The gantry could only rotate ~210° in one direction, then had to "unwind" before rotating back. This **cable wrap** problem forced the **stop-scan-step** acquisition sequence.

**Slip-ring design** (introduced commercially in 1989, Toshiba / Siemens):

1. **Stationary part (stator):** Concentric circular metallic tracks (rings) are mounted on the **stationary** frame of the gantry. Two rings carry the **high-voltage AC** (or DC) to the X-ray tube; a smaller set of rings carries **low-voltage control** and **data** signals.
2. **Rotating part (rotor/brushes):** Spring-loaded **graphite/silver-alloy brushes** ride on the rings, sliding (slipping) over them while the rotor spins.
3. **Continuous rotation:** Because power is delivered through the rings instead of a cable wrap, the gantry can rotate **continuously in one direction**, typically 2–4 revolutions per second.
4. **High-frequency inverter:** Modern slip rings use a **high-frequency (~50 kHz) low-voltage inverter** to step up the voltage on the rotor, eliminating the heavy high-voltage cable entirely (especially in the low-voltage slip-ring design).

> [!NOTE]
> There are two slip-ring electrical designs:
> - **High-voltage slip ring (HVSR):** ~120–150 kV transmitted directly across the brushes. Prone to brush arcing.
> - **Low-voltage slip ring (LVSR):** Mains AC rectified to DC, then chopped to high frequency, transmitted across the brushes, then re-inverted to high voltage on the rotor side. **More reliable**, used in virtually all modern scanners.

## 2.2 Pitch — The Most Important Parameter in Spiral/Multi-Slice CT

### 2.2.1 Beam Pitch ($P_b$)

$$P_b = \frac{\text{Table feed per rotation } (d)}{\text{Total beam width } (W)}$$

For a single-slice scanner, $W = T$ (one collimated slice), so $P_b = d/T$.

For a multi-slice scanner with $N$ detector rows each of nominal thickness $T$:
$$W = N \cdot T$$

Hence:
$$P_b = \frac{d}{N \cdot T}$$

### 2.2.2 Detector Pitch (sometimes used by Siemens)

$$P_d = \frac{d}{T} = N \cdot P_b$$

Note: $P_d = N \cdot P_b$. The relationship is a simple scaling.

### 2.2.3 Physical Meaning of Pitch

- $P = 1.0$ → table travels exactly one beam-width per rotation. **"Just-touching" helix** — every point on the patient is irradiated once. Standard reference.
- $P < 1.0$ → **overlapping** helix; data oversampled along z; better longitudinal resolution, higher patient dose.
- $P > 1.0$ → **gaps** in the helix along z; lower dose, but possible under-sampling artefacts and lower SNR.
- $P > 1.5$ → typically only used for very fast screening (e.g., lung nodule search) due to image quality loss.

## 2.3 Multi-Slice CT Detector Geometry — Cone Beam

A fundamental change in MSCT is that the X-ray beam is no longer a "fan" confined to the scan plane. It opens in the z-direction as a **cone (or pyramid)**, with the half-angle:

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{W/2}{\text{Focus-to-iso distance (FOD)}}$$

For a 320-row scanner covering 16 cm in one rotation, $\alpha$ can exceed **7°**, which causes **cone-beam artefacts** if the reconstruction algorithm ignores the z-divergence. This is why MSCT reconstruction uses **3D back-projection / cone-beam reconstruction** (Feldkamp–Davis–Kress algorithm) rather than simple 2D fan-beam filtered back-projection.

## 2.4 Adaptive Axial Interpolation in Helical CT

Because the data in spiral CT is acquired on a helix (not a plane), each reconstructed axial slice must be **interpolated** from the helical projection data that brackets the desired z-position.

**Two classic interpolators:**

| Interpolation | Helical range used | Slice sensitivity profile (SSP) | Typical use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **360° Linear Interpolation (360° LI)** | 2 full rotations | Wider, broader shoulders | High-contrast, low-noise |
| **180° LI (Cardiac / Short-scan)** | 1 rotation + 180° | Narrower, sharper | High-z-resolution, cardiac CT |

The 180° LI exploits the fact that a fan-beam projection at angle $\beta$ is equivalent (with 180° rotation) to a projection at $\beta + 180°$ in the opposite direction, doubling the effective sampling along z.

## 2.5 Multi-Slice Reconstruction — z-Filtering

For MSCT, interpolation is generalised into a **z-axis filter** $h(z)$ applied across multiple detector rows. The effective slice sensitivity profile is the convolution:

$$\text{SSP}_{\text{eff}}(z) = \text{SSP}_{\text{detector}}(z) * h(z)$$

A common choice is a smooth raised-cosine (e.g., a **Shepp-Logan** or **Hamming** z-filter) which trades longitudinal resolution against noise and dose.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Beam pitch** | $P_b = \dfrac{d}{N \cdot T}$ | dimensionless; $d$ = table feed per rotation |
| 2 | **Detector pitch** | $P_d = \dfrac{d}{T} = N \cdot P_b$ | Used by Siemens; equals $N$ times beam pitch |
| 3 | **Table feed** | $d = P_b \cdot N \cdot T$ | mm/rotation |
| 4 | **z-coverage per rotation** | $L_z = N \cdot T$ | mm |
| 5 | **Volume coverage speed** | $v_z = \dfrac{P_b \cdot N \cdot T}{T_{\text{rot}}}$ | mm/s |
| 6 | **Beam cone half-angle** | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{N \cdot T}{2 \cdot \text{FOD}}$ | FOD in mm; $\alpha$ in radians |
| 7 | **Scan length covered in time $t$** | $L = v_z \cdot t = P_b \cdot N \cdot T \cdot \dfrac{t}{T_{\text{rot}}}$ | mm |
| 8 | **Relative dose vs. axial** | $\text{CTDI}_{\text{spiral}} \approx \dfrac{1}{P_b} \cdot \text{CTDI}_{\text{axial}}$ | Pitch < 1 ⇒ dose ↑ |
| 9 | **Multi-slice helical oversampling factor** | $S = P_b \cdot N$ | Effective number of projections per slice |
| 10 | **Number of rotations for a length $L$** | $N_{\text{rot}} = \dfrac{L}{d} = \dfrac{L}{P_b \cdot N \cdot T}$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical memory hook for KTU exams:** $P_b = d/(N \cdot T)$ is the *only* pitch definition officially adopted by IEC and used in KTU textbooks. Many old solutions still use $P = d/T$ (which is *detector* pitch for single-slice and equals *beam* pitch only when $N = 1$). Always state which definition you are using.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cardiac CT angiography (CCTA):** Requires sub-millimetre isotropic voxels with no motion artefact. Achievable only with MSCT + short rotation time + prospective ECG gating + low pitch (~0.2 during one heartbeat).
- **Whole-body trauma CT:** 64-slice scanner covers head-to-toes in ~20 s with sub-millimetre reformats.
- **CT perfusion (stroke imaging):** Repeated rapid spiral scans over a 4 cm slab of brain every 1–2 s to track contrast bolus.
- **Industrial CT (non-destructive testing):** Cone-beam spiral CT scans turbine blades, castings, and composite parts in 3D with micrometre resolution.
