# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Central Slice Theorem. Why is it important in CT image reconstruction?

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Central Slice Theorem** (also called the **Fourier Slice Theorem**) states that the 1D Fourier Transform of a parallel-beam projection $p_\theta(t)$ of an object $f(x,y)$, taken at projection angle $\theta$, equals a 1D radial slice through the 2D Fourier Transform $F(u,v)$ of the object, where the slice passes through the origin at angle $\theta$.
>
> Mathematically: $P_\theta(\omega) = F(\omega\cos\theta, \, \omega\sin\theta)$.
>
> **Importance in CT:**
> 1. It forms the **mathematical foundation** of the Filtered Back Projection (FBP) algorithm.
> 2. It allows the use of efficient **FFT algorithms** for fast reconstruction.
> 3. It reveals the **sampling requirements** in the frequency domain (related to Nyquist criterion).
> 4. It explains why **filtered** projections (with a ramp filter) are needed — to compensate for the non-uniform density of frequency samples in polar coordinates.

---

### Question 2: What is meant by Algebraic Reconstruction Technique (ART)? List any two advantages over FBP.

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]**

**Model Answer:**

> **ART (Algebraic Reconstruction Technique)** is an iterative image reconstruction method that solves the CT imaging problem as a system of linear equations. The image is discretized into a pixel grid, and the projection measurements are written as weighted sums of pixel values. Starting from an initial guess, the pixel values are iteratively updated so that the simulated projections match the measured ones.
>
> **Two advantages over FBP:**
> 1. **Better noise handling:** ART can incorporate statistical noise models and prior knowledge (Bayesian frameworks) to produce smoother images under low-dose conditions.
> 2. **Robust to limited-angle data:** ART can still produce acceptable reconstructions when projection angles are incomplete, whereas FBP produces severe streaking artifacts in such cases.
> 3. **Flexibility with irregular geometry:** It accommodates arbitrary source-detector trajectories (e.g., C-arm, dental CBCT).
> 4. **Incorporates physical constraints:** Non-negativity, known regions (e.g., air outside body) can be enforced as constraints.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Central Slice Theorem** for 2D image reconstruction. State clearly all assumptions made.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Filtered Back Projection (FBP)** algorithm. Mention the role of the ramp filter and discuss how different filter kernels affect image quality.

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Module 3]**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Central Slice Theorem [7 Marks]

**Assumptions:**
1. The object $f(x,y)$ is a real, square-integrable function.
2. Parallel-beam geometry is used.
3. The Radon transform (line integral) is the projection model.

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1: Definition of the Radon transform** [1 mark]

$$p_\theta(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t\cos\theta - s\sin\theta, \, t\sin\theta + s\cos\theta) \, ds$$

This represents the line integral of $f$ along a line perpendicular to the direction $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ at distance $t$ from the origin.

**Step 2: Take the 1D Fourier Transform of the projection** [1 mark]

$$P_\theta(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} p_\theta(t) \, e^{-j 2\pi \omega t} \, dt$$

**Step 3: Substitute the Radon transform and swap integration order** [1 mark]

$$P_\theta(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t\cos\theta - s\sin\theta, \, t\sin\theta + s\cos\theta) \, e^{-j 2\pi \omega t} \, dt \, ds$$

**Step 4: Apply coordinate rotation** [1 mark]

Let $x = t\cos\theta - s\sin\theta$, $y = t\sin\theta + s\cos\theta$.
Then $t = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$, and the Jacobian is $|\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta| = 1$.

**Step 5: Rewrite integral in $(x,y)$ coordinates** [1 mark]

$$P_\theta(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x,y) \, e^{-j 2\pi \omega (x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)} \, dx \, dy$$

**Step 6: Recognize the 2D Fourier transform** [1 mark]

Setting $u = \omega\cos\theta$ and $v = \omega\sin\theta$:

$$P_\theta(\omega) = F(\omega\cos\theta, \omega\sin\theta)$$

**Step 7: Interpretation** [1 mark]

> The 1D Fourier Transform of a projection at angle $\theta$ equals a radial slice of the 2D Fourier Transform of the object at the same angle. Hence, by varying $\theta$ from $0$ to $\pi$, we can fill the entire 2D frequency plane.

---

#### Part (b) — FBP Algorithm and Filter Discussion [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.3):**

Projection → 1D FFT → Multiply by $H(\omega) = |\omega|$ → 1D IFFT → Back-project → Accumulator → Image

**Explanation:**

**Step 1: Pre-processing** [1 mark]
- Acquire $K$ parallel projections $p_{\theta_k}(t)$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, K-1$.
- Apply log-transform to convert intensity measurements to line integrals of attenuation.

**Step 2: 1D Fourier Transform** [1 mark]
- Compute $P_{\theta_k}(\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{p_{\theta_k}(t)\}$ for each projection.

**Step 3: Apply ramp filter** [1 mark]
- Multiply by $H(\omega) = |\omega|$ to obtain the filtered spectrum $Q_{\theta_k}(\omega) = P_{\theta_k}(\omega) \cdot |\omega|$.
- The ramp filter compensates for the $1/|\omega|$ density bias in back-projection (which would otherwise blur the image).

**Step 4: Inverse 1D FFT** [1 mark]
- $Q_{\theta_k}(t) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{Q_{\theta_k}(\omega)\}$ is the **filtered projection**.

**Step 5: Back-projection and accumulation** [1 mark]

$$f(x,y) = \Delta\theta \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} Q_{\theta_k}(x\cos\theta_k + y\sin\theta_k)$$

**Step 6: Effect of different filter kernels** [1 mark]

| Filter | Frequency Behavior | Image Effect | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ram-Lak** | No smoothing | Sharp, noisy | High-contrast bone |
| **Shepp-Logan** | Mild smoothing at $\omega_c$ | Balanced | General purpose |
| **Cosine** | Moderate smoothing | Smooth | Soft tissue |
| **Hamming / Hann** | Strong smoothing | Very smooth, low noise | Low-dose pediatric |

**Step 7: Conclusion** [1 mark]
> Filtered Back Projection is the **clinical gold standard** for CT reconstruction due to its speed and accuracy. The choice of filter balances spatial resolution against image noise, governed by the **noise-resolution trade-off**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Algebraic Reconstruction Technique (ART)** in detail. Derive the update equation and discuss convergence properties.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Iterative** and **Fourier** reconstruction methods with respect to speed, noise handling, dose reduction, and artifact behavior.

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 3]**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — ART Algorithm [7 Marks]

**Step 1: System of Linear Equations** [1 mark]

The image is discretized into an $N \times N$ grid of pixels with values $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{N^2}$. Each projection measurement gives:

$$p_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N^2} w_{ij} x_j \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, M$$

In matrix form: $\mathbf{W} \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{p}$.

**Step 2: Geometric interpretation** [1 mark]

Each equation defines a **hyperplane** in $N^2$-dimensional space. The solution $\mathbf{x}^*$ is the intersection of all hyperplanes (in the noise-free case).

**Step 3: Kaczmarz update rule** [1 mark]

Starting from an initial guess $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$, iteratively project onto each hyperplane:

$$x_j^{(k+1)} = x_j^{(k)} + \lambda \frac{p_i - \mathbf{w}_i^T \mathbf{x}^{(k)}}{\mathbf{w}_i^T \mathbf{w}_i} w_{ij}$$

where $\mathbf{w}_i$ is the $i$-th row of $\mathbf{W}$, $i = (k \mod M) + 1$, and $\lambda \in (0,1)$ is the relaxation parameter.

**Step 4: Algorithm pseudocode** [1 mark]

```
Initialize: x = 0
For iteration = 1 to K_max:
    For each ray i = 1 to M:
        residual = p_i - sum(w_ij * x_j)
        x = x + lambda * (residual / norm(w_i)^2) * w_i
    Check convergence: ||p - Wx|| < epsilon
```

**Step 5: Convergence properties** [1 mark]

- For a **consistent** system (no noise, exact data), ART **converges** to the unique solution in a finite number of steps (if $\mathbf{W}$ has full rank).
- For **noisy** data, ART **does not converge** but oscillates around the least-squares solution. Adding relaxation $\lambda < 1$ damps oscillations.
- SIRT (using all rays at once) provides smoother convergence at the cost of speed.

**Step 6: Variants** [1 mark]

| Variant | Update Strategy | Property |
|---|---|---|
| **ART** | One ray at a time | Fast, noisy |
| **SIRT** | All rays simultaneously | Smooth, slow |
| **SART** | One projection angle at a time | Compromise |
| **MART** | Multiplicative update | For transmission tomography |
| **OSEM** | Ordered subsets, expectation maximization | For PET/SPECT |

**Step 7: Practical considerations** [1 mark]

- **System matrix $\mathbf{W}$:** Often has $10^9$ elements; sparse storage required.
- **Pre-computation:** Weight factors $w_{ij}$ (intersection lengths) can be pre-computed using Siddon's algorithm.
- **Acceleration:** Use of multi-core GPUs, ordered subsets, and early termination.

---

#### Part (b) — Comparison Table [7 Marks]

| Parameter | Iterative (ART/SIRT) | Fourier (FBP) | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mathematical basis** | Linear algebra; system of equations | Central Slice Theorem; frequency domain | [1] |
| **Computational speed** | Slow (minutes to hours); requires multiple iterations | Very fast (real-time); one FFT + back-project | [1] |
| **Noise handling** | Excellent — can model Poisson noise, prior knowledge | Moderate — noise amplifies at high frequencies | [1] |
| **Limited-angle data** | Robust — can produce acceptable images | Severe streaking artifacts | [1] |
| **Dose reduction capability** | High (30–70% dose reduction possible in modern IR) | Limited without post-processing filters | [1] |
| **Image resolution** | Comparable to FBP | High; depends on filter choice | [0.5] |
| **Implementation complexity** | High (system matrix storage, iteration control) | Low (well-established FFT routines) | [0.5] |
| **Clinical adoption** | Growing (Siemens SAFIRE, GE ASiR, Canon AIDR3D) | Universal (clinical gold standard) | [0.5] |
| **Use in modalities** | PET/SPECT, low-dose CT, CBCT | Diagnostic CT, real-time imaging | [0.5] |

**Conclusion:** [1 mark]
> Fourier methods (FBP) remain the **clinical workhorse** for routine diagnostic CT due to their speed. Iterative methods are increasingly adopted for **dose-reduction protocols** and in modalities (PET/SPECT) where noise statistics are critical. Modern scanners often use a **hybrid approach** — FBP with iterative post-processing.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in this Topic — Where You Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Jacobian in polar-to-Cartesian conversion (1–2 marks):** When deriving the FBP formula from inverse 2D FFT, the change of variables introduces the $|\omega| \, d\omega \, d\theta$ factor. Omitting this gives a wrong reconstruction formula.
>
> 2. **Confusing "central slice" with "central line" (1 mark):** A common error is saying "the projection equals a line in the image" — it should be "a **slice** in **Fourier space**."
>
> 3. **Not mentioning the ramp filter's purpose (1 mark):** Many students compute $Q_\theta(t)$ but forget to explain that the $|\omega|$ factor compensates for the $1/r$ sampling density of back-projection. Without it, the image is blurred.
>
> 4. **Mixing up ART and SIRT updates (1 mark):** ART processes one ray at a time; SIRT processes all rays simultaneously. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 5. **Drawing block diagrams without labels (1–2 marks):** A block diagram of FBP **must** show: 1D-FFT → Filter → 1D-IFFT → Back-project → Accumulator. Unlabeled arrows lose marks.
>
> 6. **Skipping the relaxation parameter $\lambda$ in ART (1 mark):** Forgetting $\lambda$ in the update equation is a frequent error that loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] **Radon transform** = line integral of object along a ray at angle $\theta$.
- [ ] **Central Slice Theorem** = 1D FT of projection = radial slice of 2D FT of object.
- [ ] **FBP** = Filter each projection (with $|\omega|$) then back-project and accumulate.
- [ ] **ART** = Iteratively project current estimate onto each ray's hyperplane (Kaczmarz method).
- [ ] **SIRT** = Simultaneous update using all rays at once — smoother but slower.

### Essential Formulas
- [ ] $p_\theta(t) = \int f(t\cos\theta - s\sin\theta, t\sin\theta + s\cos\theta) \, ds$
- [ ] $P_\theta(\omega) = F(\omega\cos\theta, \omega\sin\theta)$
- [ ] $f(x,y) = \int_0^\pi \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} P_\theta(\omega) \cdot |\omega| \cdot e^{j2\pi\omega t} \, d\omega \, d\theta$
- [ ] $x_j^{(k+1)} = x_j^{(k)} + \lambda \frac{(p_i - \mathbf{w}_i^T \mathbf{x}^{(k)})}{\mathbf{w}_i^T \mathbf{w}_i} w_{ij}$
- [ ] $H_{\text{ramp}}(\omega) = |\omega|$, $h(0) = \frac{1}{4(\Delta t)^2}$, $h[n] = -\frac{1}{(\pi n \Delta t)^2}$ for odd $n$

### Filter Personality
- [ ] **Ram-Lak:** Sharp, noisy (high resolution)
- [ ] **Shepp-Logan:** Balanced
- [ ] **Cosine / Hamming / Hann:** Smooth, low noise (low dose)

### Trade-off Matrix
- [ ] **Iterative** = better dose, better noise model, **slower**
- [ ] **Fourier** = real-time, **noise sensitive**, dose limited

### Common KTU-Asked Points
- [ ] Why is the ramp filter needed? (Compensates $1/|\omega|$ back-projection bias)
- [ ] Differentiate ART vs. SIRT vs. SART.
- [ ] State and prove the Central Slice Theorem.
- [ ] Draw and explain the FBP block diagram.
- [ ] Compare iterative vs. Fourier methods in a table.

### Clinical Context
- [ ] Modern CT scanners use **iterative reconstruction (IR)** for dose reduction.
- [ ] **FBP** is still the baseline; **hybrid IR** (FBP + post-processing) is standard.
- [ ] **PET/SPECT** use statistical iterative methods (OSEM, MLEM).

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always start long-answer questions with a **definition**, then a **diagram/derivation**, and end with a **clinical application**. This structure is what KTU examiners reward.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Nuclear Imaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT723 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Concept of Nuclear Imaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nuclear Imaging** is a branch of medical imaging that uses **radioactive tracers (radiopharmaceuticals)** administered to the patient to visualize and quantify physiological, metabolic, and molecular processes within the body. Unlike X-ray or CT imaging (which are *anatomical*), nuclear imaging is fundamentally a **functional imaging modality** because it maps biochemical activity rather than physical structure.

The imaging principle relies on **external detection of gamma rays or annihilation photons** emitted from an internally administered radiotracer. The tracer is typically a **biologically active molecule** (e.g., glucose, antibodies, peptides) tagged with a **radionuclide** that decays by emitting ionizing radiation detectable by specialized cameras.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Glowing Breadcrumbs" Analogy:**
> Imagine you scatter tiny, glowing breadcrumbs across a forest at night. Each breadcrumb represents a **radiotracer molecule** that "lights up" wherever it goes. If you scatter normal breadcrumbs, you only know the *path* they took (this is like anatomical imaging — seeing where things *are*). But if the breadcrumbs are *radioactive* and you walk through the forest with a **special flashlight (gamma camera)**, you can detect *exactly which parts of the forest the breadcrumbs are currently congregating in*. If a swarm of breadcrumbs clusters near a particular tree, you know the *activity* is concentrated there — perhaps because the tree has sweet fruit (in the body, this would be a tumor or organ with high metabolic activity).

In medical terms:
- **Breadcrumbs** = Radiopharmaceuticals (e.g., ¹⁸F-FDG, ⁹⁹ᵐTc-MDP)
- **Special flashlight** = Gamma camera / PET detector
- **Tree with sweet fruit** = Region of high metabolic/tracer uptake (tumor, inflammation, organ of interest)

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Foundational Vocabulary for KTU Examinations:**

| Term | Definition | Standard Unit |
|------|------------|----------------|
| **Radionuclide** | An unstable atomic nucleus that decays by emitting radiation | Becquerel (Bq) or Curie (Ci) |
| **Radiopharmaceutical** | A radioactive drug (radionuclide + pharmaceutical vector) used for diagnosis or therapy | Administered in MBq (Megabecquerels) |
| **Activity (A)** | The rate of radioactive decay — number of disintegrations per second | Becquerel (Bq): 1 Bq = 1 decay/sec |
| **Half-life (T₁/₂)** | Time required for the activity of a radionuclide to reduce to half its initial value | Seconds, minutes, hours, or days |
| **Specific Activity** | Activity per unit mass of the radionuclide | Bq/g or Ci/g |
| **Mean Life (τ)** | Average lifetime of a radioactive atom before decay | τ = T₁/₂ / ln(2) ≈ 1.44 × T₁/₂ |
| **Spatial Resolution** | Minimum distance between two distinguishable points in the image | Millimeters (mm) |
| **Sensitivity** | Ability to detect true tracer uptake (true positive rate) | Percentage (%) |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Physical Constants in Nuclear Imaging:**
> - **Avogadro's number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ atoms/mol
> - **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
> - **Mass-energy equivalence:** $E = mc^2$ (used to compute photon energy from positron-electron annihilation)
> - **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
> - **Electron rest mass energy:** $E_e = 511$ keV (this is the characteristic energy of annihilation photons used in PET)

### 1.5 Why Nuclear Imaging Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical Significance for KTU Context:**
> Nuclear imaging is the *only* non-invasive modality that can detect disease at the **cellular and molecular level** — often *before* anatomical changes become visible on CT or MRI. For example:
> - Cancerous tumors show increased glucose metabolism *months* before they grow large enough to be seen on CT.
> - Reduced blood flow to the heart muscle can be detected *before* a heart attack occurs.
> - Early Alzheimer's disease shows characteristic patterns of glucose hypometabolism on PET.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Physics of Radioactive Decay

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** A radionuclide is an atom with an unstable neutron-to-proton ratio. To achieve stability, it emits particles or photons in a process called **radioactive decay**. The emitted radiation (gamma rays, beta particles, or positrons) is what the external detector captures.

#### 2.1.1 Modes of Decay Relevant to Nuclear Imaging

| Decay Mode | Particle Emitted | Clinical Use | Example Radionuclide |
|------------|-------------------|----------------|----------------------|
| **Gamma (γ) decay** | High-energy photon (no change in Z or A) | SPECT imaging | ⁹⁹ᵐTc (140 keV) |
| **Beta-plus (β⁺) decay** | Positron (e⁺) | PET imaging (after annihilation) | ¹⁸F (511 keV annihilation) |
| **Isomeric transition (IT)** | Internal conversion + γ emission | Single-photon imaging | ⁹⁹ᵐTc, ¹¹¹In |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** SPECT uses **single-photon emitters** (emit one γ photon per decay), while PET uses **positron emitters** (emit a positron that annihilates with a nearby electron to produce **two 511 keV photons at 180°** to each other — this coincidence detection is the key to PET).

#### 2.1.2 Positron-Electron Annihilation (PET Physics)

When a β⁺ emitter decays, the emitted positron travels a short distance (typically **1–3 mm** in tissue, called the **positron range**), then combines with a nearby electron. Their combined mass is converted into energy via Einstein's equation, producing **two 511 keV photons** that travel in nearly opposite directions (180° ± 0.25°).

The energy released is:

$$E_{total} = 2 m_e c^2$$

where $m_e$ is the electron rest mass (in energy units, 511 keV) and $c$ is the speed of light.

Since momentum must be conserved, the two photons share the energy equally and travel in opposite directions:

$$E_{photon} = m_e c^2 = 511 \text{ keV}$$

### 2.2 The Decay Equation (Exponential Law of Radioactivity)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most tested equation** in nuclear imaging physics for KTU exams. Memorize it thoroughly.

The activity of a radioactive sample decreases **exponentially** with time. The rate of decay is proportional to the number of undecayed nuclei present at that instant.

Let $N(t)$ = number of undecayed nuclei at time $t$, and $\lambda$ = decay constant (probability of decay per nucleus per unit time). Then:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N$$

Integrating both sides from $N_0$ (initial nuclei) to $N(t)$:

$$\int_{N_0}^{N(t)} \frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \int_0^t dt$$

This gives the **exponential decay law**:

$$N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$

The corresponding **activity** $A(t) = \lambda N(t)$ is:

$$A(t) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$

where $A_0$ is the initial activity and $A(t)$ is the activity at time $t$.

### 2.3 Half-Life and Decay Constant Relationship

The half-life $T_{1/2}$ is defined as the time required for $A(t)$ to fall to $A_0/2$:

$$\frac{A_0}{2} = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$

Taking natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\lambda T_{1/2}$$

$$\ln(2) = \lambda T_{1/2}$$

Therefore:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln(2)}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{T_{1/2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key relationship to remember:** $\lambda \cdot T_{1/2} = \ln(2) \approx 0.693$

### 2.4 Mean Life (Average Lifetime)

The **mean life** $\tau$ is the average lifetime of a radioactive atom before decay. For exponential decay, the mean value of $t$ (weighted by decay probability) is:

$$\tau = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{T_{1/2}}{\ln(2)} \approx 1.44 \, T_{1/2}$$

### 2.5 Number of Radioactive Nuclei Required for a Given Activity

Given a desired activity $A$ (in Bq = decays/sec), the number of radioactive atoms $N$ required at that moment is:

$$N = \frac{A}{\lambda} = \frac{A \cdot T_{1/2}}{\ln(2)}$$

For practical calculations with $T_{1/2}$ in seconds.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TABLE: Nuclear Imaging Essential Formulas for KTU Examinations**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description of Variables | Units |
|---|----------------|------------|------------------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Exponential Decay Law | $A(t) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$ | $A_0$ = initial activity, $A(t)$ = activity at time $t$, $\lambda$ = decay constant | $A$ in Bq, $t$ in seconds |
| 2 | Decay Constant | $\lambda = \dfrac{\ln(2)}{T_{1/2}}$ | $T_{1/2}$ = half-life | $\lambda$ in $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 3 | Half-Life | $T_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln(2)}{\lambda} = \dfrac{0.693}{\lambda}$ | Same as above | seconds/minutes/hours |
| 4 | Mean Life | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 1.44 \, T_{1/2}$ | Average lifetime of a radioactive atom | seconds |
| 5 | Number of Atoms | $N = \dfrac{A}{\lambda}$ | Number of undecayed atoms for a given activity | dimensionless count |
| 6 | Annihilation Photon Energy | $E = m_e c^2 = 511$ keV | Energy of each photon in PET coincidence | keV |
| 7 | Curie to Becquerel | $1 \text{ Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \text{ Bq}$ | Unit conversion | Bq |
| 8 | Becquerel Definition | $1 \text{ Bq} = 1 \text{ decay/sec}$ | SI unit of activity | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 9 | Total Annihilation Energy | $E_{total} = 2 m_e c^2 = 1.022$ MeV | Sum of two 511 keV photons | MeV |
| 10 | Spatial Resolution (PET) | $\text{Resolution} \approx 1.2 \sqrt{\frac{d}{200}}$ (in mm, with d in mm) | Empirical formula, $d$ = crystal thickness | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Markdown safety note:** The vertical pipe `|` has been replaced with `\vert` or written descriptively throughout the formula sheet to prevent breaking the markdown table structure.

### 2.7 Radiopharmaceuticals: The Heart of Nuclear Imaging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **radiopharmaceutical** consists of two parts:
> 1. **Radionuclide** — provides the detectable signal (γ-ray or positron).
> 2. **Pharmaceutical vector** — determines *where* the tracer goes in the body (e.g., glucose analog for brain metabolism, bone-seeking diphosphonate for skeletal imaging).

#### 2.7.1 Common Radiopharmaceuticals (High-Yield for KTU)

| Radiopharmaceutical | Radionuclide | Pharmaceutical Vector | Clinical Application | Imaging Modality |
|----------------------|---------------|--------------------------|------------------------|------------------|
| **¹⁸F-FDG** (fluorodeoxyglucose) | ¹⁸F (Fluorine-18) | Glucose analog | Oncology (tumor detection), neurology, cardiac viability | **PET** |
| **⁹⁹ᵐTc-MDP** | ⁹⁹ᵐTc (Technetium-99m) | Methylene diphosphonate | Bone scans (metastases, fractures) | **SPECT** |
| **⁹⁹ᵐTc-sestamibi** | ⁹⁹ᵐTc | Sestamibi (lipophilic cation) | Myocardial perfusion imaging | **SPECT** |
| **²⁰¹Tl-chloride** | ²⁰¹Tl (Thallium-201) | Thallium ion (K⁺ analog) | Myocardial perfusion | **SPECT** |
| **⁶⁷Ga-citrate** | ⁶⁷Ga (Gallium-67) | Citrate | Infection/inflammation imaging | **SPECT** |
| **¹¹¹In-octreotide** | ¹¹¹In (Indium-111) | Octreotide (somatostatin analog) | Neuroendocrine tumors | **SPECT** |
| **⁶⁸Ga-PSMA** | ⁶⁸Ga (Gallium-68) | PSMA ligand | Prostate cancer | **PET** |
| **⁸²Rb-chloride** | ⁸²Rb (Rubidium-82) | Rubidium ion (K⁺ analog) | Myocardial perfusion | **PET** |

#### 2.7.2 Ideal Properties of a Diagnostic Radionuclide

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** You may be asked to list the ideal properties of a radiopharmaceutical. Memorize this list:
> - **Short half-life** (matches imaging duration) — minimizes patient radiation dose
> - **Pure gamma emitter** (or pure positron emitter) — no particulate radiation dose
> - **Decay energy suitable for detection** (100–500 keV for γ; 511 keV for PET)
> - **No toxic daughter products**
> - **Easy and inexpensive production**
> - **Easily incorporated into biologically active molecules**
> - **High target-to-background ratio** (selective uptake)

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Nuclear imaging drives numerous engineering applications:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial and Engineering Relevance:**
> - **Cyclotron design:** Production of short-lived PET isotopes (¹⁸F, ¹¹C, ¹³N, ¹⁵O) requires particle accelerators.
> - **Detector engineering:** Scintillation crystals (NaI(Tl), BGO, LSO, LYSO) must be optimized for photon energy and timing resolution.
> - **Generator systems:** ⁹⁹Mo/⁹⁹ᵐTc generators ("moly cows") are radiochemical engineering marvels that produce ⁹⁹ᵐTc on demand.
> - **Image reconstruction algorithms:** Filtered back-projection and iterative reconstruction (OSEM) require advanced signal processing.
> - **Hybrid systems:** PET/CT and SPECT/CT scanners combine functional and anatomical imaging.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Exponential Decay Law from First Principles

**Goal:** Starting from the physical fact that the rate of decay is proportional to the number of undecayed nuclei, derive the exponential decay law $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$.

#### Step 1: State the Fundamental Assumption

The rate at which radioactive nuclei decay (i.e., the number of decays per unit time) is **directly proportional** to the number of undecayed nuclei $N(t)$ present at that instant. Mathematically:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} \propto -N(t)$$

The negative sign indicates that $N$ *decreases* over time (since nuclei are being lost to decay).

#### Step 2: Introduce the Proportionality Constant

Define the **decay constant** $\lambda$ (a positive number) such that:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N(t)$$

Here, $\lambda$ has units of inverse time (e.g., $\text{s}^{-1}$).

#### Step 3: Separate Variables

Rearrange to put all $N$ terms on one side and $t$ on the other:

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \, dt$$

#### Step 4: Integrate Both Sides

Integrate from the initial condition $N(0) = N_0$ to the current state $N(t)$:

$$\int_{N_0}^{N(t)} \frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \int_0^t dt$$

The left side integrates to the natural logarithm:

$$\ln(N(t)) - \ln(N_0) = -\lambda t$$

#### Step 5: Solve for $N(t)$

Combine the logarithms:

$$\ln\left(\frac{N(t)}{N_0}\right) = -\lambda t$$

Exponentiate both sides:

$$\frac{N(t)}{N_0} = e^{-\lambda t}$$

Finally:

$$\boxed{N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}}$$

#### Step 6: Convert to Activity

Since activity $A = \lambda N$, and $\lambda$ is constant:

$$A(t) = \lambda N(t) = \lambda N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t} = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$

where $A_0 = \lambda N_0$ is the initial activity.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Half-Life to Decay Constant Relationship

**Goal:** Show that $\lambda T_{1/2} = \ln(2)$.

#### Step 1: Apply the Definition of Half-Life

By definition, after one half-life, the activity is half of its initial value:

$$A(T_{1/2}) = \frac{A_0}{2}$$

#### Step 2: Substitute into the Decay Law

Using $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$A_0 \, e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}} = \frac{A_0}{2}$$

#### Step 3: Cancel $A_0$ from Both Sides

$$e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

#### Step 4: Take the Natural Logarithm

$$-\lambda T_{1/2} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln(2)$$

#### Step 5: Solve for the Relationship

$$\boxed{\lambda T_{1/2} = \ln(2) \approx 0.693}$$

#### Step 6: Solve for $\lambda$ Explicitly

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln(2)}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{T_{1/2}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Mean Life (Average Lifetime) Derivation

**Goal:** Show that the mean life $\tau$ of a radioactive atom equals $1/\lambda$.

#### Step 1: Define the Probability Density Function

The probability that a nucleus decays between time $t$ and $t + dt$ is:

$$p(t) \, dt = \lambda \, e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

This is the **normalized decay probability** (the nucleus must survive until time $t$, then decay in the next instant $dt$).

#### Step 2: Compute the Mean (Expected) Lifetime

The mean life is the expected value of $t$:

$$\tau = \int_0^\infty t \, p(t) \, dt = \int_0^\infty t \, \lambda \, e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

#### Step 3: Evaluate the Integral

Using integration by parts (let $u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$):

$$du = dt, \quad v = -e^{-\lambda t}$$

Then:

$$\tau = \left[-t \, e^{-\lambda t}\right]_0^\infty + \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

The boundary term: at $t \to \infty$, $t e^{-\lambda t} \to 0$ (exponential decay dominates); at $t = 0$, the term is $0$. So the boundary contribution is zero.

The remaining integral:

$$\int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_0^\infty = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

#### Step 4: Final Result

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{T_{1/2}}{\ln(2)} \approx 1.44 \, T_{1/2}}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Positron-Electron Annihilation Photon Energy

**Goal:** Show that each photon produced in PET annihilation has 511 keV of energy.

#### Step 1: Initial State

A positron (mass $m_e$) and an electron (mass $m_e$) are at rest and annihilate. Total initial energy:

$$E_{initial} = 2 m_e c^2$$

where $m_e c^2$ is the rest mass energy of one electron (or positron, since they have equal mass).

#### Step 2: Final State

Two photons are produced, each with energy $E_\gamma$, traveling in opposite directions (to conserve momentum).

Total final energy:

$$E_{final} = 2 E_\gamma$$

#### Step 3: Apply Conservation of Energy

$$E_{initial} = E_{final}$$

$$2 m_e c^2 = 2 E_\gamma$$

#### Step 4: Solve for Photon Energy

$$\boxed{E_\gamma = m_e c^2 = 511 \text{ keV}}$$

The numerical value comes from:
- $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg
- $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $E = m_e c^2 = (9.109 \times 10^{-31})(3 \times 10^8)^2 = 8.187 \times 10^{-14}$ J
- Converting: $E = 8.187 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J} \times \frac{1 \text{ eV}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}} = 5.11 \times 10^5 \text{ eV} = 511 \text{ keV}$

### 3.5 Worked Example Problem (KTU Examination Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem:** A patient is administered 400 MBq of ⁹⁹ᵐTc-MDP for a bone scan. The half-life of ⁹⁹ᵐTc is 6 hours. Calculate:
> (a) The decay constant $\lambda$.
> (b) The activity remaining after 4 hours.
> (c) The number of radioactive ⁹⁹ᵐTc atoms initially present in the dose.

#### Part (a): Decay Constant

Given: $T_{1/2} = 6 \text{ hours} = 6 \times 3600 = 21600 \text{ s}$

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln(2)}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{21600 \text{ s}} = 3.208 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

#### Part (b): Activity After 4 Hours

Given: $A_0 = 400$ MBq, $t = 4$ hours

$$A(t) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$A(4) = 400 \times e^{-(3.208 \times 10^{-5})(4 \times 3600)}$$

$$A(4) = 400 \times e^{-0.4619}$$

$$A(4) = 400 \times 0.6300$$

$$A(4) = 252 \text{ MBq}$$

Alternatively, using half-lives directly:
- Number of half-lives elapsed: $n = 4/6 = 0.6667$
- $A(4) = A_0 \times (1/2)^n = 400 \times (0.5)^{0.6667} = 400 \times 0.6300 = 252$ MBq ✓

#### Part (c): Number of Atoms Initially

Using $N_0 = A_0 / \lambda$:

$$N_0 = \frac{400 \times 10^6 \text{ Bq}}{3.208 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}^{-1}} = 1.247 \times 10^{13} \text{ atoms}$$

So approximately **12.47 trillion** radioactive atoms are present in the dose at injection time.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of SPECT vs. PET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparison table is a favorite KTU question. Memorize the key differences.**

| Parameter | **SPECT** (Single Photon Emission CT) | **PET** (Positron Emission Tomography) |
|------------|----------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| Radioactive decay | Single γ photon emission | β⁺ decay → annihilation → two 511 keV photons |
| Photon energy | Typically 100–200 keV (e.g., ¹⁴⁰ keV for ⁹⁹ᵐTc) | Always **511 keV** (fixed) |
| Detection principle | Collimator-based (mechanical collimation) | Electronic **coincidence detection** (no collimator) |
| Sensitivity | Lower (collimator rejects many photons) | Higher (no collimator, full 4π detection) |
| Spatial resolution | 7–15 mm | 4–6 mm |
| Quantification | Semi-quantitative | Truly quantitative (standardized uptake value, SUV) |
| Tracer half-life | Longer (hours) — easier logistics | Shorter (minutes) — needs on-site cyclotron |
| Common isotopes | ⁹⁹ᵐTc, ²⁰¹Tl, ¹¹¹In, ⁶⁷Ga | ¹⁸F, ¹¹C, ¹³N, ¹⁵O, ⁶⁸Ga, ⁸²Rb |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (cyclotron needed) |
| Typical clinical use | Bone scans, myocardial perfusion | Oncology, neurology, cardiac viability |

### 3.7 The Decay Chain Concept: ⁹⁹Mo/⁹⁹ᵐTc Generator

> [!NOTE]
> The ⁹⁹Mo (molybdenum-99) → ⁹⁹ᵐTc (technetium-99m) **generator** is a critical radiochemistry tool. ⁹⁹Mo has a half-life of 66 hours, while its daughter ⁹⁹ᵐTc has a half-life of only 6 hours.

This is an example of a **transient equilibrium**:
- The parent (⁹⁹Mo) decays to the daughter (⁹⁹ᵐTc).
- The daughter then decays independently.
- After a few daughter half-lives (~24 hours), the daughter's activity approaches a constant ratio with the parent's activity.
- The generator is "milked" (eluted with saline) periodically to extract fresh ⁹⁹ᵐTc.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram: Nuclear Imaging Workflow

This block diagram illustrates the end-to-end workflow of a nuclear imaging study, from radiopharmaceutical production to final clinical interpretation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Clinical History<br>and Imaging Request] --> B[Radiopharmaceutical<br>Selection and Dose Calculation]
    C[Cyclotron or<br>Radioisotope Generator] --> B
    B --> D[Intravenous Injection<br>of Radiotracer into Patient]
    D --> E[Uptake Period<br>Minutes to Hours]
    E --> F[Patient Positioning<br>on Imaging Table]
    F --> G{Choice of<br>Modality}
    G -->|SPECT Path| H1[Gamma Camera<br>with Collimator]
    G -->|PET Path| H2[Ring of Scintillation<br>Detectors PET]
    H1 --> I1[Single Photon<br>Detection per Event]
    H2 --> I2[Coincidence Detection<br>of 511 keV Photon Pairs]
    I1 --> J[Image Reconstruction<br>Filtered Backprojection<br>or Iterative OSEM]
    I2 --> J
    J --> K[Attenuation Correction<br>Using CT or Transmission Source]
    K --> L[3D Functional Image<br>Volume Generated]
    L --> M[Radiologist Interpretation<br>and Diagnostic Report]
    M --> N[Clinical Decision<br>Treatment Planning]
```

**Block-by-Block Explanation:**

1. **Clinical Trigger:** A physician orders a nuclear imaging study (e.g., suspected bone metastasis → bone scan).
2. **Radiopharmaceutical Preparation:** A suitable tracer (e.g., ⁹⁹ᵐTc-MDP) is prepared from a generator or cyclotron.
3. **Administration:** The tracer is injected intravenously.
4. **Biodistribution Phase:** The tracer distributes in the body; specific tissues absorb it based on metabolic activity.
5. **Imaging:** Either a gamma camera (SPECT) or a PET ring detects the emitted radiation.
6. **Reconstruction:** Raw data is processed into cross-sectional images.
7. **Attenuation Correction:** Tissue absorption of photons is mathematically compensated.
8. **Interpretation:** A radiologist reads the image and reports findings.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Gamma Camera (SPECT Detector) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Patient with<br>Radiotracer] --> A2[Gamma Photons<br>140 keV for Tc99m]
    A2 --> B1[Lead Collimator<br>Septal Holes Define<br>Direction]
    B1 --> C1[Scintillation Crystal<br>NaI Thallium doped]
    C1 --> D1[Photomultiplier Tube<br>Array PMT]
    D1 --> E1[Positioning Circuit<br>Computes X Y Location]
    D1 --> F1[Energy Window<br>Discrimination Circuit]
    E1 --> G1[Analog to Digital<br>Converter]
    F1 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Computer Image<br>Processing Unit]
    H1 --> I1[Display Monitor<br>2D Projection Image]
```

**Component-Level Function:**

| Component | Function | Engineering Notes |
|------------|------------|---------------------|
| **Lead Collimator** | Allows only photons traveling in a specific direction to reach the detector (parallel-hole, fan-beam, or pinhole types) | Trades off sensitivity vs. resolution; thicker collimator = better resolution but lower sensitivity |
| **Scintillation Crystal (NaI:Tl)** | Converts each gamma photon into thousands of visible light photons | NaI is bright but hygroscopic; needs sealed housing |
| **Photomultiplier Tube (PMT)** | Converts the light flash into an amplified electrical pulse | Gain of 10⁶ typical; modern cameras use SiPM (silicon PM) instead |
| **Positioning Circuit** | Computes the (x, y) coordinates of the original photon interaction | Uses Anger logic — weighted centroid of PMT signals |
| **Energy Discrimination** | Rejects scattered photons (which have lower energy) | Typically a 15–20% energy window around 140 keV |
| **Computer System** | Builds a 2D projection image from the event list | Image is a histogram of detected events |

### 4.3 Block Diagram: PET Detector Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A3[Patient with<br>Positron Emitter] --> B3[Positron Emission<br>and Annihilation]
    B3 --> C3[Two 511 keV Photons<br>at 180 Degrees]
    C3 --> D3[Detector Ring A<br>Multiple Scintillator Crystals]
    C3 --> D4[Detector Ring B<br>Opposite Side of Ring]
    D3 --> E3[Coincidence Timing<br>Window 6 to 12 ns]
    D4 --> E3
    E3 --> F3{Valid Coincidence<br>Detected Yes or No}
    F3 -->|Yes| G3[Line of Response<br>LOR Recorded]
    F3 -->|No| H3[Event Rejected<br>Scatter or Random]
    G3 --> I3[Sinogram Data<br>Stored]
    I3 --> J3[Iterative Reconstruction<br>OSEM or MAP]
    J3 --> K3[3D PET Image<br>Volume with SUV Quantification]
```

**Key Engineering Concepts in PET:**

- **Line of Response (LOR):** The straight line connecting the two coincident detectors. The annihilation must have occurred *somewhere* along this line.
- **Coincidence Window:** A short time interval (~6–12 ns) during which two detected photons are assumed to originate from the same annihilation event. If they fall outside the window, they are rejected as random coincidences.
- **Time-of-Flight (TOF) PET:** Modern PET scanners measure the *time difference* between the two photons' arrivals to localize the annihilation point more precisely along the LOR, improving signal-to-noise ratio.
- **Scintillator Crystals:** Modern PET uses LSO (lutetium oxyorthosilicate) or LYSO crystals because of their high density, fast decay time, and high light output.

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Radiopharmaceutical Design Philosophy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A4[Target Tissue<br>e.g. Tumor] --> B4[Identify Biological<br>Target Molecule]
    B4 --> C4[Select Vector<br>Ligand or Substrate]
    C4 --> D4[Select Radionuclide<br>SPECT or PET]
    D4 --> E4[Synthesize<br>Radiopharmaceutical]
    E4 --> F4[Quality Control<br>Purity and Activity]
    F4 --> G4[Patient<br>Administration]
    G4 --> H4[Tracer Localizes<br>at Target Site]
    H4 --> I4[External Detection<br>Imaging]
```

### 4.5 Comparative Block: SPECT vs. PET Detection Philosophy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[SPECT Principle] --> S2[Single Gamma Photon<br>Emitted]
    S2 --> S3[Photon Travels to<br>Camera]
    S3 --> S4[Lead Collimator<br>Selects Direction]
    S4 --> S5[1 Photon Detected<br>per Event]
    S5 --> S6[2D Projection<br>Image Acquired]
    S6 --> S7[Multiple Projections<br>Around Patient]
    S7 --> S8[Tomographic<br>Reconstruction]

    P1[PET Principle] --> P2[Positron Emitted<br>Annihilation Occurs]
    P2 --> P3[Two 511 keV<br>Photons 180 Degrees]
    P3 --> P4[No Collimator<br>Needed]
    P4 --> P5[2 Photons Detected<br>Coincidence]
    P5 --> P6[Line of Response<br>Recorded]
    P6 --> P7[Surrounding Ring<br>Captures All LORs]
    P7 --> P8[3D Image<br>Reconstructed]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** SPECT requires a *physical* collimator to determine photon direction, which discards ~99% of emitted photons (limiting sensitivity). PET uses *electronic* coincidence detection to determine photon direction, achieving much higher sensitivity — this is why PET is preferred for quantitative imaging despite higher cost.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** KTU Part A questions test definitions, key concepts, and direct application. Aim for crisp, accurate 3-4 sentence answers.

---

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Question]**

**Define nuclear imaging. Mention any two radiopharmaceuticals used in clinical nuclear imaging with their applications.**

**Model Answer:**

Nuclear imaging is a non-invasive medical imaging modality that uses internally administered radioactive tracers (radiopharmaceuticals) to visualize and quantify physiological, metabolic, and molecular functions within the body. Unlike X-ray or CT, which primarily show anatomy, nuclear imaging provides **functional information** by detecting gamma rays or annihilation photons emitted from the radiotracer.

Two commonly used radiopharmaceuticals are:

1. **¹⁸F-FDG (Fluorodeoxyglucose):** A glucose analog labeled with the positron emitter ¹⁸F. It accumulates in cells with high glucose metabolism (e.g., cancer cells) and is widely used in **PET oncology** for tumor detection, staging, and treatment monitoring.

2. **⁹⁹ᵐTc-MDP (Technetium-99m Methylene Diphosphonate):** A bone-seeking tracer labeled with ⁹⁹ᵐTc (a single-photon emitter). It is used in **SPECT bone scintigraphy** to detect skeletal metastases, fractures, and bone infections.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Question]**

**State the exponential decay law. Derive the relationship between decay constant and half-life.**

**Model Answer:**

The **exponential decay law** states that the activity of a radioactive sample decreases exponentially with time according to:

$$A(t) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$

where $A_0$ is the initial activity, $A(t)$ is the activity at time $t$, and $\lambda$ is the **decay constant** (probability of decay per atom per unit time).

**Derivation of $\lambda$ vs $T_{1/2}$ relationship:**

By definition, after one half-life $T_{1/2}$, the activity is half of its initial value:

$$A(T_{1/2}) = \frac{A_0}{2}$$

Substituting into the decay law:

$$A_0 \, e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}} = \frac{A_0}{2}$$

$$e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$-\lambda T_{1/2} = -\ln(2)$$

$$\boxed{\lambda T_{1/2} = \ln(2) \approx 0.693}$$

Therefore: $\lambda = \dfrac{\ln(2)}{T_{1/2}}$ and $T_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{\lambda}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each (typically (a) and (b)). You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Examiners strictly mark step-by-step derivations and diagrams.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the basic principle of nuclear imaging with a block diagram of a gamma camera. List the key components and their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A patient is administered 600 MBq of ⁹⁹ᵐTc-MDP for a bone scan. The half-life of ⁹⁹ᵐTc is 6 hours. Calculate:
- (i) The decay constant. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) The activity remaining after 8 hours. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) The number of radioactive atoms in the initial dose. **(3 marks)**

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Principle of Nuclear Imaging (Gamma Camera / SPECT):**

Nuclear imaging is based on the principle of **internal radiation emission**. A radiopharmaceutical is administered to the patient, and the radionuclide within it decays by emitting **gamma rays**. An external detector (the gamma camera) captures these gamma rays and forms an image showing the **spatial distribution** of the radiotracer, which reflects the underlying **physiological function** of the tissue.

**Block Diagram of a Gamma Camera:**

```
Patient → Lead Collimator → NaI(Tl) Scintillation Crystal
   ↓
Photomultiplier Tube (PMT) Array
   ↓
Positioning Circuit + Energy Window Discriminator
   ↓
ADC → Computer Image Processing → Display
```

**Key Components and Functions:**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|------------|----------|
| 1 | **Lead Collimator** | Physically restricts incoming gamma photons to those traveling in a defined direction (typically perpendicular to the crystal). This is essential for spatial localization. Types include parallel-hole, pinhole, and fan-beam. |
| 2 | **Scintillation Crystal (NaI:Tl)** | Converts each absorbed gamma photon into a burst of visible light photons (~3000 photons per 140 keV gamma). |
| 3 | **Photomultiplier Tubes (PMTs)** | Convert the light flash into an amplified electrical signal. Multiple PMTs (typically 50–100) in a hexagonal array view the crystal from behind. |
| 4 | **Positioning Circuit (Anger Logic)** | Computes the (x, y) coordinates of the original gamma interaction by computing the centroid of the PMT signals weighted by their position. |
| 5 | **Energy Discriminator** | Rejects scattered photons (which have reduced energy) by accepting only pulses within a 15–20% energy window centered on the photopeak (e.g., 140 keV for ⁹⁹ᵐTc). |
| 6 | **ADC and Computer** | Digitizes the event list and reconstructs a 2D projection image. |
| 7 | **Display** | Final image shown for diagnostic interpretation. |

**Working Principle:** When a gamma photon passes through the collimator and strikes the crystal, it produces a light flash. The PMT array converts this to electrical pulses. The positioning circuit determines where the flash occurred (x, y), and the energy circuit checks if the photon energy matches the expected photopeak. Valid events are accumulated over time to form the image.

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

Given:
- Initial activity: $A_0 = 600$ MBq
- Half-life: $T_{1/2} = 6$ hours $= 21600$ s
- Time elapsed: $t = 8$ hours $= 28800$ s

**(i) Decay constant (2 marks):**

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln(2)}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{21600 \text{ s}} = 3.208 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda \approx 3.21 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}^{-1}}$$

**(ii) Activity after 8 hours (2 marks):**

Using $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$A(8) = 600 \times e^{-(3.208 \times 10^{-5})(28800)}$$

$$A(8) = 600 \times e^{-0.924}$$

$$A(8) = 600 \times 0.3968$$

$$\boxed{A(8) \approx 238.1 \text{ MBq}}$$

Alternative (using half-lives):
- Number of half-lives: $n = 8/6 = 1.333$
- $A(8) = 600 \times (0.5)^{1.333} = 600 \times 0.3968 = 238.1$ MBq ✓

**(iii) Number of atoms in the initial dose (3 marks):**

Using $N_0 = A_0 / \lambda$:

$$N_0 = \frac{600 \times 10^6 \text{ Bq}}{3.208 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}^{-1}}$$

$$N_0 = 1.87 \times 10^{13} \text{ atoms}$$

$$\boxed{N_0 \approx 1.87 \times 10^{13} \text{ radioactive atoms}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Compare SPECT and PET imaging modalities. Explain with diagrams how positron-electron annihilation produces the 511 keV photons used in PET. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a radiopharmaceutical? List the ideal properties of a diagnostic radiopharmaceutical. Explain the principle of ⁹⁹Mo/⁹⁹ᵐTc generator with a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Comparison of SPECT and PET:**

| Feature | SPECT | PET |
|---------|--------|-----|
| Decay type | Single γ-photon emission | β⁺ (positron) decay → annihilation |
| Photon energy | 100–200 keV (variable) | 511 keV (fixed) |
| Detection method | Physical collimator | Electronic coincidence (no collimator) |
| Sensitivity | Lower (~0.01–0.1%) | Higher (~1–10%) |
| Spatial resolution | 7–15 mm | 4–6 mm |
| Quantification | Semi-quantitative | Fully quantitative (SUV) |
| Common isotopes | ⁹⁹ᵐTc, ²⁰¹Tl, ¹¹¹In | ¹⁸F, ¹¹C, ¹³N, ¹⁵O, ⁶⁸Ga |
| Cyclotron needed? | No (generator-based) | Usually yes (for short-lived isotopes) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Clinical use | Bone, cardiac perfusion | Oncology, neurology, cardiac viability |

**Positron-Electron Annihilation in PET:**

When a β⁺ emitter (e.g., ¹⁸F) decays, the emitted positron travels a short distance (1–3 mm, the "positron range") in tissue, losing kinetic energy. It then encounters a nearby electron and **annihilates**. By Einstein's mass-energy equivalence, the combined rest mass is converted into two photons.

The annihilation process can be illustrated as:

```
      β⁺ emitter
           |
           |  decay
           ↓
       Positron (e⁺)
           |
           |  travels 1-3 mm
           ↓
    e⁺ + e⁻ (at rest)
           |
           |  annihilation
           ↓
    γ₁ (511 keV) ←———→ γ₂ (511 keV)
    
    (two photons at 180° ± 0.25°)
```

**Energy Calculation:**

Total initial energy (rest mass of positron + electron):

$$E_{initial} = 2 m_e c^2 = 2 \times 511 \text{ keV} = 1022 \text{ keV}$$

By conservation of energy and momentum (since the two particles are essentially at rest):

$$E_{total} = E_{\gamma_1} + E_{\gamma_2} = 2 E_\gamma$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{E_\gamma = 511 \text{ keV}}$$

The two photons travel in **opposite directions** (180° apart, with a slight deviation of ±0.25° due to residual momentum) at the **speed of light**. When two detectors on opposite sides of the patient register a 511 keV photon within a coincidence window (~6–12 ns), the system assumes they originated from the same annihilation event along a **Line of Response (LOR)**.

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Definition of Radiopharmaceutical:**

A **radiopharmaceutical** is a radioactive compound used for diagnosis or therapy in nuclear medicine. It consists of two parts:
1. **Radionuclide** — emits detectable radiation (γ photon or positron).
2. **Pharmaceutical vector** — a biologically active molecule (substrate, ligand, antibody) that determines *where* the tracer localizes in the body.

**Ideal Properties of a Diagnostic Radiopharmaceutical:**

| # | Property | Reason |
|---|------------|----------|
| 1 | **Short half-life** | Matches imaging time, minimizes patient radiation dose |
| 2 | **Pure γ or β⁺ emitter** | No charged-particle radiation, lower dose |
| 3 | **Appropriate γ energy (100–500 keV)** | Efficiently detected by gamma camera; not too low (absorbed) or too high (poor interaction) |
| 4 | **No toxic daughter products** | Patient safety |
| 5 | **High target-to-background ratio** | Image contrast and specificity |
| 6 | **Easy production and low cost** | Practical clinical availability |
| 7 | **Easy chemical incorporation** | Can be attached to various pharmaceutical vectors |
| 8 | **High specific activity** | Small mass of radionuclide can be detected |
| 9 | **Sterile and pyrogen-free** | Patient safety (injected) |
| 10 | **Stable in vivo** | Doesn't break down before reaching target |

**Principle of ⁹⁹Mo/⁹⁹ᵐTc Generator ("Moly Cow"):**

The ⁹⁹Mo/⁹⁹ᵐTc generator is a radiochemical system that produces fresh ⁹⁹ᵐTc on demand at clinical sites without requiring a cyclotron. The principle is **transient secular equilibrium**.

```
       99Mo (parent)
       T½ = 66 hours
            |
            |  β⁻ decay
            ↓
       99mTc (daughter)
       T½ = 6 hours
            |
            |  γ (140 keV)
            ↓
       99Tc (stable)
```

**Working Mechanism:**

1. **Parent isotope:** ⁹⁹Mo (Molybdenum-99), produced in nuclear reactors, has a half-life of 66 hours. It is loaded onto an alumina column inside a shielded container.

2. **Decay:** ⁹⁹Mo undergoes β⁻ decay to form ⁹⁹ᵐTc (metastable Technetium-99m), which is in an excited nuclear state.

3. **Elution:** When saline is passed through the column, ⁹⁹ᵐTc (as pertechnetate, TcO₄⁻) is washed out, while ⁹⁹Mo remains bound to the column. The eluted ⁹⁹ᵐTc is collected in a sterile vial.

4. **Transient Equilibrium:** Because the parent half-life (66 h) is much longer than the daughter half-life (6 h), the daughter's activity reaches a **maximum** after about 24 hours, then decays with the parent's half-life.

5. **Re-growth:** The ⁹⁹ᵐTc activity regrows on the column as more parent ⁹⁹Mo decays. The generator can be **milked (eluted)** every 6–24 hours to obtain fresh ⁹⁹ᵐTc for clinical use.

**Diagram:**

```
   ┌────────────────────────────┐
   │  Lead Shielded Container   │
   │   ┌────────────────────┐   │
   │   │ Alumina Column     │   │
   │   │ with 99Mo bound    │   │
   │   │   ↓                │   │
   │   │ 99mTc eluted with  │   │
   │   │ sterile saline     │   │
   │   └────────────────────┘   │
   │            |                │
   │            ↓                │
   │   ┌────────────────────┐   │
   │   │ Collection Vial    │   │
   │   │ (99mTcO4 in saline)│   │
   │   └────────────────────┘   │
   └────────────────────────────┘
```

**Clinical Use:** ⁹⁹ᵐTc eluted from the generator can be combined with various pharmaceutical kits to produce ⁹⁹ᵐTc-MDP (bone scan), ⁹⁹ᵐTc-sestamibi (cardiac scan), ⁹⁹ᵐTc-DMSA (renal scan), ⁹⁹ᵐTc-HMPAO (brain scan), etc.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in Nuclear Imaging Questions:**

1. **Confusing decay constant $\lambda$ with activity $A$:** $\lambda$ is a property of the radionuclide (constant for a given isotope), while $A$ changes with time. Examiners deduct marks if you use them interchangeably.

2. **Unit conversion errors:** Always convert half-life to **seconds** when computing $\lambda$ in SI units, or vice versa. A common error is to compute $e^{-\lambda t}$ with mismatched units (e.g., $\lambda$ in $\text{s}^{-1}$ but $t$ in hours).

3. **Forgetting to convert MBq to Bq:** 1 MBq = $10^6$ Bq. Mixing units in $N = A/\lambda$ leads to answers off by a factor of $10^6$.

4. **Confusing SPECT and PET energy values:** SPECT photon energies vary (e.g., 140 keV for ⁹⁹ᵐTc, 70–80 keV for ²⁰¹Tl X-rays). PET always uses **511 keV annihilation photons**. Examiners specifically test this.

5. **Not drawing the block diagram for camera components:** Marks are explicitly allocated for block diagrams of the gamma camera or PET system. A textual answer without a diagram will lose at least 2–3 marks.

6. **Stating the formula without derivation:** Examiners expect the derivation of the half-life vs. decay constant relationship, not just the final formula. Marks are awarded for *showing* the integration step.

7. **Confusing half-life with mean life:** $T_{1/2} = 0.693/\lambda$, but $\tau = 1/\lambda$. They differ by a factor of 0.693.

8. **Omitting the unit in the final answer:** Always state the unit of activity (Bq, MBq), energy (keV, MeV), or time (s, hours).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

#### Core Definitions
- ✅ **Nuclear imaging** = functional imaging using radiopharmaceuticals
- ✅ **Radiopharmaceutical** = radionuclide + pharmaceutical vector
- ✅ **Activity** $A$ = rate of decay, in Becquerels (Bq) or Curies (Ci)
- ✅ **Half-life** $T_{1/2}$ = time for activity to halve
- ✅ **Decay constant** $\lambda$ = probability of decay per unit time
- ✅ **Mean life** $\tau = 1/\lambda = 1.44 \, T_{1/2}$

#### Key Formulas (Must Memorize)
- ✅ $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$
- ✅ $\lambda T_{1/2} = \ln(2) = 0.693$
- ✅ $E_\gamma = m_e c^2 = 511$ keV (PET annihilation)
- ✅ $N = A/\lambda$
- ✅ $1 \text{ Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10}$ Bq

#### SPECT vs. PET (Most Tested Comparison)
- ✅ SPECT: single γ photon, uses **collimator**, lower sensitivity
- ✅ PET: two 511 keV photons, **coincidence detection**, no collimator, higher sensitivity
- ✅ PET annihilation produces photons at **180° ± 0.25°**
- ✅ PET photon energy is always **511 keV**

#### Gamma Camera Components (Block Diagram Required)
- ✅ Collimator → Scintillation crystal (NaI:Tl) → PMT array → Positioning circuit → Energy discriminator → ADC → Computer → Display

#### Common Radiopharmaceuticals
- ✅ **¹⁸F-FDG** — PET, oncology
- ✅ **⁹⁹ᵐTc-MDP** — SPECT, bone
- ✅ **⁹⁹ᵐTc-sestamibi** — SPECT, cardiac
- ✅ **²⁰¹Tl-chloride** — SPECT, cardiac
- ✅ **⁶⁷Ga-citrate** — SPECT, infection

#### ⁹⁹Mo/⁹⁹ᵐTc Generator
- ✅ Parent ⁹⁹Mo: $T_{1/2} = 66$ hours (from reactor)
- ✅ Daughter ⁹ᵐTc: $T_{1/2} = 6$ hours (γ emission at 140 keV)
- ✅ Generator is "milked" with saline every 6–24 hours
- ✅ Principle: **transient secular equilibrium**

#### Ideal Radiopharmaceutical Properties
- ✅ Short half-life, pure γ or β⁺ emitter, appropriate energy (100–500 keV for γ), high target-to-background ratio, easy production, no toxic daughter

#### Units and Conversions (Frequently Tested)
- ✅ 1 Bq = 1 decay/sec
- ✅ 1 Ci = $3.7 \times 10^{10}$ Bq
- ✅ 1 MeV = 1000 keV
- ✅ $E = mc^2$ for energy-mass conversion

#### Clinical Applications (Must Know for Viva/Practical)
- ✅ **Oncology:** ¹⁸F-FDG PET for tumor detection, staging
- ✅ **Cardiology:** ⁹⁹ᵐTc-sestamibi SPECT for myocardial perfusion
- ✅ **Neurology:** ¹⁸F-FDG PET for dementia and epilepsy
- ✅ **Bone:** ⁹⁹ᵐTc-MDP SPECT for skeletal metastases

#### Energy of Annihilation Photons (Derivable in Exam)
- ✅ $E_\gamma = m_e c^2 = 511$ keV (from $E = mc^2$ with $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For KTU Module 4 questions on Nuclear Imaging, always:
> 1. Draw a block diagram when asked about a system.
> 2. Show step-by-step derivation for any formula.
> 3. Compare SPECT vs. PET whenever the comparison is asked.
> 4. Use correct units throughout the calculation.
> 5. Mention at least 2–3 clinical applications to demonstrate applied knowledge.

topics.title: Radiation Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT723 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Radiation Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Radiation Detectors — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radiation Detector (KTU Syllabus Definition):**
> A *radiation detector* is an instrument that interacts with ionizing radiation (X-rays, γ-rays, β-particles, α-particles, or neutrons) and converts the energy deposited by the radiation into a measurable, quantifiable signal — typically an electrical pulse, a visible photon (scintillation), or a stored chemical/physical change — that can be correlated to the **type**, **energy**, **intensity**, and **spatial distribution** of the incident radiation.

In medical imaging, detectors form the **backbone of every digital imaging chain** — from radiographic flat-panel detectors, to γ-cameras in nuclear medicine, to the multi-element CT detector arrays. The quality of any clinical image (contrast, SNR, dose) is fundamentally limited by the *intrinsic performance* of the detector chosen.

## 1.2 Broad Classification of Radiation Detectors

Radiation detectors are broadly classified based on the **physical mechanism** used to register the radiation:

| Classification | Mechanism | Typical Devices |
|---|---|---|
| **Gas-filled detectors** | Ionization of gas molecules | Ionization chamber, Proportional counter, Geiger–Müller (GM) counter |
| **Scintillation detectors** | Visible-light emission from excited atoms | NaI(Tl), CsI(Tl), BGO, LSO, plastic scintillators |
| **Semiconductor (solid-state) detectors** | Electron–hole pair generation in a crystal | CdZnTe (CZT), Si, Ge, HgI₂ |
| **Thermoluminescent detectors** | Trapped electron release on heating | TLD chips (LiF, CaF₂) |
| **Photographic detectors** | Chemical reduction of AgBr | Film badges, radiographic films |
| **Neutron-specific detectors** | Nuclear reactions (n,α) or (n,γ) | BF₃ tube, He-3 proportional counter |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:**
> The syllabus explicitly focuses on **gas-filled detectors**, **scintillation detectors**, and **semiconductor detectors**, with supporting treatment of TLDs and film dosimeters.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Radiation Thermometer"

Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to count raindrops falling on a tin roof. You can:
1. **Just count** the drops (how many per minute) — this is like a **GM counter**.
2. **Estimate drop size** by how loud the sound is — this is like a **proportional counter** (pulse height ∝ energy).
3. **Only know if *any* drop fell**, but lose the count if many fall at once — this is **saturation** (dead time behavior).
4. See a *flash of light* on the roof (scintillator) and use a camera to record it — this is a **scintillation + PMT** system.
5. Measure the *heat absorbed* by a chocolate piece left on the roof — this is a **TLD**.

Each "detector" registers the same rain (radiation) but tells you something different about it.

## 1.4 Key Performance Parameters (Defined Upfront)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every radiation detector used in medical imaging is evaluated using the following **four canonical metrics**:
> 1. **Efficiency (ε)** — Fraction of incident radiations actually recorded.
> 2. **Energy Resolution (R)** — Ability to distinguish radiations of close energies.
> 3. **Dead Time (τ)** — Minimum time the detector needs to recover between two events.
> 4. **Sensitivity (S)** — Signal output per unit radiation dose / fluence.

These four metrics will appear repeatedly in the KTU Part B questions.

# Radiation Detectors — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Gas-Filled Detectors — Operational Principle

A gas-filled detector consists of a **cylindrical or planar chamber** filled with a gas (Ar, Kr, Ne, or a P-10 mixture: 90% Ar + 10% CH₄) at moderate pressure, with a central anode wire and an outer cathode wall. A high voltage **V** is applied across the electrodes.

When ionizing radiation enters the chamber, it creates **primary ion pairs** (electron + positive ion). Under the influence of the electric field, these charges drift toward the electrodes and produce a measurable current pulse.

The number of ion pairs initially created by radiation of energy $E$ in a gas of ionization energy $W$ is:

$$ N_{\text{primary}} = \frac{E}{W} $$

where for air, **W ≈ 33.97 eV/ip (≈ 34 eV per ion pair)**.

## 2.2 The Six Operating Regions of a Gas-Filled Detector

As the applied voltage increases (with constant radiation intensity), the detector output transitions through **six distinct regions**. This is the single most heavily tested concept in the KTU module.

| Region | Voltage Range | Behavior | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I — Recombination** | Very low V | Ions recombine before collection | Current < saturation |
| **II — Ionization (Saturation)** | Moderate V | All ions collected, no multiplication | Current ∝ E (chamber region) |
| **III — Proportional** | Higher V | Secondary ionization (Townsend avalanche) | Pulse ∝ E |
| **IV — Limited Proportional** | Even higher V | Space charge distorts proportionality | Pulse no longer ∝ E |
| **V — Geiger–Müller** | High V | Avalanche spreads along entire anode | Pulse ∝ V, independent of E |
| **VI — Continuous Discharge** | Excessive V | Gas breaks down, detector damaged | Unusable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**
> - **Region II → Ionization Chamber** (radiotherapy dosimetry).
> - **Region III → Proportional Counter** (spectroscopy, low-activity counting).
> - **Region V → Geiger–Müller Counter** (radiation protection survey meter).

### 2.2.1 Townsend Avalanche & Gas Multiplication

In the proportional region, each primary electron accelerates and ionizes further gas molecules, producing an **avalanche**. The multiplication factor $M$ is given by the **Townsend equation**:

$$ M = \exp\left[\int_0^{a} \alpha(x)\, dx\right] $$

where $\alpha(x)$ is the **first Townsend ionization coefficient** (ionizations per unit length) and $a$ is the anode wire radius. The total charge collected per primary event is then:

$$ Q = N_{\text{primary}} \cdot e \cdot M $$

The pulse height is therefore **proportional to the original radiation energy E**, which is why this region is called "proportional."

### 2.2.2 Geiger–Müller Region — The Quench

In the GM region, the avalanche becomes self-propagating: UV photons emitted during the avalanche photoionize the gas at distant points, triggering *new* avalanches along the entire anode. Without intervention, this would lead to a **continuous discharge** (Region VI). To prevent this, a **quenching agent** is added:

- **Halogen-quenched tubes** (e.g., Br₂, Cl₂) — modern, lower operating voltage (~500 V), long life.
- **Organic-quenched tubes** (e.g., ethanol, formic acid vapor) — older, higher voltage (~800–1000 V), short life (~10⁸ counts).

## 2.3 Scintillation Detectors — Theory of Operation

A scintillator is a material that **emits visible or near-visible light** promptly after absorbing ionizing radiation. The sequence of events is:

1. **Absorption** — Radiation interacts in the scintillator volume (photoelectric, Compton, or pair production).
2. **Energy transfer** — Excites atoms/molecules of the host lattice.
3. **Luminescence** — De-excitation releases a **scintillation photon** (typical λ = 400–500 nm).
4. **Light collection** — Photons are guided to a **photomultiplier tube (PMT)** or **silicon photomultiplier (SiPM)**.
5. **Signal conversion** — PMT converts photons → electrons → multiplied cascade → electrical pulse.

### 2.3.1 Key Scintillator Properties (KTU Table)

| Scintillator | Density (g/cm³) | Z_eff | Decay Time (ns) | Light Yield (ph/keV) | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NaI(Tl)** | 3.67 | 50 | 230 | ~38 | γ-camera, general nuclear medicine |
| **CsI(Tl)** | 4.51 | 54 | 1000 (slow) / 16 (fast) | ~52 | CT detectors, fluoroscopy |
| **BGO (Bi₄Ge₃O₁₂)** | 7.13 | 74 | 300 | ~9 | PET (older generation) |
| **LSO/LYSO (Lu₂SiO₅:Ce)** | 7.4 | 66 | 40 | ~30 | Modern PET, time-of-flight |
| **Plastic (BC-408)** | 1.03 | ~6 | 2.1 | ~10 | Fast timing, beta detection |
| **CdWO₄** | 7.9 | 64 | 5000 | ~12 | CT, security scanners |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Z_eff matters:** Higher Z_eff → higher photoelectric absorption probability → better detection efficiency for γ-photons in nuclear medicine (typical γ-energies: 140 keV for ⁹⁹ᵐTc, 511 keV for annihilation photons).

## 2.4 Semiconductor Detectors — Solid-State Ionization

In a semiconductor detector (e.g., **Si**, **Ge**, **CdZnTe (CZT)**, **HgI₂**), the absorption of radiation creates **electron–hole pairs** instead of ion pairs. The average energy required to create one e–h pair is:

$$ W_{\text{semi}} \approx 3.6 \text{ eV (for Si at 300 K)} $$

This is **~10× smaller** than the W-value for gas (34 eV). Therefore, for the same radiation energy, a semiconductor produces ~10× more charge carriers → **much better energy resolution** and **better signal-to-noise ratio**.

### 2.4.1 Why CZT Dominates Medical Imaging Today

CZT (Cadmium Zinc Telluride) is the detector of choice in modern **dedicated cardiac SPECT cameras** (e.g., GE Discovery NM 530c) and **photon-counting CT (PCCT)** because:
- High Z_eff ≈ 50, density ≈ 5.8 g/cm³ → excellent γ-stopping power.
- Operates at **room temperature** (unlike Ge which needs liquid-N₂ cooling).
- Can be fabricated into **pixelated arrays** → direct conversion → superior spatial resolution.
- Energy resolution ~3–5% at 140 keV (vs. ~10% for NaI(Tl)+PMT).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every equation** required to solve Module-4 KTU problems. Memorize the *form*, the *symbols*, and the *units*.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_{\text{ip}} = E / W$ | Primary ion pairs (gas) | $E$ in eV, $W \approx 34$ eV for air |
| 2 | $Q = N_{\text{ip}} \cdot e \cdot M$ | Total collected charge | $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C |
| 3 | $M = \exp\!\left[\int_0^a \alpha(x)\,dx\right]$ | Townsend gas multiplication | Dimensionless; valid Region III |
| 4 | $R = \frac{\Delta E}{E} \times 100\%$ | Energy resolution (FWHM) | %; lower is better |
| 5 | $n = n_0 \exp(-\mu x)$ | Attenuation (governs efficiency) | $\mu$ in cm⁻¹ |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_g = 1 - \exp(-\mu x)$ | Geometric/intrinsic efficiency | Fraction (0–1) |
| 7 | $n_m = \frac{n_t}{1 - n_t \tau}$ | Observed rate correction | $\tau$ = dead time, $n_t$ = true rate |
| 8 | $N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t} = N_0 \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{t/t_{1/2}}$ | Radioactive decay | $\lambda = \ln 2 / t_{1/2}$ |
| 9 | $W_{\text{semi}} \approx 3.6$ eV | e–h pair creation energy in Si | Compare with 34 eV for gas |
| 10 | $R_{\text{Fano}} = \sqrt{\frac{F \cdot W}{E}}$ | Theoretical Fano-limited resolution | $F$ ≈ 0.1 (Fano factor for Si) |
| 11 | $N = \sigma \cdot \Sigma \cdot \phi$ | Neutron reaction rate | Cross-sections in barns (1 b = 10⁻²⁴ cm²) |
| 12 | $\text{Response}_R = \frac{\text{Output signal}}{\text{Input dose rate}}$ | Detector sensitivity | V/cGy·min⁻¹ typical |

> **Critical Formatting Note:** All absolute-value or "such-that" bars in the table above use vertical bars that are *part of the equation text* (e.g., for $F$-factor ratios) but are *outside* the table cell delimiter syntax. Students rewriting this in exams should use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Detector Family | Clinical Application | Why Chosen |
|---|---|---|
| **Ionization chamber** | Radiotherapy dose calibration (Farmer-type, 0.6 cm³) | Linear response, absolute dose standard |
| **GM counter** | Personnel radiation protection surveys | Cheap, robust, high sensitivity, no energy info |
| **Proportional counter** | Tissue-equivalent neutron dosimetry, low-energy X-ray spectroscopy | Pulse ∝ energy, modest amplification |
| **NaI(Tl) scintillation** | γ-camera (SPECT) planar imaging | High light yield, moderate Z_eff, well-proven |
| **CZT semiconductor** | Dedicated cardiac SPECT, photon-counting CT | Best energy resolution, direct conversion |
| **LSO/LYSO scintillator** | PET ring detectors | Short decay time → excellent timing resolution |
| **TLD (LiF:Mg,Ti)** | Patient entrance/exit dose, ring badges | Small, tissue-equivalent, reusable after annealing |
| **Film (Gafchromic)** | Brachytherapy, IMRT QA | High spatial resolution, archival record |

## 2.7 Counting Statistics — The "Why" Behind Noise

All radiation events are **random in time**. The probability of $k$ events occurring in a fixed time interval follows a **Poisson distribution**:

$$ P(k) = \frac{\bar{k}^{\,k}\, e^{-\bar{k}}}{k!} $$

For large $\bar{k}$ (> 20), Poisson → **Gaussian** with standard deviation:

$$ \sigma = \sqrt{N} $$

The **relative standard deviation** (used to express statistical uncertainty in counts) is:

$$ \text{RSD} = \frac{\sigma}{N} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering consequence:** To halve the noise in a nuclear-medicine image, you must collect **4×** the counts → either 4× the scan time or 4× the injected activity (the latter is *not* clinically acceptable for patient dose).
