lambda max times T equals b"] D --> F["Engineering Application
Compute total IR power from skin"] E --> G["Engineering Application
Choose correct detector band LWIR"] ``` **Educational Note:** Both Wien's law and Stefan–Boltzmann's law emerge from the **same** Planck's law — one by differentiation, the other by integration. KTU frequently asks: *"How are these two laws related to Planck's law?"* ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 KTU Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model QP]** > *Define infrared thermal imaging. State the wavelength range used in medical applications.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Infrared thermal imaging is a non-contact, non-ionizing imaging technique that detects the **infrared radiation emitted from the surface of biological tissues** and converts it into a **visible temperature map (thermogram)**. It is based on the principle of **blackbody radiation**, wherein every object above absolute zero emits IR radiation proportional to its surface temperature. The **wavelength range used in medical applications is the Long-Wave Infrared (LWIR) band, 8 – 14 µm**. This range is chosen because: - The peak emission wavelength of human skin (~ 33 °C) calculated using Wien's law is ~ 9.35 µm, which lies in this band. - The **8–14 µm band is an atmospheric transmission window**, allowing photons to reach the camera with minimal absorption. - The emissivity of skin in this band is ε ≈ 0.98, treating it as a near-perfect blackbody. > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – December 2022, Model QP]** > *State and explain Stefan–Boltzmann law. Why is it significant in thermal imaging?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Stefan–Boltzmann law** states that the **total radiant power per unit area emitted by a blackbody is directly proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature**: $$ M = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 $$ where σ = 5.670 × 10⁻⁸ W · m⁻² · K⁻⁴ is the Stefan–Boltzmann constant, T is the absolute temperature in Kelvin, and ε is the emissivity. **Significance in thermal imaging:** The **T⁴ dependence** means that even a small change in surface temperature (e.g., 1 °C, as occurs during inflammation or tumor growth) produces a *relatively large* change in emitted power (≈ 1.2 % per °C). This high sensitivity allows thermal cameras to detect subtle, clinically meaningful temperature variations and convert them into a visible thermogram for diagnosis. --- ### 5.2 KTU Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions are 14 marks each with sub-parts of 7 + 7 marks. Two alternative questions (A and B) are provided to mirror the **internal choice** format. #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model QP]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **principle of operation of an infrared thermal imaging system**. Mention the role of the focal plane array. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Stefan–Boltzmann law** from **Planck's law** of blackbody radiation. Mention the value of the Stefan–Boltzmann constant. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Principle of Operation (7 marks):** A thermal imaging system operates on the principle of detecting **infrared radiation emitted by the body** in the **8–14 µm LWIR band** and converting it into a visible temperature map. The block diagram consists of the following stages: 1. **Heat Source (Human Body):** The human body maintains a surface temperature of ~ 32 °C and emits IR photons in the LWIR band. 2. **IR Optics (Germanium / ZnSe lens):** These materials transmit IR efficiently and focus the emitted radiation onto the detector. 3. **Atmospheric Window:** The 8–14 µm band is chosen because atmospheric water vapor and CO₂ have minimal absorption in this range. 4. **Focal Plane Array (FPA):** The heart of the system. A 2D array (e.g., 320 × 240 or 640 × 480) of microbolometer pixels, each containing a heat-sensitive material (VOₓ or a-Si) whose **electrical resistance changes with absorbed IR energy**. The FPA is read out line-by-line, producing a voltage map proportional to the incident IR power. 5. **Signal Processing:** Amplification, low-pass filtering, and 16-bit analog-to-digital conversion produce a digital image. 6. **Display:** A false-color palette (rainbow or ironbow) maps each pixel's digital value to a color, producing the **thermogram**. 7. **Clinical Interpretation:** The clinician analyzes the temperature distribution for abnormalities. The **Focal Plane Array** is the **transduction element** that converts incoming IR photons (radiant energy) into electrical signals — without it, no thermogram can be produced. Modern uncooled FPAs (microbolometers) eliminate the need for cryogenic cooling, enabling portable clinical cameras. **(b) Derivation of Stefan–Boltzmann Law (7 marks):** [Full step-by-step derivation as in **Section 3.1** above. Reproduce steps 1 through 9 with proper annotations. The final boxed equation is $M = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ with $\sigma \approx 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W}\,\text{m}^{-2}\,\text{K}^{-4}$.] --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – December 2023 Model QP]** **(a)** State **Wien's displacement law**. Calculate the **peak emission wavelength** of a normal human body at 37 °C. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the working of a **microbolometer-based focal plane array**. Compare it with a cooled photon detector. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Wien's Displacement Law (7 marks):** **Statement:** Wien's displacement law states that the **wavelength at which a blackbody emits maximum radiation is inversely proportional to its absolute temperature**: $$ \lambda_{\max} = \frac{b}{T} $$ where **b = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m · K** is Wien's displacement constant. **Calculation for human body at T = 37 °C = 310 K:** $$ \lambda_{\max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{310} \ \text{m} $$ $$ \lambda_{\max} \approx 9.35 \ \mu\text{m} $$ This wavelength falls in the **8–14 µm long-wave infrared (LWIR) atmospheric window**, confirming why medical thermal cameras are designed for this band. Any deviation in the surface temperature (e.g., 1 °C increase) shifts $\lambda_{\max}$ to a slightly shorter wavelength and changes the spectral radiance curve, both of which are detected by the FPA. **(b) Microbolometer Working Principle (7 marks):** A **microbolometer** is an **uncooled thermal IR detector** consisting of a thin membrane (~ 200 nm) of a temperature-sensitive resistive material (typically **vanadium oxide (VOₓ)** or **amorphous silicon (a-Si:H)**) suspended over a silicon read-out integrated circuit (ROIC) by thermally isolating legs. **Working principle:** 1. IR photons from the target scene strike the microbolometer membrane. 2. The absorbed radiation **raises the membrane's temperature** by a tiny amount (milli-Kelvins). 3. The membrane's **electrical resistance changes** (VOₓ has a large temperature coefficient of resistance, TCR ≈ −2 %/K). 4. The change in resistance is **measured by applying a bias current** and reading the voltage drop. 5. Each pixel produces a voltage proportional to incident IR power, which is digitized into a temperature value. **Comparison Table with Cooled Photon Detectors:** | Parameter | Microbolometer | Cooled Photon Detector (HgCdTe) | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Cooling Required | None (room temp) | Yes (LN₂ or Stirling, 77 K) | | Mechanism | Resistance change vs. T | Photon-induced electron transition | | NETD | 0.05 – 0.1 °C | ≤ 0.025 °C | | Response Time | ~ 10 ms | Microseconds | | Cost | Low | Very high | | Clinical Suitability | **Excellent (routine use)** | Limited (research-grade) | | Power Consumption | < 2 W | 5 – 15 W (with cryocooler) | The microbolometer is **the dominant technology in clinical thermography** because it eliminates cryogenic maintenance while delivering sufficient sensitivity for medical diagnosis. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in IR Imaging Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the unit conversion to Kelvin.** Students plug 37 °C directly into Stefan–Boltzmann or Wien's law. Marks are deducted immediately. **Always convert to Kelvin first.** Example: 37 °C = 310 K, *not* 37. > > 2. **Confusing radiance with exitance.** Spectral radiance *B*₍λ₎ (W · m⁻² · sr⁻¹ · m⁻¹) and spectral exitance *M*₍λ₎ = π *B*₍λ₎ differ by the factor π (Lambertian assumption). Examiners explicitly test this. > > 3. **Stating Planck's law without stating the underlying assumption.** A common 1-mark loss: the model assumes a **perfect blackbody cavity** with discrete energy levels. Always mention this. > > 4. **Incorrect Δ*M* / ΔT relationship.** The exact relationship is dM/dT = 4σT³, not σT³. A frequent ½-mark loss. > > 5. **Wrong wavelength band.** Stating "IR imaging uses 0.7 – 1.3 µm" loses full marks. The correct medical band is **8 – 14 µm (LWIR)**, with peak at **~ 9.35 µm**. > > 6. **Omitting emissivity.** The skin has ε ≈ 0.98, not 1.0. Always include the ε factor in the Stefan–Boltzmann expression for a real surface. > > 7. **Mixing up "thermography" with "thermometry".** Thermometry measures a *single* point temperature; thermography produces a 2D *image*. Examiners deduct for this conflation. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** Thermal imaging is the visualization of naturally emitted blackbody IR radiation from tissues in the 8–14 µm band as a 2D false-color temperature map. - **Key Physical Window:** The **8–14 µm LWIR atmospheric window** matches the ~ 9.35 µm peak emission of the human body (Wien's law at 310 K). - **Planck's Law:** $B_\lambda(\lambda,T) = \dfrac{2hc^2}{\lambda^5}\dfrac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda kT}-1}$ — the foundation of all thermal physics. - **Stefan–Boltzmann Law:** $M = \varepsilon\sigma T^4$, with σ = **5.670 × 10⁻⁸** W · m⁻² · K⁻⁴. Yields ~ 1.2 % power change per °C at body temperature. - **Wien's Displacement Law:** $\lambda_{\max} = b/T$ with **b = 2.898 × 10⁻³** m · K. Always use **Kelvin**. - **Human Skin Emissivity:** ε ≈ 0.98 (near-perfect blackbody) in the LWIR band. - **Two Detector Families:** **Cooled photon detectors (HgCdTe, InSb)** — high sensitivity, expensive, research-grade. **Uncooled thermal detectors (microbolometer)** — lower sensitivity, inexpensive, dominant in clinical use. - **NETD Requirement:** Clinical thermal cameras require **NETD ≤ 100 mK (0.1 °C)** to detect inflammation and tumor signatures. - **Safety:** IR photons at 9.35 µm carry **~ 0.13 eV** energy — **non-ionizing, safe for repeated use, pregnancy, neonates**. - **Block Diagram Stages (10 blocks to remember):** Body → Blackbody emission → Atmosphere → Optics → FPA → Analog front-end → ADC/DSP → Display processor → Thermogram → Clinical interpretation. - **Clinical Applications:** Breast cancer screening, peripheral vascular disease, burn depth assessment, Raynaud's phenomenon, fever triage, diabetic foot, sports-injury inflammation, neonatal monitoring. - **Advantages:** Non-contact, non-ionizing, non-invasive, real-time, no contrast agent, repeatable, radiation-free. - **Limitations:** Limited to surface temperature; cannot see deep tissues; sensitive to ambient temperature; requires thermal stabilization chamber for high accuracy. - **Numerical Constants to Memorize:** h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s, c = 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s, k = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K, σ = 5.670 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²K⁴, b = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K, ε_skin = 0.98.

topics.title: Physics of Thermography - IR Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT723 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Physics of Thermography - IR Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Medical Thermography?

**Medical Thermography** (also called **Digital Infrared Thermal Imaging — DITI**) is a non-invasive, non-contact, radiation-free diagnostic imaging modality that maps the surface temperature distribution of the human body by detecting the **infrared (IR) radiation** naturally emitted from the skin. Every object with a temperature above absolute zero (0 K or **−273.15 °C**) emits electromagnetic radiation, and human skin (at ~305 K) emits predominantly in the **long-wave infrared (LWIR)** band of 8 µm to 14 µm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Thermography is the pictorial representation of the *emitted* infrared radiation from a body surface. It is a *passive* imaging technique (no external radiation is sent into the body, unlike X-ray or CT), making it 100% safe with no ionizing radiation exposure.

## 1.2 The Infrared Spectrum for Medical Use

The infrared window used in clinical thermography is typically the **atmospheric transmission window of 8 µm – 14 µm** (sometimes 3 µm – 5 µm), because IR radiation in this band is least absorbed by atmospheric water vapor and CO₂ and can therefore reach the detector cleanly.

| IR Sub-band | Wavelength Range | Physical Origin | Medical Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Near Infrared (NIR) | 0.75 µm – 1.4 µm | Reflected solar / LED | Optical imaging, pulse oximetry overlap |
| Short-Wave IR (SWIR) | 1.4 µm – 3 µm | Hot emitters (~700 K) | Industrial, not body |
| Mid-Wave IR (MWIR) | 3 µm – 8 µm | Hot sources, some skin emission | High-temperature clinical |
| **Long-Wave IR (LWIR)** | **8 µm – 14 µm** | **Thermal skin emission (305 K)** | **Primary medical thermography band** |
| Far IR (FIR) | 15 µm – 1000 µm | Deep thermal | Limited use |

## 1.3 What is an IR Detector?

An **Infrared (IR) Detector** is a transducer that converts incident infrared photons (or thermal energy carried by them) into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, or change in resistance). It is the heart of every thermal camera, just as the CCD/CMOS sensor is the heart of a digital camera.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Grand Families of IR Detectors (KTU High-Yield):**
> 1. **Thermal Detectors** — absorb IR energy, warm up, change some temperature-dependent property (resistance, voltage, polarization). They are *slow* but *broadband* and work without cryogenic cooling.
> 2. **Photon (Quantum) Detectors** — absorb individual IR photons and directly generate charge carriers. They are *fast* and *sensitive* but often require **cryogenic cooling** (e.g., liquid nitrogen at 77 K).

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Warm Handprint" Analogy

Imagine you walk barefoot across a cold tile floor at night and then place your hand on a wall. Although the room is dark, you *feel* a warm hand-shaped ghost on the wall. Your skin is essentially a crude IR detector — it absorbs the long-wave infrared photons emitted by the warm spot and your nerve endings convert that energy into a thermal sensation.

A thermographic camera does the same thing, but quantitatively:
- Instead of a *naked hand*, it uses a **specialized IR-sensitive surface** (the detector).
- Instead of *nerve endings*, it uses **electronics** to convert the heat into voltage.
- Instead of *mental interpretation*, a computer rebuilds a **2-D thermal image (thermogram)** where pixel brightness = temperature.

> [!TIP]
> **Why skin emission matters:** Human skin has an **emissivity ε ≈ 0.98** in the 8–14 µm band — it behaves almost like a perfect blackbody. This is what makes accurate medical thermography possible: the camera can infer skin temperature directly from emitted radiance using Planck's law.

---

## 1.5 The Blackbody Concept (Foundation of Thermography)

A **Blackbody** is an *idealized* object that absorbs **100%** of all incident electromagnetic radiation at every wavelength and, when in thermal equilibrium, re-emits the maximum possible thermal radiation. The spectral distribution of its emitted radiation depends *only* on its absolute temperature **T** (in Kelvin) — given by **Planck's Law**.

**Real human skin** is *not* a perfect blackbody, but it is very close. We characterize it by its **emissivity (ε)**, a dimensionless number between 0 (perfect mirror) and 1 (perfect blackbody).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Emissivity of human skin: ε ≈ 0.98 ± 0.01** in the 8–14 µm band. This is why medical thermography is highly accurate in this window.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Blackbody Radiation

A thermography system is entirely governed by four fundamental laws of blackbody radiation. Every KTU exam question on thermography physics is rooted in these equations.

### Pillar 1 — Planck's Radiation Law (Spectral Emittance)

Planck's law gives the **spectral radiant emittance** $M_\lambda$ (W·m⁻²·µm⁻¹) of a blackbody at temperature T and wavelength λ:

$$M_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]^{-1}$$

where $h$ is **Planck's constant** = $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c$ is the speed of light, and $k_B$ is the **Boltzmann constant** = $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.

> [!NOTE]
> For a *real* (non-black) body, the emittance is multiplied by emissivity: $M_\lambda^{\text{real}} = \varepsilon(\lambda)\, M_\lambda^{\text{black}}$.

### Pillar 2 — Wien's Displacement Law

The wavelength at which a blackbody emits with **maximum intensity** is *inversely proportional* to its absolute temperature:

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{b}{T}$$

where $b$ is **Wien's displacement constant** = $2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K (≈ 2898 µm·K).

**Worked Insight for Skin:** Plug in T = 305.15 K (32 °C skin):

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{305.15} \approx 9.50 \;\mu\text{m}$$

This sits squarely inside the **8–14 µm atmospheric window** — a beautiful piece of physics that explains *why* nature (and engineering) chose this band for thermal cameras.

### Pillar 3 — Stefan–Boltzmann Law (Total Power)

The **total** radiant power per unit area emitted by a blackbody is:

$$M = \sigma T^4$$

where $\sigma$ is the **Stefan–Boltzmann constant** = $5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴.

For a real (gray) body: $M = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$.

### Pillar 4 — Kirchhoff's Law of Thermal Radiation

At thermal equilibrium, the **spectral emissivity equals the spectral absorptivity** for every wavelength:

$$\varepsilon(\lambda) = \alpha(\lambda)$$

This is the reason a *good absorber* is a *good emitter*. Skin (ε ≈ 0.98) absorbs 98% of incident IR and re-emits 98% of blackbody radiance.

---

## 2.2 IR Detector Performance Metrics (The "Detector Spec Sheet")

To compare detectors, engineers use standardized parameters. These are the **most-asked parameters in KTU Part B questions.**

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Unit | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Responsivity** | $\mathcal{R}$ or $R$ | Output signal (V or A) per unit incident IR power | V/W or A/W | How *strong* the response is |
| **Noise Equivalent Power** | **NEP** | Incident power required to produce a signal-to-noise ratio of 1 in a 1-Hz bandwidth | W | Lower is **better** — most sensitive detector limit |
| **Detectivity** | $D$ | Reciprocal of NEP: $D = 1/\text{NEP}$ | W⁻¹ | Higher is better |
| **Specific Detectivity** | **D\*** (D-star) | Normalized detectivity: $D^* = D \sqrt{A \cdot \Delta f}$ | cm·Hz¹ᐟ²·W⁻¹ (Jones) | Allows fair comparison between detectors of different area/bandwidth |
| **Response Time / Time Constant** | $\tau$ | Time for output to reach 63.2% of final value | s, ms, µs | Critical for *real-time* thermography |
| **Spectral Response Range** | $\lambda_1 – \lambda_2$ | Wavelengths at which the detector responds | µm | Defines the application band |

The defining relation for **D-star** is:

$$D^* = \frac{\sqrt{A_s \cdot \Delta f}}{\text{NEP}}$$

where $A_s$ is the sensitive detector area and $\Delta f$ is the noise bandwidth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick for D-star:** Bigger D-star = better detector. The unit "Jones" (cm·Hz¹ᐟ²·W⁻¹) is named after R. Clark Jones, who defined the metric.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table contains **every high-yield equation** for Module 4. Memorize the units, not just the symbols.

| # | Law / Quantity | Formula | Constants & Units | Real-World Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Planck's spectral emittance | $M_\lambda = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5}\left[\exp\!\left(\tfrac{hc}{\lambda k_B T}\right) - 1\right]^{-1}$ | W·m⁻²·µm⁻¹ | Detector calibration, radiance-to-temperature conversion |
| 2 | Wien's Displacement | $\lambda_{\max} = \frac{b}{T}$ | b = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K | Selecting camera spectral band |
| 3 | Stefan–Boltzmann | $M = \sigma T^4$ | σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ | Total radiated power estimation |
| 4 | Real-body emittance | $M_{\text{real}} = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | ε dimensionless (skin ≈ 0.98) | Skin-temperature-from-radiance inversion |
| 5 | Kirchhoff's Law | $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ | dimensionless | Justifies using skin as a near-blackbody |
| 6 | Peak Spectral Emittance | $M_{\lambda_{\max}} \propto T^5$ | W·m⁻²·µm⁻¹ | Imaging system sensitivity design |
| 7 | Bolometer Resistance | $R(T) = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)\right]$ | Ω; α = TCR (K⁻¹) | Microbolometer pixel design |
| 8 | Responsivity | $\mathcal{R} = \frac{V_s}{P_{\text{in}}}$ | V/W | Detector output prediction |
| 9 | NEP | $\text{NEP} = \frac{V_n}{\mathcal{R}}$ | W | Detector sensitivity floor |
| 10 | Specific Detectivity | $D^* = \frac{\sqrt{A_s \Delta f}}{\text{NEP}}$ | cm·Hz¹ᐟ²·W⁻¹ (Jones) | Fair cross-detector comparison |
| 11 | Thermographic Sensitivity | $\Delta T_{\min} \propto \frac{1}{D^*}$ | mK, m°C | Smallest temperature difference a camera can resolve (NETD) |

---

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

In a real medical thermal camera, the chain is:

1. **Skin emits** IR per Planck's law modified by ε ≈ 0.98.
2. **Optics** (germanium or chalcogenide lenses — IR-transparent glass) collect and focus the IR onto the detector array.
3. **Detector** converts each pixel's IR power into a voltage per responsivity $\mathcal{R}$.
4. **Electronics** amplify and digitize.
5. **Software** inverts the radiance to a temperature using the inverse Planck function:

$$T = \frac{h c}{\lambda k_B \left[\ln\!\left(\frac{2 \pi h c^2 \varepsilon}{\lambda^5 M_\lambda} + 1\right)\right]}$$

The NEP and D-star of the chosen detector literally *limit* the smallest temperature difference the camera can show. Modern uncooled microbolometer cameras achieve **NETD (Noise Equivalent Temperature Difference) < 30 mK**, which is sufficient for breast cancer screening, vascular imaging, and inflammation mapping.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through the **exact derivations a KTU board examiner expects** in a 14-mark derivation question. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly — no skipped steps.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Wien's Displacement Law from Planck's Law

**Goal:** Prove that $\lambda_{\max} T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K.

### Step 1 — Start with Planck's spectral emittance

We begin with the spectral emittance function:

$$M_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1}$$

### Step 2 — Find the maximum by setting $\partial M_\lambda / \partial \lambda = 0$

To find the wavelength where emittance peaks, take the partial derivative with respect to λ (holding T constant) and set it equal to zero. Substitute $x = \frac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}$ to simplify. The equation reduces to:

$$\frac{x\, e^{x}}{e^{x} - 1} = 5$$

### Step 3 — Solve the transcendental equation numerically

This transcendental equation has a unique real solution:

$$x = 4.9651\ldots$$

We denote this as $x_{\text{max}} = 4.9651$.

### Step 4 — Back-substitute the definition of x

Recall that $x = \frac{h c}{\lambda_{\max} k_B T}$, therefore:

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{h c}{x_{\max}\, k_B T}$$

### Step 5 — Insert constants to get Wien's constant b

$$\lambda_{\max} T = \frac{h c}{x_{\max}\, k_B} = b$$

Plugging in the values:

$$b = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{(4.9651)(1.381 \times 10^{-23})} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m·K}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m·K} \approx 2898\;\mu\text{m·K}\;}$$

### Step 6 — Apply to human skin (T = 305 K)

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{305} = 9.50 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m} = 9.50\;\mu\text{m}$$

This confirms that skin's peak emission lies in the **8–14 µm LWIR window** — the reason thermography cameras use this band.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stefan–Boltzmann Law by Integrating Planck's Law

**Goal:** Prove that total power $M = \sigma T^4$.

### Step 1 — Total emittance is the integral of spectral emittance over all wavelengths

$$M(T) = \int_{0}^{\infty} M_\lambda(\lambda, T)\, d\lambda = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1}\, d\lambda$$

### Step 2 — Change of variable

Let $u = \frac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}$, so $\lambda = \frac{h c}{u k_B T}$ and $d\lambda = -\frac{h c}{u^2 k_B T} du$. Limits: $\lambda \to 0 \Rightarrow u \to \infty$ and $\lambda \to \infty \Rightarrow u \to 0$. Flipping limits removes the negative sign.

### Step 3 — Substitute

$$M(T) = \frac{2 \pi k_B^{4} T^{4}}{h^{3} c^{2}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{u^{3}}{e^{u} - 1}\, du$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the integral

The integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{u^3}{e^u - 1} du = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$ (a standard result, evaluated by expanding $\frac{1}{e^u - 1}$ as a geometric series and integrating term-by-term).

### Step 5 — Assemble the constants into σ

$$M(T) = \frac{2 \pi^{5} k_B^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}} \cdot T^{4} = \sigma T^{4}$$

with:

$$\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^{5} k_B^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-4}$$

### Step 6 — Conclude

$$\boxed{\;M = \sigma T^{4} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\, T^{4}\;\text{W·m}^{-2}\;}$$

For a gray body with emissivity ε: $M_{\text{real}} = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Detectivity Relationship (D\* and NEP)

**Goal:** Derive the expression for specific detectivity $D^*$.

### Step 1 — Define NEP from signal-to-noise ratio

The **Noise Equivalent Power** is the incident radiant power $P_{\text{in}}$ that produces an RMS output signal equal to the RMS noise $V_n$ in a 1-Hz electrical bandwidth:

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{\mathcal{R} \cdot P_{\text{in}}}{V_n} = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \text{NEP} = \frac{V_n}{\mathcal{R}}$$

where $\mathcal{R}$ is the responsivity (V/W).

### Step 2 — NEP scales with the square root of bandwidth

Because thermal (Johnson) noise has white-noise spectral density, the RMS noise voltage scales as $V_n \propto \sqrt{\Delta f}$. Therefore:

$$\text{NEP}(\Delta f) = \text{NEP}(1\;\text{Hz}) \cdot \sqrt{\Delta f}$$

### Step 3 — Detectivity is the inverse of NEP

$$D = \frac{1}{\text{NEP}} = \frac{\mathcal{R}}{V_n}$$

### Step 4 — Normalize to detector area and bandwidth

To make detectivity independent of detector area and bandwidth, multiply by $\sqrt{A_s \cdot \Delta f}$:

$$D^* = D \cdot \sqrt{A_s \cdot \Delta f} = \frac{\mathcal{R} \sqrt{A_s \cdot \Delta f}}{V_n} \quad[\text{units: cm·Hz}^{1/2}\text{·W}^{-1}]$$

$$\boxed{\;D^* = \frac{\sqrt{A_s \cdot \Delta f}}{\text{NEP}} = \frac{\mathcal{R} \sqrt{A_s \cdot \Delta f}}{V_n}\;}$$

### Step 5 — Engineering interpretation

A high-$D^*$ detector can resolve weaker IR signals from smaller areas in a given noise bandwidth. The best uncooled microbolometers reach $D^* \sim 10^{8}$ Jones; cooled photon detectors (e.g., HgCdTe) reach $D^* \sim 10^{11}$ Jones.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU-style Calculation)

**Problem:** A patient has a localized inflamed region on the skin at temperature 38.5 °C, surrounded by normal skin at 32.0 °C. The skin emissivity is 0.98. Find (i) the peak emission wavelength of the inflamed region, and (ii) the ratio of total emitted power density of inflamed skin to normal skin.

### Part (i) — Peak wavelength

Convert temperatures to Kelvin:

$$T_{\text{inf}} = 38.5 + 273.15 = 311.65\;\text{K}$$
$$T_{\text{norm}} = 32.0 + 273.15 = 305.15\;\text{K}$$

Apply Wien's displacement law to the inflamed region:

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{311.65} = 9.30 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m} = 9.30\;\mu\text{m}$$

This is within the LWIR window (8–14 µm) — the inflamed skin is therefore perfectly visible to a thermographic camera.

### Part (ii) — Power ratio

Use Stefan–Boltzmann (emissivity cancels out in the ratio):

$$\frac{M_{\text{inf}}}{M_{\text{norm}}} = \left(\frac{T_{\text{inf}}}{T_{\text{norm}}}\right)^{4} = \left(\frac{311.65}{305.15}\right)^{4}$$

Compute the ratio inside the parentheses:

$$\frac{311.65}{305.15} = 1.0213$$

Raise to the fourth power:

$$(1.0213)^{4} \approx 1.088$$

The inflamed region emits **~8.8% more power** than the surrounding skin. The thermal camera must be sensitive enough (low NEP, high D\*) to detect this 8.8% radiance contrast — a job that modern microbolometer arrays can do with ease.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Medical Thermography Imaging Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["PATIENT SKIN SURFACE  T approx 305 K  epsilon approx 0.98"]:::source
    B["IR EMISSION  LWIR 8 to 14 micrometre  Planck radiance"]:::source
    C["OPTICAL LENS  Germanium or Chalcogenide  IR transparent"]:::opt
    D["SCANNER or FPA  Focal Plane Array 320x240 pixels"]:::opt
    E["IR DETECTOR ARRAY  Bolometer or MCT photon detector"]:::det
    F1["THERMAL DETECTOR  Bolometer  Thermopile  Pyroelectric"]:::det
    F2["PHOTON DETECTOR  Photoconductive  Photovoltaic  Photoemissive"]:::det
    G["SIGNAL CONDITIONING  Amplifier and ADC converter"]:::sig
    H["RADIANCE TO TEMPERATURE  Inverse Planck algorithm"]:::sig
    I["THERMOGRAM DISPLAY  2D pseudo colour image"]:::out
    J["DIAGNOSTIC INTERPRETATION  Inflammation  Vascular  Oncology"]:::out

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E
    E --> F1
    E --> F2
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    G --> H --> I --> J

    classDef source fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef opt fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef det fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sig fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#6d28d9,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 IR Detector Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["IR DETECTORS"]:::root
    B["THERMAL DETECTORS  Slow ms response  No cooling  Broadband"]:::th
    C["PHOTON DETECTORS  Fast ns response  Often cooled  Narrowband"]:::ph

    A --> B
    A --> C

    B --> B1["BOLOMETER  Resistance change dR with dT"]:::th
    B --> B2["THERMOPILE  Seebeck voltage from many junctions"]:::th
    B --> B3["PYROELECTRIC  Spontaneous polarization change with dT"]:::th
    B --> B4["MICROBOLOMETER ARRAY  Uncooled FPA for medical cameras"]:::th

    C --> C1["PHOTOCONDUCTIVE  Conductivity change PbS InSb"]:::ph
    C --> C2["PHOTOVOLTAIC  p-n junction photocurrent MCT"]:::ph
    C --> C3["PHOTOEMISSIVE  Photon ejects electron from cathode"]:::ph
    C --> C4["QWIP and MCT  Quantum well HgCdTe cooled to 77K"]:::ph

    classDef root fill:#1e293b,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef th fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ph fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Microbolometer Pixel Functional Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INFRARED PHOTON  8 to 14 micrometre"]:::io
    B["ABSORBING MEMBRANE  SiNx or VOxs thin film"]:::core
    C["THERMAL ISOLATION LEGS  Low thermal conductance"]:::core
    D["TEMPERATURE RISE  delta T from absorbed IR power"]:::core
    E["TCR RESISTOR  Resistance dR per dT"]:::core
    F["READOUT CIRCUIT  Bias and integrate pixel voltage"]:::io
    G["PIXEL VOLTAGE OUTPUT  Proportional to incident power"]:::io

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G

    classDef io fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams for the KTU exam:** A block diagram question (e.g., "Draw and explain the block diagram of a medical thermography system") carries 7 marks — 3 for the diagram, 2 for labels, 2 for explanation. Always draw arrows from source → optics → detector → processing → display, and label the function of *every* block.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define emissivity. Why is human skin considered a near-perfect blackbody in the 8–14 µm band? [3 Marks]
**Model Answer:**
Emissivity (ε) of a surface is the ratio of the thermal radiation emitted by the surface to the radiation emitted by a perfect blackbody at the same temperature. It is a dimensionless number between 0 and 1.
Human skin has an emissivity of **ε ≈ 0.98 ± 0.01** in the 8–14 µm LWIR band. This is because the skin's outer epidermal layer (stratum corneum) is highly water-rich, and water absorbs strongly in this band — by Kirchhoff's law ($\varepsilon = \alpha$), strong absorbers are also strong emitters. This makes skin behave almost like a perfect blackbody, justifying its use in medical thermography.

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 similar]`

### Question 2. State Wien's displacement law. A skin lesion is at 40 °C. Find its peak emission wavelength. [3 Marks]
**Model Answer:**
Wien's displacement law states that the wavelength at which a blackbody's spectral emittance is maximum is inversely proportional to its absolute temperature: $\lambda_{\max} T = b$, where $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K.

For T = 40 °C = 313.15 K:

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{313.15} = 9.25\;\mu\text{m}$$

This wavelength lies in the long-wave infrared atmospheric window (8–14 µm), confirming that the lesion's peak IR emission can be detected by a medical thermographic camera.

> `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Block Diagram + Detector Types
**a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a medical thermography system. Label all blocks. [7 Marks]
**b)** Compare thermal detectors and photon (quantum) detectors of infrared radiation. Give two examples of each. [7 Marks]

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Introduction (1 mark):** Medical thermography is a passive, non-contact, non-ionizing imaging technique that maps the skin's surface temperature by detecting its naturally emitted long-wave infrared (LWIR) radiation (8–14 µm).

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** The system has six functional blocks:

1. **Source (Patient Skin)** — at ~305 K with ε ≈ 0.98, emits IR per Planck's law.
2. **Optics (Germanium / Chalcogenide lens)** — focuses LWIR onto the detector. Standard glass blocks IR, so exotic materials are required.
3. **Detector Array (Focal Plane Array)** — converts IR photons to electrical signal per pixel. Common choice: 320×240 or 640×480 uncooled microbolometer.
4. **Signal Conditioning** — low-noise amplifier, multiplexer, 14-bit ADC.
5. **Processing Unit** — applies inverse Planck's law to compute temperature from radiance, applies emissivity correction, generates pseudo-colour map.
6. **Display** — thermogram on LCD, with absolute and differential temperature readouts.

**Working (3 marks):** Each pixel's output voltage is proportional to incident IR power via the responsivity $\mathcal{R}$. The processor converts this to radiance $L$ (W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹·µm⁻¹), then inverts Planck's law to get temperature T. A false-colour palette (e.g., ironbow: dark = cold, white = hot) is mapped onto pixel values, producing the thermogram.

#### Model Solution to (b):

| Feature | Thermal Detectors | Photon (Quantum) Detectors |
|---|---|---|
| Operating principle | IR energy → heat → ΔT → Δproperty | IR photon → electron-hole pair → photocurrent |
| Response time | Slow (ms) | Fast (ns to µs) |
| Spectral response | Broadband (pan-chromatic) | Narrowband, wavelength-selective |
| Cooling needed? | **No** (room temperature) | **Yes** (often 77 K, liquid N₂) |
| Sensitivity (D\*) | Lower (10⁷–10⁸ Jones) | Higher (10¹⁰–10¹¹ Jones) |
| Cost & complexity | Low | High |
| Example 1 | **Bolometer** (vanadium-oxide resistor) | **Photoconductive** (PbS, InSb) |
| Example 2 | **Pyroelectric** (LiTaO₃, TGS crystal) | **Photovoltaic** (HgCdTe, InGaAs) |

### Question B (14 Marks) — Blackbody Laws + Detector Specs
**a)** State and derive Stefan–Boltzmann law from Planck's radiation law. [7 Marks]
**b)** Define responsivity, NEP, and specific detectivity (D\*) of an IR detector. A detector has NEP = 1.5 nW at Δf = 1 Hz, sensitive area 1 mm × 1 mm. Calculate its D\*. [7 Marks]

#### Model Solution to (a):
**Statement (1 mark):** The total radiant power emitted per unit area of a blackbody is proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature: $M = \sigma T^4$, with $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴.

**Derivation (6 marks):** Follow the derivation in Section 3.2 of these notes — integrate Planck's spectral emittance over all wavelengths, substitute $u = hc/\lambda k_B T$, and evaluate the integral $\int_0^\infty u^3/(e^u-1)du = \pi^4/15$. Final boxed result:

$$\boxed{M = \sigma T^4 = \frac{2\pi^5 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^2}\, T^4}$$

**Engineering use (0 mark for content, 1 mark for clarity):** For a real gray body, $M_{\text{real}} = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$. With ε = 0.98 for skin and T = 305 K, $M_{\text{skin}} \approx 492\;\text{W·m}^{-2}$.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Definitions (3 marks):**
- **Responsivity $\mathcal{R}$** = output signal (V or A) per unit incident IR power: $\mathcal{R} = V_s/P_{\text{in}}$.
- **NEP** = incident power giving SNR = 1 in 1-Hz bandwidth: $\text{NEP} = V_n/\mathcal{R}$.
- **Specific Detectivity D\*** = detectivity normalized to detector area and noise bandwidth: $D^* = \sqrt{A_s \Delta f}/\text{NEP}$ in Jones (cm·Hz¹ᐟ²·W⁻¹).

**Numerical calculation (4 marks):**
Given: NEP = 1.5 nW = $1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ W; $A_s = 1\;\text{mm} \times 1\;\text{mm} = 1 \times 10^{-2}\;\text{cm} \times 1 \times 10^{-2}\;\text{cm} = 1 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{cm}^2$; $\Delta f = 1$ Hz.

Apply the formula:

$$D^* = \frac{\sqrt{A_s \cdot \Delta f}}{\text{NEP}} = \frac{\sqrt{(1 \times 10^{-4})(1)}}{1.5 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$D^* = \frac{1 \times 10^{-2}}{1.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 6.67 \times 10^{6}\;\text{cm·Hz}^{1/2}\text{·W}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{D^* \approx 6.67 \times 10^{6}\;\text{Jones}}$$

**Interpretation:** This is a modest D* — characteristic of an uncooled microbolometer. For comparison, a cooled MCT (HgCdTe) photon detector would have D* in the range of $10^{10}$–$10^{11}$ Jones.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C → K** in Wien's law or Stefan–Boltzmann problems — costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up Stefan–Boltzmann (T⁴) with peak spectral emittance (T⁵)** — these are different laws; examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Wrong units in D\*** — Jones = cm·Hz¹ᐟ²·W⁻¹, NOT m·Hz¹ᐟ²·W⁻¹. The "cm" is historical and must be used.
> 4. **Writing NEP as "noise equivalent voltage"** — NEP is a *power*, not a voltage. Common slip.
> 5. **In the block-diagram question**, drawing arrows in the wrong direction (e.g., skin ← lens ← detector) — that loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Not stating emissivity of skin (≈0.98)** when discussing a thermography problem — this is a 1-mark "must-mention" item.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Read this 10 minutes before entering the exam hall. If you can tick every box, you are exam-ready.**

### Foundational Physics
- ☐ Thermography is **passive** (no X-ray, no ionizing radiation). The body itself is the IR source.
- ☐ Human skin is a **near-blackbody** in the 8–14 µm band with **ε ≈ 0.98**.
- ☐ Skin at T = 305 K peaks at **λ_max ≈ 9.5 µm** (Wien's law).
- ☐ The four pillars: **Planck, Wien, Stefan–Boltzmann, Kirchhoff** — name them, give the equation, give the constants.
- ☐ Stefan–Boltzmann constant σ = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸** W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴.
- ☐ Wien's constant b = **2.898 × 10⁻³** m·K.
- ☐ Boltzmann constant k_B = **1.381 × 10⁻²³** J/K; Planck's constant h = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴** J·s.

### IR Detector Types
- ☐ **Two families**: Thermal (slow, uncooled) and Photon/Quantum (fast, often cooled).
- ☐ **Thermal examples**: Bolometer, Thermopile, Pyroelectric detector.
- ☐ **Photon examples**: Photoconductive (PbS), Photovoltaic (HgCdTe/MCT), Photoemissive.
- ☐ **Microbolometer** = the workhorse of modern uncooled medical thermal cameras (VO_x or a-Si pixel resistor on a thermally isolated membrane).
- ☐ **HgCdTe (MCT)** cooled to 77 K = gold standard for high-sensitivity research cameras.

### Detector Specs
- ☐ Responsivity $\mathcal{R}$ = V/W.
- ☐ NEP = lowest detectable power in 1-Hz bandwidth; **lower = better**.
- ☐ D* = specific detectivity in Jones (cm·Hz¹ᐟ²·W⁻¹); **higher = better**.
- ☐ NEP $\propto \sqrt{\Delta f}$ — bandwidth matters.
- ☐ Response time τ: bolometer = ms; photon detector = µs–ns.

### Medical Imaging Applications (Bonus 1-mark points)
- ☐ Breast cancer screening (thermography as adjunct to mammography).
- ☐ Peripheral vascular disease and Raynaud's syndrome.
- ☐ Burn-depth assessment.
- ☐ Inflammatory arthritis (joint inflammation mapping).
- ☐ Carpal tunnel syndrome.
- ☐ Skin cancer (melanoma) thermal signature.

### Exam Strategy
- ☐ Always write **units** in derivations.
- ☐ Always **box** your final answer.
- ☐ In numerical questions, show **all substitution steps** to gain partial marks even if the final answer slips.
- ☐ In a 7-mark "compare" question, **use a table** — examiners love tables, they speed up valuation.
